title: Messy real-world data
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Messy real-world data
text:
The hook
You ask a hundred people for their gender on a paper form and type the answers into a spreadsheet. Back come "Male", "male", "M", " Male " with a stray space, and "MALE" in a fit of enthusiasm. To you they all mean the same thing. To pandas they are five different values — because the computer compares text exactly, character for character, and a capital M is not a lowercase m.
That gap between what a human reads and what a machine stores is the daily reality of real-world data. The same trouble shows up everywhere:

Inconsistent text — "Mumbai", "mumbai ", "MUMBAI" are one city to you, three categories to a value_counts().
Numbers wearing a costume — "₹1,200" looks like a price, but the currency symbol and the comma make it text, and you can’t add text.
The same idea, many spellings — "M", "male", and "Male" all mean one category that you want counted once.

In Chapter 4 you handled missing values and wrong types: holes in the table and columns stored as the wrong dtype. This chapter is about the rows that are present and look fine but are written inconsistently — the messy strings and formats that quietly split one real category into many and hide numbers inside text. The fixes are a small, dependable set of pandas string methods.

You’ll need from before: counting and repairing columns (M3 Ch4). Here we repair a different kind of mess — not the gaps, but the formatting.

title: Messy real-world data
section: Text the computer sees, not text you read
text:
Text the computer sees, not text you read
Start with the gender column from the hook. Put it in a Series and ask value_counts() how many of each value there are.






Every entry comes back as its own category. "  Male" (with leading spaces) and "male" are different; "Female " (trailing space) and "FEMALE" are different. Two real groups — male and female — have shattered into six. Any analysis built on this column is wrong before it starts: a group-by would make six groups, a chart six bars, a model six categories where there are two.
Two differences cause almost all of this:

Whitespace — invisible spaces at the start or end. "Male" and "Male " look identical on screen but are not equal.
Case — "Male", "male", and "MALE" differ only in capitalisation.

Pandas gives you a vectorised string toolkit under the .str accessor that fixes both across a whole column at once — no loop. .str.strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace; .str.lower() forces everything to lowercase. Chain them.






The six categories have already collapsed toward three: "male", "female", and the stragglers "m" and "f". Stripping and lowercasing did the heavy lifting — "  Male", "male", and "MALE" are now the single string "male". What remains is a question of meaning, not formatting: "m" and "f" are abbreviations that stand for the full words. That is the next job.

title: Messy real-world data
section: Consolidating categories that mean the same thing
text:
Consolidating categories that mean the same thing
.str.strip().str.lower() fixes how text is written. It cannot know that "m" means "male" — that is domain knowledge only you have. To fold synonyms and abbreviations into one label, write the mapping down explicitly and apply it with .replace(), which swaps any value it finds as a dictionary key for the corresponding value.






Now there are exactly two categories: male with 3 and female with 3. Notice the order of operations — strip and lowercase first, then map. If you tried to map the raw values you would need keys for "  Male", "MALE", "Male " and every other variant; by normalising case and spacing first, your mapping only has to know the clean spellings ("m", "f") it still needs to fold in. A small, readable dictionary instead of a sprawling one.
This pattern — normalise, then map — is how you turn a column that a survey, a web form, or three different data-entry clerks filled in into the tidy set of categories your analysis expects.

title: Messy real-world data
text:
Numbers hiding inside strings
The other classic mess is a number that arrives dressed as text. Prices, amounts, and measurements often carry a symbol or a separator: "₹1,200", "$3.50", "1,050 kg". To a person these are clearly quantities. To pandas the currency symbol, the thousands comma, and the unit are ordinary characters, so the whole value is a string — and string columns refuse to do arithmetic. Try to sum them and pandas either errors or, worse, glues the texts end to end.
The fix is two steps: strip the non-numeric characters, then convert what remains to a real number with .astype(float).






Each .str.replace(old, new, regex=False) deletes one offending character: first the ₹ symbol, then the thousands comma, leaving a clean numeric string like "1200". Only then does .astype(float) succeed, turning "1200" into 1200.0. With a real numeric column, .sum() and .mean() work as expected — the total comes to 3784.0, which would have been impossible on the original text. Passing regex=False tells pandas to treat the pattern as a literal string, which is the safe choice when removing punctuation that could otherwise be read as a regular expression.
The same recipe handles units and other currencies: drop a "$" the same way, or strip a trailing " kg" before converting. The shape never changes — remove what isn’t part of the number, then astype the rest — and a column you couldn’t do maths on becomes one you can.
A quick word on dates, the third common offender: they arrive as "2026-06-29", "29/06/2026", and "June 29, 2026", all text. Pandas has a dedicated tool for them, pd.to_datetime, which parses many formats into a real date type. The principle is the same one running through this whole chapter — a value that looks like a date or a number is still text until you convert it.

title: Messy real-world data
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Pandas compares text exactly, so "  Male", "male", and "M" are different values — case, whitespace, and abbreviations split one real category into many.
.str.strip() removes stray whitespace and .str.lower() forces one case; chaining them (.str.strip().str.lower()) normalises a whole column at once.
To fold synonyms together, normalise first, then map with .replace({...}) — a small dictionary of clean spellings to one label.
A number wearing a symbol or comma ("₹1,200") is text: strip the non-numeric characters with .str.replace(..., regex=False), then .astype(float) before doing any maths.

title: Messy real-world data
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made values so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Write clean_text(s) that normalises one string: trim the whitespace from both ends and lowercase it. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Python strings have the same two methods pandas uses on a column. s.strip() removes the spaces at each end, and .lower() forces lowercase. Chain them on the input: strip first, then lower.



Show solution

def clean_text(s):
    return s.strip().lower()
s.strip() drops the leading and trailing spaces, turning "  Hello " into "Hello"; .lower() then makes it "hello". A string that is already clean, like "male", passes through unchanged. This is exactly what .str.strip().str.lower() does to a whole column, one value at a time.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write to_number(s) that turns a price string into a float. Strip a leading currency symbol (handle both ₹ and $) and any thousands commas, then convert what’s left. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Remove each unwanted character with str.replace(old, ""), which deletes every copy of old. Chain three of them — one for "₹", one for "$", one for "," — so a value with none of them (like "3.5") is untouched. Then wrap the result in float(...).



Show solution

def to_number(s):
    return float(s.replace("₹", "").replace("$", "").replace(",", ""))
Each .replace(old, "") strips one character: "₹1,200" loses its ₹ to become "1,200", then its comma to become "1200", which float(...) reads as 1200.0. A plain "3.5" has none of those characters, so the three replaces leave it alone and float(...) returns 3.5. This is the single-value version of the .str.replace(...).astype(float) chain you used on a column.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Take a messy category column such as ["M", "male", " Male "], normalise it (strip + lowercase + map to one label), and report the unique values before and after so the consolidation is visible.
Rubric — a good answer:

Builds a Series from a messy category column and shows its unique values before cleaning (three or more variants).
Normalises with .str.strip().str.lower() and folds abbreviations together with .replace({...}).
Reports the unique values after, showing they collapse to the single intended label.



If you’re stuck

series.unique() lists the distinct values, so print it once before and once after cleaning. Between the two prints, chain .str.strip().str.lower() and then .replace({"m": "male"}) to fold the abbreviation into the full word.



Show one possible answer

import pandas as pd

messy = pd.Series(["M", "male", " Male "])
print("before:", list(messy.unique()))

cleaned = messy.str.strip().str.lower().replace({"m": "male"})
print("after :", list(cleaned.unique()))
Before cleaning, unique() shows three variants — ['M', 'male', ' Male '] — because case and the stray spaces make them distinct. After .str.strip().str.lower() they all become "male", and .replace({"m": "male"}) folds the abbreviation in, so after prints the single label ['male']: one real category, counted once.

title: Messy real-world data
text: Check yourself

title: Messy real-world data
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
Real datasets rarely arrive tidy. With Chapter 4’s missing-value and type tools and this chapter’s string toolkit, you can take a column written by many hands — inconsistent case, stray spaces, currency symbols, abbreviations — and turn it into clean text and real numbers your analysis can trust. Next we move from cleaning single columns to combining whole tables: joining datasets together and grouping rows to summarise them.

title: Environments & reproducibility
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Environments & reproducibility
text:
The hook
You finish an analysis, send it to a teammate, and they get different numbers. Or you rerun your own notebook next month and the result has shifted. Nothing is “broken” — the work was not reproducible. For data work that’s a real problem: a result nobody can reproduce is a result nobody should trust.
Reproducibility means same inputs → same result, anywhere. Three things make it happen: pinned package versions (M1 Ch13), the exact data, and — the new idea here — a fixed random seed so anything random comes out the same every time. This chapter is the what and why of all three.

You’ll need from before: pinning versions in a requirements.txt (M1 Ch13), and the notebook “Restart & Run All” habit (M3 Ch11).

title: Environments & reproducibility
text:
Why randomness breaks reproducibility
Lots of data work uses randomness: shuffling rows, splitting train/test sets, sampling, initialising a model. By default the random-number generator starts from a different place each run, so you get different numbers each time — and so does your teammate. That’s exactly what makes results drift.
The fix is a seed: a fixed starting point for the generator. Set the same seed and the “random” numbers are identical every run, on every machine. Press Run a few times — the output never changes:






Run it again: same five numbers. Change the seed to a different fixed number and you’ll get a different but equally repeatable sequence. The randomness is still useful — it’s now anchored.

💡 On your own computer (this page runs in the browser; the commands below are for your real machine). This is a real transcript — the same seeded script run twice gives byte-for-byte identical output:
$ cat sample.py
import numpy as np
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
print(rng.integers(0, 100, size=5))

$ python sample.py
[ 8 77 65 43 43]
$ python sample.py
[ 8 77 65 43 43]

Two runs, identical output — because the seed anchors the generator. Without that line, the two runs would print different numbers.

title: Environments & reproducibility
text:
The three things to pin






Note🧱 For Java developers





This is the data-science version of a reproducible build. Pinned requirements.txt ≈ locked dependency versions (a Maven BOM / Gradle lockfile); the vendored dataset ≈ a checked-in test fixture rather than a live external call; and the random seed ≈ controlling nondeterminism (like fixing a clock or RNG in a test) so a run is repeatable. The goal is the same: same inputs in, same artifact out, on any machine.



A reproducible analysis pins three things:

Package versions — a requirements.txt of name==version lines (M1 Ch13), so everyone runs the same pandas, the same NumPy.
The data — the exact bytes you analysed. This course vendors its datasets (a CSV checked into the repo, loaded offline via lib/data.py) rather than fetching a URL that might change or vanish.
The random seed — one fixed seed near the top, so every shuffle, split, and sample repeats.


⚠️ Watch out: a seed makes randomness repeatable, but it does not make a tiny or biased sample correct. Reproducible and right are different goals — pin the seed for repeatability, and still check your data and method.

title: Environments & reproducibility
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Reproducible means same inputs → same result, on any machine — the baseline for trusting a data analysis.
A fixed random seed anchors the random-number generator, so randomness comes out identical every run.
Pin three things: package versions (requirements.txt), the exact data (vendored, not a changing URL), and the seed.
A seed buys repeatability, not correctness — still check that the data and method are sound.

title: Environments & reproducibility
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
A seeded generator gives the same numbers every time. Complete first_draw so it seeds a generator with seed and returns its first integer in [0, 100). Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

np.random.default_rng(seed) builds a generator anchored to that seed. Calling it twice with the same seed gives the same first number.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def first_draw(seed):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    return int(rng.integers(0, 100))
Because the generator is seeded, first_draw(42) returns the same number every call — which is why the two first_draw(42) results are equal.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write is_reproducible(seed) — return True if two separately seeded generators produce the same five numbers (they always should, with the same seed). Complete the marked lines.









If you’re stuck

Build b exactly like a: np.random.default_rng(seed).integers(0, 100, size=5). Same seed, same sequence, so the lists are equal.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def is_reproducible(seed):
    a = np.random.default_rng(seed).integers(0, 100, size=5)
    b = np.random.default_rng(seed).integers(0, 100, size=5)
    return list(a) == list(b)
Two generators seeded with the same number walk the same path, so a and b hold the same five integers — the essence of a reproducible random step.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). In two or three sentences, explain to a teammate how you’d make a data analysis reproducible, naming the three things you’d pin.
Rubric — a good answer:

Names pinned package versions (a requirements.txt).
Names the exact data (vendored / versioned, not a changing URL).
Names a fixed random seed, and notes it gives repeatability, not correctness.



Show one possible answer

answer = """
To make my analysis reproducible I pin three things: a requirements.txt so everyone
installs the same package versions, the exact dataset (checked into the repo, not a
URL that could change), and a fixed random seed at the top so every shuffle and
split repeats. That gets the same result on any machine — though I still check the
data and method are sound, since a seed makes randomness repeatable, not correct.
"""
print(answer.strip())
Pinning versions + data + seed is the recipe; the caveat that repeatability isn’t correctness is what separates a careful answer from a rote one.

title: Environments & reproducibility
text: Check yourself

title: Environments & reproducibility
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now make a data analysis run the same way for everyone — pinned versions, vendored data, a fixed seed. That’s the last habit M3 sets up before the craft turns practical again: next we confront data ethics — where data comes from, who it represents, and what that means for the conclusions you draw.

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text:
The hook
Imagine judging a baking contest where one score is the cake’s weight in grams (say 1,200) and another is its sweetness on a scale of 0 to 10 (say 7). If you naively add the scores, the weight swamps the sweetness — not because weight matters more, but because its numbers are bigger. Many machine-learning models fall into exactly this trap: they read a column measured in thousands as more important than a column measured in tenths, purely because of its raw size.
This chapter is a preview of feature preparation — the step between a clean table and a model. We won’t train anything yet; the goal is to see the two moves you’ll reach for again and again:

Scaling numeric columns onto a common range, so no feature wins on size alone.
Encoding a text category into numbers, so a model can read it at all.

We’ll use the Palmer Penguins again — a familiar table from earlier chapters — and prepare two of its columns the way you would before handing them to a model.

You’ll need from before: loading a CSV and selecting columns (M3 Ch2), and cleaning out missing values (M3 Ch4). We’ll assume the columns we touch are clean.

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text:
Why models care about scale
A model that measures distance or sums up weighted features treats a column’s raw numbers at face value. Penguin body mass runs from about 2,700 to 6,300 grams; bill length runs from about 32 to 60 millimetres. To such a model, a 100-gram difference in mass looks a hundred times larger than a 1-millimetre difference in bill length — even though, biologically, both might be equally telling.
Left unscaled, the big-numbered column dominates: it dictates which penguins look “close” and which look “far apart”, and the smaller column barely registers. Scaling fixes this by rewriting every numeric column onto a shared range, so each one contributes on its own merits rather than on the accident of its units.
Let’s see the size gap first-hand, then close it.






One column lives in the thousands, the other in the tens. The two scaling recipes below put them on equal footing — one by squeezing into a fixed window, the other by re-centring around the average.

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text:
Min-max scaling to [0, 1]
The simplest recipe is min-max scaling: slide and squeeze a column so its smallest value becomes 0, its largest becomes 1, and everything else lands proportionally in between. The formula is:
\[
x_{\text{scaled}} = \frac{x - \min}{\max - \min}
\]
Subtracting the minimum moves the smallest value to 0; dividing by the range (max - min) shrinks the largest down to 1. Every column treated this way ends up in the same [0, 1] window, whatever its original units.






The scaled column now runs from exactly 0.0 to 1.0. A penguin sitting halfway between the lightest and heaviest birds scores around 0.5. Because the whole operation is vectorized, pandas applies it to all 342 values at once — no loop.
Min-max scaling is intuitive and keeps the shape of the data, but it has one soft spot: a single very large outlier stretches the max, squashing every other value into a thin band near zero. When outliers are a worry, the next recipe is steadier.

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text:
Standardization (z-score) to mean 0, std 1
Standardization re-expresses each value as how many standard deviations it sits from the mean. That number is called a z-score:
\[
z = \frac{x - \text{mean}}{\text{std}}
\]
Subtracting the mean re-centres the column on 0; dividing by the standard deviation rescales the spread so the column’s std becomes 1. Unlike min-max, there is no fixed ceiling — a z-score can be -2.5 or +3.1 — but typical values cluster between about -3 and +3. A z-score of 0 means “exactly average”; +1 means “one standard deviation above average”.






The new column has mean 0.0 and std 1.0, exactly as promised. Note the ddof=0: that asks pandas for the population standard deviation (divide by n), which is what most scaling tools — including scikit-learn’s StandardScaler — use. Pandas defaults to the sample std (divide by n - 1), so passing ddof=0 keeps our numbers aligned with the standard tool you’ll meet later.
So which do you pick? Min-max when you want a guaranteed bounded range (some models and image pipelines expect [0, 1]); standardization when your data has outliers or roughly bell-shaped spread. Both share the same purpose: put every numeric feature on comparable footing so the model weighs them by signal, not by size.

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text:
One-hot encoding a category
Scaling handles numbers. But penguins also have a species column holding text — Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo. A model that multiplies and adds cannot read the word “Gentoo”; it needs numbers.
A tempting shortcut is to map the species to 0, 1, 2. That quietly lies to the model: it implies Gentoo (2) is “twice” Chinstrap (1) and that the categories have an order, which they do not. One-hot encoding avoids the lie. It replaces the single text column with one 0/1 column per category: a row gets a 1 in the column for its own species and 0 everywhere else. No category is treated as bigger than another.
Pandas does this in one call with pd.get_dummies:






One text column became three numeric columns — Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo — one per species in the data. Read any row and exactly one of the three holds a 1 (or True, pandas’ 0/1 flavour): that row’s species. The information is identical to the original label, but now it is in a form a model can do arithmetic on, with no false ordering implied.
In a full pipeline you would scale the numeric columns and one-hot the categorical ones, then glue them back into a single table of numbers — the input a model actually trains on. That assembly is a later chapter; for now you’ve met the two moves it is built from.

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Many models read a column’s raw size as importance, so features are scaled to a common range to give each a fair say.
Min-max scaling maps a column to [0, 1] with (x - min) / (max - min) — bounded, but sensitive to outliers.
Standardization (z-score) maps a column to mean 0, std 1 with (x - mean) / std — unbounded, steadier with outliers; use ddof=0 to match standard tools.
One-hot encoding with pd.get_dummies turns one text category into one 0/1 column per value, so a model reads it as numbers with no false order.

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made lists so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Write min_max_scale(values), which scales a list of numbers into [0, 1] using the min-max formula. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The lowest value should map to 0 and the highest to 1. For each value v, subtract the low end and divide by the range: (v - lo) / (hi - lo). Build the new list with a comprehension over values.



Show solution

def min_max_scale(values):
    lo = min(values)
    hi = max(values)
    return [(v - lo) / (hi - lo) for v in values]

run_tests([
    ("0..10", min_max_scale([0, 5, 10]), [0.0, 0.5, 1.0]),
    ("shifted", min_max_scale([2, 4, 6]), [0.0, 0.5, 1.0]),
])
Subtracting lo moves the smallest value to 0; dividing by hi - lo (the range) pulls the largest down to 1. The middle value of [2, 4, 6] lands at 0.5, exactly halfway.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write standardize(values), which returns the z-score of each value, rounded to two decimals. Use NumPy’s population standard deviation (np.array(...).std(), which divides by n). Complete the marked lines.









If you’re stuck

a.mean() is the average and a.std() is the population standard deviation (NumPy divides by n by default). For each value, compute (v - a.mean()) / a.std(), wrap it in float(...), and round(..., 2). For [2, 4, 6, 8] the mean is 5 and the std is √5 ≈ 2.236.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def standardize(values):
    a = np.array(values, dtype=float)
    return [round(float((v - a.mean()) / a.std()), 2) for v in values]

run_tests([
    ("four values", standardize([2, 4, 6, 8]), [-1.34, -0.45, 0.45, 1.34]),
])
The mean of [2, 4, 6, 8] is 5 and the population std is √5 ≈ 2.236. So (2 - 5) / 2.236 ≈ -1.34 and (8 - 5) / 2.236 ≈ 1.34, with the two middle values at ±0.45. The z-scores are symmetric because the values are evenly spaced around their mean.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset and one-hot encode the species column with pd.get_dummies. Report how many new columns appear and what each one represents, then explain in a sentence why a model needs numbers rather than the original text labels.
Rubric — a good answer:

Loads penguins with load_csv and calls pd.get_dummies on df["species"].
Reports that 3 new columns appear (Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo) — one per species — and that each row has a single 1/True marking its species.
Explains that a model does arithmetic on numbers, so text categories must become numeric, and one-hot avoids implying a false order between the species.



If you’re stuck

pd.get_dummies(df["species"]) returns the encoded table. Its number of columns is encoded.shape[1], and list(encoded.columns) names them. Compare a row of the encoded table to its original species to see the single 1.



Show one possible answer

import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins")
encoded = pd.get_dummies(df["species"])

print("new column count:", encoded.shape[1])
print("new columns:", list(encoded.columns))
print(encoded.head())
print(
    "A model multiplies and adds numbers, so 'Gentoo' must become numeric. "
    "One-hot uses one 0/1 column per species, so no species is treated as "
    "larger than another."
)
This prints 3 new columns — Adelie, Chinstrap, Gentoo — one per species, each row carrying a single True. Mapping species to 0, 1, 2 instead would falsely imply an order and relative size; one-hot encoding sidesteps that.

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text: Check yourself

title: Scaling & encoding (a preview)
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You’ve previewed the two everyday moves of feature preparation: scaling numeric columns (min-max or z-score) so none wins on size alone, and one-hot encoding a category so a model can read it as numbers. These are the bridge between a clean table and a model’s input. The actual modelling — fitting a line or a classifier to prepared features and measuring how well it does — is where the next module picks up.

title: Statistical plots
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Statistical plots
text:
The hook
A column of 342 numbers tells you almost nothing when you stare at it. .describe() helps — a mean here, a standard deviation there — but a table of summary statistics still asks you to imagine the data. A picture hands it to you directly. One glance at the right chart and you know whether your penguins cluster around a typical weight, how spread out they are, and whether two measurements move together.
This chapter is about three workhorse charts that answer three different questions:

A histogram answers “what does one column look like?” — its distribution.
A boxplot answers “where is the middle, how wide is the spread, anything weird?”
A scatter plot answers “do these two columns move together?” — a relationship.

We’ll read all three from the Palmer Penguins dataset you already met: 344 birds measured at Palmer Station, Antarctica. Each plot is a few lines of Matplotlib on top of a pandas column, and the skill we’re after is less about the code and more about reading the result.

You’ll need from before: drawing axes with Matplotlib (M3 Ch7), and the idea of a mean, variance, and standard deviation as the centre and spread of data (M2 Ch11). Matplotlib loads itself the moment you import matplotlib.pyplot, so there is nothing to install.

title: Statistical plots
section: A histogram: the shape of one distribution
text:
A histogram: the shape of one distribution
A column of body masses ranges from light birds to heavy ones, but how are the values spread out in between? A histogram answers that. It chops the range into equal slices called bins, counts how many values fall in each bin, and draws a bar of that height. The result is the distribution — the silhouette of the data.






Read the shape from left to right. The bars are tallest around 3500–4000 g, so that is where most penguins sit — the centre of the distribution, near the mean of about 4202 g. The bars then trail off to the right, toward the 6000 g birds, making a longer tail on the heavy side than the light side. That mild lean is called a right skew, and it is exactly why the mean (4202 g) sits a little above the median (4050 g): a few heavy penguins pull the average up. The takeaway: this column has a single hump centred near 4200 g, a typical range of roughly 3000–5500 g, and a gentle tail toward heavier birds.
The bin count matters. Too few bins and you blur real detail into one fat bar; too many and random wobble looks like structure. Twelve bins is a reasonable starting point here; nudging it up or down a little is a normal part of looking at data.

title: Statistical plots
section: A boxplot: median, spread, and outliers
text:
A boxplot: median, spread, and outliers
A histogram shows the whole shape. A boxplot is more compact: it draws a five-number summary so you can compare the centre and spread of a column at a glance.
The pieces are worth naming. The box spans the first quartile (Q1) to the third quartile (Q3) — the middle 50% of the data, a range called the interquartile range (IQR). The line inside the box is the median (the middle value). The whiskers stretch out to the rest of the typical data, reaching at most 1.5 × IQR past each quartile. Any point beyond a whisker is drawn on its own and flagged as a possible outlier.






Read it from the middle outward. The median line sits near 4050 g — half the penguins are lighter, half heavier. The box runs from about 3550 g (Q1) to 4750 g (Q3), so the middle 50% of birds span an IQR of roughly 1200 g. The whiskers reach down to the lightest bird (≈ 2700 g) and up to the heaviest (≈ 6300 g), and crucially no points are drawn past the whiskers — by the 1.5 × IQR rule, this column has no flagged outliers. The takeaway: a typical penguin weighs about 4050 g, the central half of the flock falls within a 1200 g band, and nothing is extreme enough to stand out as anomalous.
That last point is what makes boxplots a first stop in data cleaning: a stray dot far above the top whisker is your cue to check for a typo or a genuinely unusual record before it skews everything downstream.

title: Statistical plots
section: A scatter plot: a relationship between two variables
text:
A scatter plot: a relationship between two variables
The histogram and boxplot each describe one column. A scatter plot describes two at once: put one variable on the x-axis, the other on the y-axis, and draw one dot per record. The pattern of the cloud tells you whether the two move together.
Penguins offer a natural pair: do birds with longer flippers also weigh more?






The dots form a clear upward-sloping band from lower-left to upper-right: penguins with longer flippers tend to be heavier. That is a positive relationship, and a strong one — the correlation here is about r ≈ 0.87, close to the maximum of 1.0, which is why the cloud looks almost like a fuzzy line rather than a shapeless blob. The takeaway: flipper length and body mass rise together tightly enough that knowing one gives you a good guess at the other.
Read scatter shapes like a vocabulary:

Upward band → positive relationship (both rise together).
Downward band → negative relationship (one falls as the other rises).
Shapeless cloud → little or no relationship.

One caution worth carrying forward: a relationship is not proof that one variable causes the other. Longer flippers do not make a penguin heavy; both grow together because bigger birds are bigger all over. “Correlation is not causation” is a phrase you’ll meet again — a scatter shows the what, never the why.

title: Statistical plots
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A histogram shows the distribution of one column — bin the values, draw bar heights, and read the centre, the spread, and the shape (symmetric or skewed).
A boxplot shows a five-number summary of one column: the box is the middle 50% (Q1–Q3), the line is the median, the whiskers reach the typical range, and points beyond them are outliers.
A scatter plot shows the relationship between two columns: an upward band is positive, a downward band is negative, a shapeless cloud is none.
Penguin body mass centres near 4050–4200 g with a mild right skew, and flipper length vs body mass is a strong positive relationship (r ≈ 0.87).

title: Statistical plots
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made lists so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
A histogram and a boxplot both lean on the centre and spread of a column. Write mean_and_std(values) to return a tuple (mean, std), each rounded to 2 decimals, using NumPy’s population standard deviation. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

a.mean() is the average and a.std() is the population standard deviation. Wrap each in float(...), then round(..., 2), and return them as a tuple (rounded_mean, rounded_std). The std of [2, 4, 6, 8] is sqrt(5) ≈ 2.236, which rounds to 2.24.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def mean_and_std(values):
    a = np.array(values)
    return (round(float(a.mean()), 2), round(float(a.std()), 2))
np.array(values) turns the list into a NumPy array; .mean() averages it and .std() gives the population standard deviation (dividing by n, not n − 1). float(...) makes the types predictable and round(..., 2) trims each to two decimals, so [2, 4, 6, 8] returns (5.0, 2.24).



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
A histogram is, at heart, counting how many values land in each bin. Write count_in_range(values, lo, hi) that returns how many values fall in one bin — between lo and hi inclusive of both ends. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Loop over the values and keep the ones where lo <= v <= hi is true. A generator expression sum(1 for v in values if lo <= v <= hi) counts them; wrap it in int(...) so the return type is a plain integer.



Show solution

def count_in_range(values, lo, hi):
    return int(sum(1 for v in values if lo <= v <= hi))
The chained comparison lo <= v <= hi is True only when v is inside the bin (both ends included). Summing 1 for each match counts them, and int(...) returns a plain integer — so [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] has 3 values in [2, 4].



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset and draw either a histogram or a boxplot of body_mass_g, then describe the distribution in a print statement: where is it centred, how spread out is it, and is there any skew?
Rubric — a good answer:

Loads penguins with load_csv and plots body_mass_g as a histogram or a boxplot.
Describes the centre (median ≈ 4050 g, mean ≈ 4202 g) and the spread (most birds roughly 3000–5500 g).
Comments on the shape — a mild right skew, since the mean sits above the median.



If you’re stuck

Select the column with df["body_mass_g"].dropna(), then call ax.hist(mass, bins=12) for a histogram or ax.boxplot(mass) for a boxplot. For the description, read the centre off mass.mean() and mass.median() and compare them: a mean above the median signals a right skew.



Show one possible answer

from lib.data import load_csv
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = load_csv("penguins")
mass = df["body_mass_g"].dropna()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.hist(mass, bins=12, edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("body mass (g)")
ax.set_ylabel("number of penguins")
ax.set_title("Distribution of penguin body mass")
plt.show()

mean, median = round(mass.mean(), 1), mass.median()
print("centre: mean", mean, "g, median", median, "g")
print("spread: most birds roughly 3000-5500 g")
print("shape: mild right skew, because the mean sits above the median")
The histogram has a single hump near 3500–4000 g and a longer tail toward heavier birds. Because the mean (≈ 4202 g) sits above the median (4050 g), a few heavy penguins pull the average up — the signature of a mild right skew.

title: Statistical plots
text: Check yourself

title: Statistical plots
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now turn a column into a picture and read it: a histogram for the shape of a distribution, a boxplot for the median and spread and any outliers, and a scatter for the relationship between two variables. That trio is the visual core of exploratory data analysis — the looking-before-modelling step every project starts with. Next we close the module by pulling these habits together into a small end-to-end analysis.

title: Reshaping
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Reshaping
text:
The hook
Picture a class register on paper. Across the top run the subjects — Maths, Science, English — and down the side run the students. Each cell holds one score. It is compact and easy to read at a glance: that is wide data, one row per student with a column for every subject.
Now picture the same marks typed into a logbook, one line at a time: Asha, Maths, 80. Asha, Science, 91. Ravi, Maths, 72. Every line is a single score with its labels beside it. That is long data — taller, more repetitive to read, but far easier for a computer to group, filter, and chart.
Neither layout is “right.” They are the same numbers in two shapes, and real work constantly moves between them: a colleague hands you a wide spreadsheet, your plotting library wants it long, your report wants it wide again. This chapter is about that move. Two pandas tools cover almost all of it — melt turns wide into long, and pivot turns long back into wide — with pivot_table adding a summary step when several values share a cell.

You’ll need from before: groupby and split-apply-combine (M3 Ch5). A pivot_table is groupby’s close cousin — a grouped summary arranged into a grid.

title: Reshaping
section: Wide vs long, side by side
text:
Wide vs long, side by side
Let’s make the class register concrete. Here it is as a wide table: one row per student, one column per subject.






This is comfortable for a human reader. But notice a hidden awkwardness: the subject is not stored as data — it is hidden in the column names. To ask “what was the average score across all subjects?” you have to know which columns are subjects and which (student) is not. That is exactly the question that long data makes easy.
In long (also called tidy) form, every row is one observation — one student, one subject, one score:



student
subject
score




Asha
math
80


Asha
science
91


Ravi
math
72


Ravi
science
65



The table is taller and repeats Asha and Ravi, but now subject is a real column you can group by, filter on, and feed to a chart. The rule of thumb for tidy data is: each variable is a column, each observation is a row. Most pandas tools — groupby, plotting, many models — are happiest with data in this shape.

title: Reshaping
text:
melt: wide → long
df.melt() gathers columns into rows. You tell it which columns are identifiers to keep as-is (id_vars), and it stacks every other column into two new columns: one holding the old column names, one holding the values.






Read what happened: student stayed an identifier, and the two measurement columns (math, science) were unpivoted into a subject column (the old names) and a score column (the old values). Two rows became four — one per score. The var_name and value_name arguments rename the two new columns; without them pandas calls them variable and value.
Now a question like “average score per subject” is a plain groupby, because subject is real data:






That returns math 76.0 and science 78.0. Try writing the same summary against the wide table and you’ll feel the difference — you’d have to name each subject column by hand. Reshaping first, then summarising, is a pattern you’ll reuse constantly.

title: Reshaping
text:
pivot: long → wide
pivot is the reverse of melt. It spreads one column’s values out into new columns. You give it three things: the column to use as the row index, the column whose values become the new column headers, and the column that fills the cells.






We are back to a student-by-subject grid. melt and pivot are a matched pair: one tall-ifies, the other wide-ns. The catch with pivot is that it expects exactly one value for each index/column combination. If two rows wanted to land in the same cell — say Asha had two maths scores — pivot raises an error, because it would not know which one to keep. That is where pivot_table comes in.

title: Reshaping
section: pivot_table: long → wide, with a summary
text:
pivot_table: long → wide, with a summary
pivot_table works like pivot but adds an aggregate step. When several values fall into the same cell, it combines them with a function you choose (aggfunc) — "mean", "sum", "count", and so on. Think of it as a groupby whose result is laid out as a grid.
Here are six sales rows, with repeats: the North store has two tea lines.






The two North tea rows (10 and 6) were summed into a single cell of 16; South coffee (5 and 7) became 12. A plain pivot could not have done this — it would have choked on the duplicate (North, tea) pair. Swap aggfunc="mean" and the same grid reports averages instead of totals. The default aggfunc is "mean", so always state the one you want to be sure.
If you stripped the column layout away, pivot_table is doing exactly what sales.groupby(["store", "product"])["units"].sum() does — the same split-apply-combine from Chapter 5, arranged as a rectangle that is easy to read and to drop into a report.

A quick pass on real data
The same move works on the penguins table. Each penguin has a species and an island; pivot_table can count how many penguins sit in each species-by-island cell in one call:






The grid shows where each species was seen — Gentoo only on Biscoe, Chinstrap only on Dream, Adelie across all three — with NaN marking empty combinations. One line turned 344 rows into a compact cross-tab.

title: Reshaping
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Wide data spreads a variable across columns (one row per subject); long (tidy) data stacks it into rows, one observation per row. Same numbers, two shapes.
melt reshapes wide → long, gathering measurement columns into a variable/value pair while keeping id_vars as identifiers.
pivot reshapes long → wide, but needs exactly one value per cell; pivot_table adds an aggfunc so it can summarise repeats into each cell.
Tidy (long) data is what groupby, plotting, and most models expect, so reshaping first then summarising is an everyday pattern.

title: Reshaping
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made data so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
When you melt a wide DataFrame (with no id_vars), every cell becomes its own row. Complete long_row_count so it returns how many rows the melted table has. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Build the table with pd.DataFrame(data), call .melt() on it, and read .shape[0] — that is the row count. Wrap it in int(...) so the return type is a plain integer. With no id_vars, melt stacks every cell, so the long row count the long row count is the rows times the columns.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def long_row_count(data):
    return int(pd.DataFrame(data).melt().shape[0])
pd.DataFrame(data).melt() unpivots every column into variable/value rows, one row per original cell; .shape[0] counts those rows and int(...) returns a plain integer. A 2×2 wide table has four cells, so it melts to four rows — rows times columns.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
After a melt, the original column names live in the variable column. Write n_variables to report how many distinct original columns there were — the number of unique values in variable. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Melt the frame, select the "variable" column (it holds the old column names), and call .nunique() to count its distinct values. Wrap the result in int(...). Each original column name appears once per row it filled, but .nunique() counts each name only once.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def n_variables(data):
    return int(pd.DataFrame(data).melt()["variable"].nunique())
After melt, the variable column repeats each old column name once per value. .nunique() counts the distinct names, so three original columns give 3 regardless of how many rows they held.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Take a small wide table — say a few days across two outlets — melt it to long, then pivot it back to wide. Confirm you recover the original shape by comparing .shape before and after.
Rubric — a good answer:

Builds a small wide DataFrame, then melts it to long with melt (keeping a sensible id_vars column as the identifier).
Pivots the long table back to wide with pivot, using the identifier as the index and the melted variable column as the new headers.
Compares the original and round-tripped shapes (e.g. prints both .shape tuples) and states whether they match.



If you’re stuck

Keep one column (like day) as id_vars when you melt, so you have something to pivot back on. After pivoting, the index column moves into the row labels; use .reset_index() to bring it back as a column before comparing shapes, and remember pivot leaves a name on the columns axis you can clear.



Show one possible answer

import pandas as pd

wide = pd.DataFrame({
    "day": ["Mon", "Tue"],
    "cafe": [12, 9],
    "kiosk": [7, 5],
})
print("original shape:", wide.shape)

long = wide.melt(id_vars="day", var_name="outlet", value_name="cups")
back = long.pivot(index="day", columns="outlet", values="cups").reset_index()
back.columns.name = None  # drop the leftover 'outlet' axis name

print("round-tripped shape:", back.shape)
print("shapes match:", wide.shape == back.shape)
The melt turns the two outlet columns into long outlet/cups rows; the pivot spreads them back into a day-by-outlet grid. After reset_index, the recovered table has the same (2, 3) shape as the original — a clean round trip that shows melt and pivot are inverses.

title: Reshaping
text: Check yourself

title: Reshaping
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now move a table between wide and long on demand: melt to tidy it for grouping and charting, pivot to lay it back out for a report, and pivot_table to summarise repeats into a clean grid. Reshaping is the quiet skill that makes the rest of your pandas work flow — most “I can’t get this into the right form” moments end with one of these three calls. That wraps the core data-wrangling toolkit; next we turn from shaping data to telling its story with charts.

title: Cleaning data
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Cleaning data
text:
The hook
Real data is messy. A sensor misfires, someone leaves a form field blank, two records get entered twice — and the clean table you imagined arrives full of holes and oddities instead. Before you can average a column, plot a relationship, or train a model on it, you have to clean the data: find the gaps, decide what to do about them, and fix the rough edges.
You met the penguins table in Chapter 2 and learned to select from it in Chapter 3. Look closely and you’ll notice it isn’t perfect: a handful of penguins are missing measurements, and 11 are missing a recorded sex. That is completely normal — almost every real dataset has gaps. This chapter teaches the small, repeatable toolkit pandas gives you for the most common cleaning jobs:

find missing values and count them, so you know the scale of the problem;
drop the incomplete rows, or fill the gaps with a chosen value;
convert a column to the right type, and remove duplicate rows.

None of these are hard, but doing them in the right order — and knowing the trade-offs — is the difference between a trustworthy analysis and a misleading one.

You’ll need from before: selecting columns and rows (M3 Ch3). We use that to inspect and repair specific parts of the table.

title: Cleaning data
section: Missing data is a hole, not a zero
text:
Missing data is a hole, not a zero
When pandas reads a CSV and a cell has no value, it doesn’t guess. It stores a special marker called NaN — short for “not a number” — that means we don’t know this value. NaN is not the same as 0, and it is not an empty string "". A body mass of 0 grams would be a (very wrong) measurement; a NaN says no measurement was recorded at all. Keeping that distinction is the whole point: if you silently treated missing masses as zero, every average you computed would be dragged toward zero and quietly lie to you.
This is why missing data breaks math. Most arithmetic with NaN gives NaN back: add a number to “we don’t know” and the answer is still “we don’t know”. Pandas is careful here — its column summaries like .mean() skip NaN by default, which is helpful but also easy to forget. The safe habit is to see your missing values first and decide what they mean, rather than letting a default quietly handle them for you.

title: Cleaning data
text:
Counting what’s missing with .isna().sum()
The first question on any new table is: where are the holes, and how many? Two methods answer it together. df.isna() returns a table the same shape as your data, filled with True wherever a value is missing and False everywhere else. On its own that is a wall of booleans — but .sum() collapses it. Because Python counts True as 1 and False as 0, summing each column gives the number of missing values per column.






Read that result top to bottom. species, island, and year have 0 missing values — they are complete. The four body measurements (bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g) are each missing 2 values, and sex is missing 11. So the gaps are not spread evenly: most of the trouble is in the sex column, where 11 penguins were never sexed.
To get a single grand total — handy as a quick “how dirty is this?” number — sum twice. The first .sum() counts per column; a second .sum() adds those column totals together.






That double-sum (2 + 2 + 2 + 2 + 11 = 19) gives 19 missing cells in all. You now know both the shape of the problem (mostly sex) and its size (19 cells out of a 344-row table). Only now is it sensible to decide what to do.

title: Cleaning data
text:
Dropping rows with .dropna()
The simplest response is to throw away every row that has any missing value. df.dropna() does exactly that and returns a new DataFrame — it does not change the original. The penguins table starts with 344 rows; after dropping every incomplete row we are left with fewer.






We go from 344 rows to 333 — 11 rows removed. Notice that the count of dropped rows matches the sex gap, because the rows missing a measurement are mostly the same rows that are also missing sex. Dropping is honest and easy, and when the gaps are few it is often the right call. But it has a cost: every dropped row takes its good values with it. Throw away a penguin because its sex is unknown and you also lose its perfectly fine body mass. When missing values are rare that loss is acceptable; when they are common, dropping can shrink a dataset dramatically.

title: Cleaning data
text:
Filling gaps with .fillna()
The alternative is to keep every row and replace each NaN with a value you choose. df.fillna(value) does this. For a text column like sex, a sensible fill is a label that says “we don’t know” out loud, so the missing-ness stays visible rather than masquerading as real data.






After filling, the sex column has 0 missing values, and value_counts() shows a new unknown group of 11 alongside male and female. For a numeric column you might instead fill with the column’s mean or median, so the gap doesn’t distort later sums — but always remember you are inventing values. A filled value is a reasonable guess, not a measurement, and any honest analysis should say which columns were filled and how.
Choosing between dropping and filling is a judgement call, not a rule:

Drop when missing rows are few and you can spare them, or when a partial row is useless for your task.
Fill when you can’t afford to lose rows, and you have a defensible value to put in (a marker like "unknown", or a column average) — and you record what you did.

title: Cleaning data
text:
Converting types with .astype()
Once the holes are handled, the next common chore is fixing types. Each column has a data type (its dtype): numbers are usually int64 or float64, text is object. Sometimes a column arrives as the wrong type — body mass, for instance, is stored as float64 (with decimals) even though grams are whole numbers. After removing the missing values, you can convert it to a plain integer with .astype(...).






The dtype changes from float64 to int64, and the masses now print without decimal points. One important catch: you must clear the NaNs before converting to int, because the integer type has no way to store a missing value — that’s why we dropna() first. Converting types matters because the type controls what you can do: maths needs numbers, and some operations (like grouping or joining) behave differently for text than for numbers.

title: Cleaning data
text:
Removing duplicates with .drop_duplicates()
The last common mess is duplicate rows — the same record entered more than once, which would double-count it in any total. df.drop_duplicates() keeps the first occurrence of each distinct row and drops the rest. You can also apply it to a subset of columns to find the unique combinations of those columns — for example, which island each species was actually seen on:






Here drop_duplicates() collapses 344 rows down to the handful of distinct species-and-island pairs, which is a quick way to see the structure of a dataset. On the full table, df.drop_duplicates() would remove any row that is an exact copy of an earlier one — the penguins table happens to have none, but in data you collect yourself, duplicate records are common and easy to miss.
Put the moves together and you have a dependable cleaning routine: count the missing values, drop or fill them, convert any wrong types, and de-duplicate. Run in that order, it turns almost any raw table into something you can compute on with confidence.

title: Cleaning data
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

NaN is pandas’ marker for a missing value — “we don’t know”, not 0 or "". Most arithmetic with NaN returns NaN, which is why gaps break math.
df.isna().sum() counts missing values per column; a second .sum() gives a grand total. For penguins: sex is missing 11, each measurement 2, for 19 missing cells overall.
df.dropna() removes every row with any NaN (penguins: 344 → 333); df.fillna(value) keeps the rows and replaces NaN with a value you choose.
.astype(...) converts a column’s type (clear NaNs first for int), and .drop_duplicates() removes repeated rows.

title: Cleaning data
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made data — with None standing in for the missing values — so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Count all the missing values in a small table. Build a DataFrame from the dict, then count the NaNs across the whole thing. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

pd.DataFrame(data) builds the table; .isna() marks every cell True/False; the first .sum() counts per column and a second .sum() adds those into one grand total. Wrap it in int(...) for a plain integer.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def count_missing(data):
    return int(pd.DataFrame(data).isna().sum().sum())
.isna() turns each cell into True (missing) or False; summing once counts the missing cells per column, and summing again totals those counts into a single number. The None values in the dict become NaN inside the DataFrame, so they are exactly what gets counted.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now decide how many rows survive a dropna(). Build a DataFrame from the dict, drop every row with any missing value, and return how many rows are left. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

pd.DataFrame(data).dropna() returns a new table with the incomplete rows removed. Its .shape is (rows, columns), so .shape[0] is the surviving row count — wrap it in int(...).



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def drop_missing(data):
    return int(pd.DataFrame(data).dropna().shape[0])
In the first case the rows are (1, 4), (None, 5), and (3, None): the first row is complete and survives, while the other two each have a NaN and are dropped — so one row remains. In the second case neither row has a gap, so both survive.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset, count the missing values per column with df.isna().sum(), then run df.dropna() and report how many rows remain (you should find 333). Print both results.
Rubric — a good answer:

Loads penguins with load_csv and prints the per-column missing counts from df.isna().sum() (showing sex at 11).
Calls df.dropna() and reports the surviving row count read from the data, not typed in by hand.
States the contrast in plain words: 344 rows in, 333 out, 11 dropped.



If you’re stuck

df.isna().sum() gives the per-column counts. For the row count after cleaning, df.dropna().shape[0] reads it straight from the cleaned table; compare it to df.shape[0] for the rows you started with.



Show one possible answer

import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins")

print("missing per column:")
print(df.isna().sum())

before = df.shape[0]
after = df.dropna().shape[0]
print(f"rows before: {before}")
print(f"rows after : {after}")
print(f"rows dropped: {before - after}")
This prints sex as the worst column (11 missing), then reports the table shrinking from 344 rows to 333 — the 11 dropped rows read straight from the data rather than typed in.

title: Cleaning data
text: Check yourself

title: Cleaning data
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
Cleaning is the unglamorous step that makes everything after it trustworthy: a mean computed on data full of silent gaps is a mean you can’t believe. You can now find missing values, drop or fill them on purpose, fix column types, and remove duplicates — the routine a data scientist runs on almost every fresh table. Next we move from repairing a table to reshaping and summarising it: grouping rows and aggregating them to answer questions like “what’s the average body mass per species?”

title: Pandas Series & DataFrame
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Pandas Series & DataFrame
text:
The hook
Open a spreadsheet and you already understand the shape of pandas. A spreadsheet is a table: rows of records, columns with names, and a header strip along the top. Pandas gives you that same table in Python — except now you can sort, filter, average, and reshape millions of rows with a line of code instead of dragging a mouse.
Pandas has exactly two objects you need to meet first, and everything else builds on them:

A Series — one column. A labelled list of values, like a single column of the spreadsheet pulled out on its own.
A DataFrame — the whole table. A collection of named columns sitting side by side, each column being a Series.

That’s the entire mental model. In this chapter we load a real dataset — 344 penguins measured at Palmer Station, Antarctica — and learn the handful of commands that let you size up any table the moment it lands on your screen.

You’ll need from before: NumPy arrays and vectorized thinking (M3 Ch1). A pandas column is a NumPy array wearing a name tag, so that intuition carries straight over.

title: Pandas Series & DataFrame
section: A Series is a labelled column
text:
A Series is a labelled column
A Python list holds values by position — values[0], values[1], and so on. A Series holds values too, but it pairs each value with a label (its index). By default the labels are 0, 1, 2, …, but the point is that the label travels with the value wherever it goes.






Notice the two columns of output: the index (0, 1, 2, 3) on the left and the values on the right, plus a dtype (here int64) telling you what kind of data this column holds. A Series is one-dimensional — a single line of data with labels.
Because a Series is built on NumPy, whole-column arithmetic and summaries come for free. There is no loop to write:






masses.mean() collapses the column to one number; masses / 1000 returns a new Series, dividing every value at once while keeping the same labels. That is the NumPy “operate on the whole array” idea, now with an index attached.

title: Pandas Series & DataFrame
section: A DataFrame is a table of Series
text:
A DataFrame is a table of Series
Stack several labelled columns side by side, sharing one index, and you have a DataFrame — pandas’ name for a table. Each column is a Series; the column names let you ask for the one you want.
You can build a small DataFrame straight from a dictionary, where each key becomes a column name and each value becomes that column’s data:






Two named columns, one shared index down the left. Real datasets, though, rarely arrive as hand-typed dictionaries — they arrive as files. So let’s load one.

title: Pandas Series & DataFrame
section: Loading a CSV and sizing it up
text:
Loading a CSV and sizing it up
We’ll use the Palmer Penguins dataset: body measurements for 344 penguins of three species. The chapter ships the CSV alongside it, and the load_csv helper reads it for you — the same call works in your browser and in our test runner.






The first thing to check on any new table is its shape. df.shape returns a tuple (rows, columns) — here (344, 8). That single line tells you the dataset has 344 records described by 8 columns, before you’ve looked at a single value.
Next, peek at the actual rows with .head(), which returns the first five by default:






Now you can see what each row is: a penguin, with its species, the island it was seen on, four body measurements, its sex, and the study year. To get the column names on their own — handy when a table is too wide to read at a glance — ask for .columns:






That returns the eight names: species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, sex, year. The _mm and _g suffixes are a gift from whoever prepared the data — the units are baked into the names.

Summarising every numeric column at once
.describe() is the fastest way to understand the numbers in a table. For every numeric column it reports the count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and the quartiles — a one-line statistical X-ray:






Read down the body_mass_g column of that summary and you’ll find a mean of about 4201.75 grams — these penguins average a little over four kilograms. The flipper_length_mm mean lands near 200.92 mm. Note the count row: it can be lower than 344 for some columns, because a few penguins have missing measurements — .describe() quietly skips them, which is a detail worth noticing early.


Pulling out one column
To work with a single column, index the DataFrame with its name in square brackets. What comes back is a Series — that same labelled column from the start of the chapter, now sourced from real data:






df["body_mass_g"] is a Series, so every Series tool applies: .mean() gives 4201.75, matching what .describe() reported. Selecting a column and summarising it is the bread-and-butter move of exploratory data analysis, and you’ll repeat it constantly.
One more useful summary for a text column is .value_counts(), which tallies how often each value appears:






That shows Adelie 152, Gentoo 124, Chinstrap 68 — Adelie is the most common species in the dataset. With .shape, .head, .columns, .describe, column selection, and .value_counts, you can walk up to almost any table and know what it holds within a minute.

title: Pandas Series & DataFrame
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A Series is a labelled 1-D column: values paired with an index, built on NumPy so whole-column maths needs no loop.
A DataFrame is a 2-D table — several named columns (each a Series) sharing one index.
df.shape returns (rows, columns); for penguins it is (344, 8). .head(), .columns, and .describe() size up a table fast.
Selecting a column with df['col'] returns a Series, so every Series summary like .mean() applies to it directly.

title: Pandas Series & DataFrame
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made data so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Build a Series from a list of numbers and return its total as an int. Complete series_total by replacing ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Wrap the list in pd.Series(...), call .sum() on it, and pass the result to int(...) so the return type is a plain integer.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def series_total(values):
    return int(pd.Series(values).sum())
pd.Series(values) turns the list into a labelled column; .sum() adds every value, and int(...) returns it as a plain Python integer.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Build a DataFrame from a dictionary, select one column by name, and return the rounded mean of that column. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

pd.DataFrame(data) builds the table; [col] selects one column as a Series; .mean() averages it. Wrap that in float(...), then round(..., 2) for two decimal places.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def column_mean(data, col):
    return round(float(pd.DataFrame(data)[col].mean()), 2)
pd.DataFrame(data)[col] is the column as a Series; .mean() averages it, float(...) makes the type predictable, and round(..., 2) trims it to two decimal places.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset, run df.describe(), and report two facts in your own print statements: the mean body mass (about 4201.75 g) and which species is most common (Adelie, with 152 birds).
Rubric — a good answer:

Loads penguins with load_csv and calls df.describe() to inspect the numbers.
Prints the mean of body_mass_g (≈ 4201.75) read from the data, not typed in by hand.
Reports the most common species using value_counts() (Adelie, 152).



If you’re stuck

Select df["body_mass_g"] and call .mean() for the first fact. For the second, df["species"].value_counts() lists species by frequency; the first row is the most common one, and .idxmax() gives only its name.



Show one possible answer

from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
print(df.describe())

mean_mass = round(df["body_mass_g"].mean(), 2)
print("mean body mass (g):", mean_mass)

counts = df["species"].value_counts()
print("most common species:", counts.idxmax(), "with", int(counts.max()), "birds")
This prints a mean body mass of 4201.75 and reports Adelie as the most common species with 152 birds — both read straight from the data rather than typed in.

Where this fits / what’s next
You can now load a table and size it up — shape, head, columns, a numeric summary, and a single column pulled out as a Series. That inspection ritual is the first thing a working data scientist does with any new dataset. Next we go from looking to choosing: selecting rows and columns by label and position, and filtering a DataFrame down to only the penguins you care about.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text:
The hook
Chapters 2 through 5 were pictures and arrows: a vector as a point, a matrix as a grid that moves points around, a dot product as a way to measure similarity. Those pictures built the intuition. But a model never draws an arrow — when it needs a dot product or a matrix multiply, it calls NumPy, millions of times a second.
This chapter does the same operations you already understand, but in real code, in one tool. Nothing new conceptually — you are translating the pictures into the lines a model actually runs.

You’ll need from before: dot products and similarity (M2 Ch5), and the NumPy array basics (M1 Ch12). That’s the toolkit we build on here.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text:
Vectors and matrices are arrays
In NumPy, a vector is a 1-D array and a matrix is a 2-D array (a list of rows). You build both with np.array:






The .shape attribute is the single most useful thing to read off an array. It is a tuple giving the size along each dimension:

v.shape is (3,) — one dimension, length 3. (The trailing comma is Python’s way of writing a one-element tuple; it is not a typo.)
M.shape is (2, 3) — two dimensions: 2 rows then 3 columns. The convention is always (rows, columns).

When code breaks in real machine learning, it is almost always a shape that doesn’t line up. Reading .shape is your first debugging move, every time.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
section: Element-wise is not the dot product
text:
Element-wise is not the dot product
Multiply two arrays with * and NumPy multiplies them position by position — this is element-wise, and it gives back an array the same shape as the inputs. That is not the dot product. The dot product multiplies position by position and then adds the results into a single number.






All three middle lines agree: 1·4 + 2·5 + 3·6 = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32. The @ operator is the one you’ll see most in real code — it is Python’s dedicated “matrix multiply” operator, and for two vectors it computes exactly the dot product from Ch5. np.dot(a, b) does the same job; @ reads more cleanly when expressions get longer.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
section: A matrix times a vector: apply a transformation
text:
A matrix times a vector: apply a transformation
Back in Ch3, a matrix was a machine that takes a point and moves it. In NumPy that machine is one @. Multiplying a matrix M by a vector v applies the transformation M describes to the point v:






Read it row by row: the first output number is the first row of M dotted with v (2·4 + 0·5 = 8), and the second is the second row dotted with v (0·4 + 3·5 = 15). A matrix–vector product is one dot product per row. That is the whole idea: a layer of a neural network takes its input vector and does exactly this — a matrix times a vector — to produce the next vector.
The shape rule is worth saying out loud: a (2, 2) matrix times a (2,) vector gives a (2,) vector. In general, multiplying a (rows, k) matrix by a length-k vector gives a length-rows vector. The inner sizes must match.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text:
A matrix times a matrix
Stacking transformations means multiplying matrices together, again with @. Each entry of the result is one row of the left matrix dotted with one column of the right matrix:






The top-left entry is row 0 of A dotted with column 0 of B: 1·5 + 2·7 = 19. Work the other three the same way and you get the full grid. For matrix–matrix multiplication the rule is (m, k) @ (k, n) gives (m, n): the two inner numbers must match and they cancel, leaving the outer two as the result shape.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text:
Transpose: flipping rows and columns
The transpose, written .T, flips a matrix over its diagonal: rows become columns and columns become rows. The element at row i, column j ends up at row j, column i.






Transpose shows up constantly: to make shapes line up for an @, or to read the same data the other way around. One handy fact you’ll meet again: (A @ B).T equals B.T @ A.T — transposing a product reverses the order. You’ll verify that yourself in the stretch exercise.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text:
The identity matrix
One special matrix does nothing: the identity, ones on the diagonal and zeros elsewhere. Build it with np.eye(n). Multiplying any vector or matrix by the identity returns it unchanged — it is the matrix equivalent of multiplying a number by 1.






The identity is the reference point for talking about inverses and “undoing” a transformation later. For now, it is a clean check that your @ is doing what you expect: an identity should always hand back exactly what it received.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A vector is a 1-D NumPy array and a matrix is a 2-D array; read .shape (as (rows, columns)) to know what you’re holding.
* multiplies element-wise; @ (and np.dot) computes the dot product for vectors and matrix multiplication for 2-D arrays.
A matrix times a vector applies a transformation — one dot product per row; the inner sizes must match.
.T transposes (flips rows and columns), and np.eye(n) is the identity matrix that leaves things unchanged.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Complete dot(a, b) so it returns the dot product of two vectors as a plain int. Use @ on NumPy arrays, then wrap the result in int(...). Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Turn each list into an array with np.array(...), combine them with @ to get the sum of products, then wrap the whole thing in int(...) so the result is a plain Python integer rather than a NumPy number.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def dot(a, b):
    return int(np.array(a) @ np.array(b))
np.array(a) @ np.array(b) multiplies the vectors position by position and adds the results: 1·4 + 2·5 + 3·6 = 32. The orthogonal pair [1, 0] and [0, 1] share no direction, so their dot product is 0. int(...) returns a plain Python integer.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write matvec(M, v) that applies matrix M to vector v and returns the result as a plain Python list. Convert both inputs with np.array, multiply with @, and turn the array back into a list with .tolist(). Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The pattern mirrors Exercise 1: np.array(M) @ np.array(v) does the matrix–vector multiply (one dot product per row of M). The result is a NumPy array, so call .tolist() on it to hand back an ordinary Python list.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def matvec(M, v):
    return (np.array(M) @ np.array(v)).tolist()
Each output entry is one row of M dotted with v. With the identity matrix every row picks out one element of v unchanged, so [5, 7] comes straight back. The scaling matrix [[2, 0], [0, 3]] doubles the first entry and triples the second: [8, 15]. .tolist() converts the NumPy array to a plain list.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Build two 2×2 matrices A and B as NumPy arrays, compute A @ B, and verify the identity (A @ B).T == B.T @ A.T — transposing a product reverses the order. Print both sides and compare them.
Rubric — a good answer:

Builds two 2×2 arrays with np.array and computes A @ B with @.
Prints (A @ B).T and B.T @ A.T so the two sides can be read side by side.
Confirms they are equal with np.array_equal(...) and prints the True result.



If you’re stuck

Pick any numbers for A and B. Compute the left side as (A @ B).T and the right side as B.T @ A.T. To compare two whole arrays for equality, use np.array_equal(left, right) — comparing with plain == gives an array of True/False rather than a single answer.



Show one possible answer

import numpy as np

A = np.array([[1, 2],
              [3, 4]])
B = np.array([[5, 6],
              [7, 8]])

left = (A @ B).T
right = B.T @ A.T

print("(A @ B).T =")
print(left)
print("B.T @ A.T =")
print(right)
print("equal?", np.array_equal(left, right))
Both sides print the same grid, and np.array_equal returns True: transposing a product flips the order of the factors. This identity is why you’ll often see .T rearranged across an @ when lining up shapes in later chapters.

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text: Check yourself

title: Linear algebra in NumPy
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You’ve moved from pictures to running code: arrays for vectors and matrices, .shape to read them, @ for dot products and matrix multiplies, and .T to flip rows and columns. These are the exact operations a model runs at every layer. Next you’ll assemble them into a single artificial neuron — its forward pass written written in the NumPy you practiced here.

title: Correlation vs causation
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Correlation vs causation
text:
The hook
Every summer, two numbers climb together: ice-cream sales and drowning deaths. Track them month by month and the pattern is unmistakable — when one goes up, so does the other. So should we ban ice cream to save lives?
Of course not. Ice cream doesn’t drown anyone. Summer heat drives both: hot weather sells more ice cream and sends more people swimming, where more drownings happen. The heat is hiding behind the scenes, pulling both strings. It is a confounder — a third variable that makes two unrelated things look linked.
This chapter is about telling those two situations apart: when numbers move together (correlation) versus when one number makes the other happen (causation). Mixing them up is one of the most common — and most expensive — mistakes in data work.

You’ll need from before: the mean and how spread is measured (M2 Ch11). We reuse the idea of “distance from the mean” to build correlation.

title: Correlation vs causation
text:
Correlation: a number from −1 to +1
Correlation turns “these two things move together” into a single number, the correlation coefficient, usually written r. It always lands between −1 and +1:

r = +1 — a perfect positive relationship. The points sit exactly on a line that rises to the right: when one variable goes up, the other goes up by a proportional amount, every time.
r = −1 — a perfect negative relationship. The points sit on a line that falls to the right: when one goes up, the other goes down.
r = 0 — no linear relationship. Knowing one variable tells you nothing about whether the other is high or low.
