Values in between measure how tight and which direction the trend is. An r of 0.9 is a strong rise-together pattern with a little scatter; an r of 0.2 is a weak, noisy one; an r of −0.6 is a moderately strong downward trend.
Two cautions worth keeping in mind:

Correlation measures linear co-movement only. A relationship can be strong but curved (think of a U-shape) and still give an r near 0, because it isn’t a straight line.
Correlation has no units and says nothing about steepness. An r of 1.0 does not mean “one-for-one”; it means the points fall perfectly on some straight line, however steep or shallow.


Seeing it: hours studied vs exam score
Here is a small, made-up class: hours each student studied and the score they got. The points drift up to the right — more study, higher score — so we expect a positive r close to 1. The plot draws the scatter and prints the r that goes with it, connecting the picture to the number.






The printed r is about 0.99 — a strong positive correlation, which matches the tidy upward band your eye sees. (The canvas above has no alt text for a screen reader, so the takeaway in words: the points climb steadily from lower-left to upper-right, and the number confirms a near-perfect upward line.)
But notice what the number can’t tell you. A strong r here is consistent with “studying raises scores” — and that’s plausible — but the number alone doesn’t prove it. That’s the whole point of the next section.

title: Correlation vs causation
text:
Causation: a higher bar
To say A causes B is to claim that changing A would change B. Correlation never establishes that on its own, because at least three different stories can produce the same rise-together pattern:

A really does cause B. Studying more does raise scores.
Reverse causation — B causes A. Maybe the relationship runs the other way from what you assumed. (Do people exercise because they’re healthy, or are they healthy because they exercise? The correlation looks the same either way.)
A confounder causes both. A hidden third variable drives A and B together, so they correlate without either causing the other — the ice-cream-and-drowning case, with summer heat behind both.

Because all three produce the same correlation, a number alone can’t pick the winner. To earn the word causation, you need more:

A controlled experiment — change A on purpose for a randomly chosen group, hold everything else as steady as you can, and see if B changes. Randomising breaks the link to confounders, which is why randomised trials are the gold standard.
Failing that, statistically controlling for the variables you suspect — for example, comparing ice-cream sales and drownings within days of the same temperature, so heat can’t be doing the work.

The headline to carry away: a correlation is a clue, not a verdict. It tells you where to look, not what’s true.

title: Correlation vs causation
text:
Spotting the confounder
The practical skill is asking, whenever two things correlate: “What else could be driving both?” A confounder is a variable that sits upstream of both measurements and creates the link.

Ice-cream sales ↔︎ drownings — confounder: summer heat.
Shoe size ↔︎ reading ability in children — confounder: age (older kids have bigger feet and read better).
Number of firefighters at a blaze ↔︎ amount of damage — confounder: the size of the fire (bigger fires draw more crews and cause more damage).

In every case, acting on the raw correlation — banning ice cream, handing out big shoes, sending fewer firefighters — would fail, because you’d be tugging on something that was never the cause. Naming the confounder is what turns a misleading number into an understood one.

title: Correlation vs causation
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Correlation is a single number, r, from −1 to +1: +1 perfect rise-together, −1 perfect opposite, 0 no linear link.
Correlation measures linear co-movement only — a strong curved relationship can still show r near 0.
Correlation is not causation. The same correlation can come from A→B, B→A (reverse causation), or a confounder driving both.
To claim causation you need an experiment or careful control of other variables — a high r on its own is not enough.
When two things correlate, ask “what else could drive both?” — that hidden third variable is the confounder.

title: Correlation vs causation
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
NumPy already knows how to compute a correlation coefficient. Complete correlation(x, y) so it returns the r between two lists, using np.corrcoef(x, y), which gives a 2×2 matrix whose off-diagonal entry [0, 1] is the r you want. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

np.corrcoef(x, y) returns a 2×2 matrix. The diagonal is each variable’s correlation with itself (always 1). The number you want — x with y — is the off-diagonal entry, np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1].



Show solution

import numpy as np

def correlation(x, y):
    return np.corrcoef(x, y)[0, 1]
[2, 4, 6, 8] is exactly 2 * the x values, so the points sit on a perfect rising line and r is +1. [8, 6, 4, 2] falls as x rises, a perfect line the other way, so r is −1.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Correlation is built on a simpler quantity: covariance, the average of “how far x is from its mean” times “how far y is from its mean”. When x and y tend to be above (or below) their means together, the products are positive and covariance is positive; when one is high while the other is low, the products are negative.
Write covariance(x, y) for the population covariance: mean of (x − mean(x)) * (y − mean(y)). Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Build it in pieces: np.mean(x) is the mean of x. The deviations are x - np.mean(x) and y - np.mean(y). Multiply the two deviation arrays element-wise, then take np.mean(...) of the result.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def covariance(x, y):
    x = np.array(x)
    y = np.array(y)
    return np.mean((x - np.mean(x)) * (y - np.mean(y)))
For [1, 2, 3, 4] the mean is 2.5, so the deviations are −1.5, −0.5, 0.5, 1.5. Multiplied by themselves they give 2.25, 0.25, 0.25, 2.25, which sum to 5; divided by 4 values that’s 1.25. When the second list runs the other way, every product flips sign, giving −1.25.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Name a pair of things from everyday life that you’d expect to correlate, state the confounder you think is really behind both, and explain why acting on the correlation as if it were causal would backfire.
Rubric — a good answer:

Names a believable correlated pair (two things that really do move together).
Identifies a plausible confounding variable that drives both.
Explains the failure: what action someone might take on the raw correlation, and why it wouldn’t get the result they want because the real cause is the confounder.



Show one possible answer

A classic pair: cities with more parks tend to have healthier residents, so a city council might think “build parks → healthier people” and pour the budget into parkland alone.
The likely confounder is household wealth. Wealthier neighbourhoods tend to have both more green space and residents who can afford good food, healthcare, and leisure time to exercise. Wealth drives both the parks and the health.
Acting on the raw correlation could backfire: dropping parks into a struggling neighbourhood without addressing income, food access, or healthcare may produce little health gain, because the parks were a marker of wealth, not the cause of the health. The fix is to compare neighbourhoods of similar wealth, or run a trial, before concluding parks themselves do the work.

title: Correlation vs causation
text: Check yourself

title: Correlation vs causation
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now read an r as strength-and-direction, and you know its limit: a correlation is a clue about a relationship, never proof that one thing causes another. That caution is the backbone of honest data analysis — and it sets up the modelling work ahead, where we fit lines to data and have to keep asking what the numbers really mean. Next we turn correlation into a line you can draw and use.

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
text:
The hook
Two cricket batters both average 40 runs an innings. One scores 38, 41, 39, 42 — clockwork. The other scores 5, 90, 8, 77 — thrilling and terrifying. Same average, completely different players. The average alone hides the story; you also need to know how much the numbers bounce around it.
That is the whole idea of this chapter. You will summarise any set of numbers with three values that work as a team: the mean (the centre), the variance (how spread out the numbers are), and the standard deviation (that same spread, back in the data’s own units so you can read it). Three numbers, and a list of any length has a story you can tell.

You’ll need from before: the idea of an average and reasoning about a set of outcomes (M2 Ch9 — Probability basics). That’s the only tool we carry in.

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
section: The mean: the centre of the data
text:
The mean: the centre of the data
The mean (the everyday “average”) is the balance point of a list. Add up the values and divide by how many there are:
mean = sum(values) / len(values)
For [2, 4, 6] that is (2 + 4 + 6) / 3 = 12 / 3 = 4.0. The mean answers “if I had to pick one number to stand in for the whole list, what would it be?” It is the centre our other two measures are taken relative to.

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
section: Variance: the average squared distance from the mean
text:
Variance: the average squared distance from the mean
Knowing the centre is not enough — our two batters share a centre. We want to measure how far the values sit from that centre, on average. Here is the recipe:

Find the mean m.
For each value v, take its distance from the mean, v - m (this is called the deviation).
Square each deviation, (v - m)**2.
Average those squared deviations.

That average is the variance:
variance = sum((v - m)**2 for v in values) / len(values)
Why square the deviations? Two reasons. First, deviations come in both signs — values below the mean give negative deviations, values above give positive ones — and if you averaged them raw they would cancel to zero every time. Squaring makes every term non-negative, so they add up instead of cancelling. Second, squaring punishes big misses more than small ones, so a wild outlier moves the variance a lot.
Work it through for [2, 4, 6], whose mean is 4.0:
variance = ((2-4)**2 + (4-4)**2 + (6-4)**2) / 3 = (4 + 0 + 4) / 3 = 8 / 3 ≈ 2.6667
And for [5, 5, 5] every value is the mean, so every deviation is zero and the variance is 0.0 — no spread at all. A variance of zero means the numbers are all identical.

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
section: Standard deviation: spread in the original units

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s see what these three numbers describe. We plot a small list of values as points, draw a vertical line at the mean, and shade the band one standard deviation to each side of it. The mean marks the centre; the band shows the typical spread.






Read the picture: the dashed line sits at the mean, 6.0, the balance point of the dots. The shaded band reaches one standard deviation (about 2.83) to each side, so it covers the values close to the centre while the far dots at 2 and 10 poke out past it. A wider band would mean more spread; if every dot landed on the line, the band would shrink to nothing and the standard deviation would be 0.

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

The mean is the centre of a list: sum(values) / len(values).
The variance is how spread out the numbers are — the average of the squared distances from the mean: sum((v - mean)**2 for v in values) / len(values).
Squaring stops positive and negative deviations from cancelling and weights big misses more heavily; a variance of 0 means every value equals the mean.
The standard deviation is sqrt(variance) — the same spread brought back into the data’s own units, so it is the number you actually report.

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
The mean is the sum of the values divided by how many there are. Complete mean(values) by replacing ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Add the values with sum(values), count them with len(values), and divide the first by the second: sum(values) / len(values).



Show solution

def mean(values):
    return sum(values) / len(values)
For [2, 4, 6] the sum is 12 and the count is 3, so the mean is 12 / 3 = 4.0. When every value is the same, the mean is that value.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now measure the spread. Write variance(values) — the average of the squared deviations from the mean. Compute the mean inside the function, then complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Each value’s deviation from the mean is v - m. Square it with (v - m) ** 2, so the list comprehension reads [(v - m) ** 2 for v in values]. The function then averages those squares.



Show solution

def variance(values):
    m = sum(values) / len(values)
    squared_deviations = [(v - m) ** 2 for v in values]
    return sum(squared_deviations) / len(values)
For [2, 4, 6] the mean is 4.0, the squared deviations are 4, 0, 4, and their average is 8 / 3 ≈ 2.667. For [5, 5, 5] every deviation is 0, so the variance is 0.0.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Take a small list of your own and compute its standard deviation — the square root of the variance — then explain in a sentence what that number tells you in the data’s own units.
Rubric — a good answer:

Computes the mean and variance of a small list, then takes the square root of the variance (variance ** 0.5 or math.sqrt(variance)).
Prints the standard deviation.
Explains it in plain words and in the data’s units — e.g. “the values sit about X away from the mean of Y, on average.”



Show one possible answer

import math

def mean(values):
    return sum(values) / len(values)

def variance(values):
    m = mean(values)
    return sum((v - m) ** 2 for v in values) / len(values)

scores = [2, 4, 6]
m = mean(scores)
v = variance(scores)
std = math.sqrt(v)

print(f"mean = {m}, variance = {round(v, 4)}, std = {round(std, 4)}")
print(f"The scores sit about {round(std, 2)} away from the mean of {m}, on average.")
For [2, 4, 6] the standard deviation is sqrt(8/3) ≈ 1.63. Because it is in the same units as the scores themselves, you can read it directly: a typical score is about 1.6 away from the centre of 4.0. A larger standard deviation would mean the scores are more scattered.

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
text: Check yourself

title: Mean, variance & standard deviation
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now boil any list of numbers down to three: the mean for its centre, the variance for how spread out it is, and the standard deviation for that spread in readable units. These show up everywhere in machine learning — for scaling features, spotting outliers, and describing how confident a model is. Next, in Ch12 — Bayes, intuitively, we turn from summarising data to updating beliefs: how a new piece of evidence should change what you already thought was likely.

title: Probability basics
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Probability basics
text:
The hook
Flip a fair coin. Will it land heads? You can’t know — but you can say how likely it is: half the time. That “half” is a probability, and probability is how AI reasons about anything it can’t be certain of: which word comes next, which class a photo belongs to, how confident a prediction is.
A probability is a single number on a fixed scale from 0 to 1. Zero means the event can’t happen; one means it’s certain; 0.5 means it’s as likely as not. Nothing is ever below 0 or above 1. Once you can put a number on “how likely”, you can compare events, combine them, and let a model act on them.

You’ll need from before: the idea that math here starts with a picture and a little arithmetic (M2 Ch1). No new tools — counting and multiplying are enough.

title: Probability basics
text:
Probability = favourable ÷ total
When every outcome is equally likely, the recipe is a count and a division:
\[
P(\text{event}) = \frac{\text{favourable outcomes}}{\text{total outcomes}}
\]

A coin has 2 equally likely outcomes (heads, tails). One of them is heads, so the chance of heads is 1 / 2 = 0.5.
A six-sided die has 6 equally likely outcomes. One of them is a 6, so the chance of rolling a 6 is 1 / 6 ≈ 0.167.
An event can cover several outcomes. “Roll an even number” is favoured by three outcomes (2, 4, 6) out of 6, so its probability is 3 / 6 = 0.5.

That last line is worth pausing on: an outcome is one single result, an event is the set of outcomes you care about. The probability is the share of total outcomes that count as favourable. Because the favourable count is never more than the total, the answer always lands between 0 and 1.

title: Probability basics
section: Independent events: the chance of both
text:
Independent events: the chance of both
Two events are independent when one happening tells you nothing about the other. A coin flip and a die roll are independent; a second coin flip doesn’t remember the first. For independent events, the chance that both happen is the product of their probabilities:
\[
P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \times P(B)
\]
So two heads in a row is 0.5 * 0.5 = 0.25 — it happens about a quarter of the time. Multiplying makes sense because each event narrows the field: half of all flips start with a head, and half of those follow with another head, leaving a quarter. Notice the product is never larger than either part, which matches intuition — asking for two things to both happen is harder than asking for one.
A quick check on the formula’s edges: if P(A) = 1 (certain), then P(A and B) is 1 * P(B) = P(B) — a certain event doesn’t change the odds of the other. If either probability is 0, the product is 0: one impossible event makes the pair impossible.






Note🧱 For Java developers





Model a probability as a double constrained to [0.0, 1.0] — not a percentage int like 50, which invites off-by-100 bugs when you multiply (0.5 * 0.5 = 0.25, but 50 * 50 = 2500 is nonsense). Independence is captured by plain multiplication: double both = pA * pB;, no special library needed.
When you want to check a probability empirically, reach for a seeded RNG so runs are reproducible: new Random(42) in Java, or random.Random(42) in Python. Counting how often two flips both come up heads over many trials converges toward 0.25 — the simulation and the formula agree, which is a good way to sanity-check either one.

title: Probability basics
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s picture the outcomes of a fair six-sided die. Each face is equally likely, so all six bars sit at the same height, 1/6 ≈ 0.167. A fair process looks flat — no outcome is favoured over another.






The bars are level because the die is fair, and they add up to 1 — together the six outcomes cover every possibility. That “flat shape sums to 1” is the picture behind the favourable-÷-total recipe: split the whole (1) evenly across the equally likely outcomes, and read off the share you care about.

title: Probability basics
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A probability is a number from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain) describing how likely an event is; 0.5 is as likely as not.
When outcomes are equally likely, P(event) = favourable / total — a head on a coin is 1/2 = 0.5, a 6 on a die is 1/6.
An event can cover several outcomes (e.g. “even” = 2, 4, 6), so its probability is the share of total outcomes that count.
Events are independent when one says nothing about the other; then P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B) — two heads is 0.5 * 0.5 = 0.25.

title: Probability basics
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Complete probability so it returns the share of outcomes that are favourable — favourable divided by total. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The recipe is one division: favourable outcomes over total outcomes, favourable / total. For a 6 on a die that’s 1 / 6.



Show solution

def probability(favourable, total):
    return favourable / total

run_tests([
    ("one head in a coin flip", probability(1, 2), 0.5),
    ("two heads out of four equally likely pairs", probability(1, 4), 0.25),
    ("even number on a die", probability(3, 6), 0.5),
])
favourable / total is the share of equally likely outcomes that count: 1 / 2 is 0.5, and 3 / 6 is also 0.5 because three of the six faces are even.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write both_independent(p_a, p_b) — the chance that two independent events both happen. For independent events you multiply their probabilities. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Independence means you multiply: p_a * p_b. Two halves give 0.5 * 0.5 = 0.25, and a certain event (1.0) leaves the other probability unchanged.



Show solution

def both_independent(p_a, p_b):
    return p_a * p_b

run_tests([
    ("two heads in a row", both_independent(0.5, 0.5), 0.25),
    ("a head then a 1-in-5 event", both_independent(0.5, 0.2), 0.1),
    ("a certain event then a 0.3 event", both_independent(1.0, 0.3), 0.3),
])
p_a * p_b combines independent events: each one narrows the field, so the chance of both is smaller than either alone — unless one is certain (1.0), which leaves the other untouched.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). A fair coin has a 0.5 chance of heads on each flip, and flips are independent. Compute the chance of getting three heads in a row, then print it. Explain in a comment why you multiply.
Rubric — a good answer:

States the per-flip probability of heads (0.5) and that the flips are independent.
Combines three independent events by multiplying their probabilities.
Prints the result (0.125) and notes that more required flips makes the chance smaller.



Show one possible answer

def both_independent(p_a, p_b):
    return p_a * p_b

p_head = 0.5  # fair coin, independent flips

# Three heads: multiply the per-flip probabilities together.
three_heads = both_independent(both_independent(p_head, p_head), p_head)
print(f"P(three heads in a row) = {three_heads}")  # 0.125
Each flip is independent, so the chance of all three is 0.5 * 0.5 * 0.5 = 0.125 — about one run in eight. Every extra required head halves the chance, because you’re asking for one more independent thing to also go your way.

title: Probability basics
text: Check yourself

title: Probability basics
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now put a number on “how likely”, and combine independent events by multiplying. Next we zoom out from single events to the whole shape of the possibilities — distributions: how probability spreads across all the outcomes at once, and what an “average” outcome means.

title: Gradients
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Gradients
text:
The hook
Picture a ball dropped into a smooth, bowl-shaped valley. Wherever it lands, it rolls downhill — and it always rolls in the steepest downhill direction it can find, until it settles at the bottom. It never has to be told where the bottom is. At each spot, the shape of the ground under it is enough.
A gradient is the math for “which way is uphill, and how steep.” It is the slope from the previous chapter, generalised: it points in the direction of steepest increase. So its negative points downhill. A learning model is that rolling ball — it measures the gradient of its error, then takes a small step in the opposite direction to make the error a little smaller. Do that again and again and you walk to the bottom of the valley. That repeated downhill walk is gradient descent, and it is how almost every model you will meet learns.

You’ll need from before: a derivative is the slope of a function at a point — how sensitive the output is to a small nudge in the input (M2 Ch6). The gradient is that same slope, used as a direction.

title: Gradients
section: The slope, used as a direction
text:
The slope, used as a direction
For a function of one input, the gradient is the derivative — the slope. There is nothing new to compute; what changes is how you read it.

The slope’s sign tells you which way is uphill. A positive slope means the function rises as x grows, so uphill is to the right; downhill is to the left.
The slope’s size tells you how steep the ground is right there.

To go downhill, you move against the slope. If the slope is positive (uphill is to the right), you step left; if it is negative, you step right. Both cases are captured by one rule — subtract the gradient:
\[\text{new} = \text{old} - \text{learning\_rate} \times \text{gradient}\]
The learning rate is your step size: a small positive number that decides how far each step travels. The gradient sets the direction (downhill) and a sense of steepness; the learning rate scales the distance you actually move.

One concrete bowl: f(x) = x^2
The simplest valley is f(x) = x^2 — a parabola, lowest at x = 0. Its slope at any point x is the derivative 2*x:

At x = 5, the gradient is 2*5 = 10 (steep, sloping up to the right) — so downhill is to the left, toward 0.
At x = -3, the gradient is 2*(-3) = -6 (sloping down to the right) — so downhill is to the right, again toward 0.
At x = 0, the gradient is 0 — flat ground, the bottom. A step moves nowhere.

Plug f(x) = x^2 into the rule and one step from x with learning rate lr is:
\[\text{new} = x - \text{lr} \times (2x)\]
From x = 5 with lr = 0.1: 5 - 0.1 * 10 = 4.0. The ball moved from 5 toward 0, exactly as the picture promised. From x = 4 with a bigger lr = 0.5: 4 - 0.5 * 8 = 0.0 — one large step lands right at the bottom.






Note🧱 For Java developers





Gradient descent is a loop that nudges a value. The shape is one you have written a hundred times:
double x = 10.0;
double lr = 0.1;            // step-size knob
for (int i = 0; i < 50; i++) {
    double grad = 2 * x;    // slope of x*x at the current x
    x = x - lr * grad;      // step downhill
}
// x is now very close to 0, the minimum
The learning rate lr is a tuning knob, like a step size in any iterative solver. Set it too small and the loop crawls — you need many iterations to reach the bottom. Set it too big and each step overshoots the minimum, landing farther up the opposite wall than where it started; the value can bounce outward and blow up to infinity instead of settling. Picking a sane step size is a real part of training models, and it is the same instinct as choosing a stable step in a numerical loop. You will tune it by feel and by watching the value settle.

title: Gradients
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s drop the ball and watch it roll. We start at x = 9 on the bowl f(x) = x^2 and take a handful of descent steps, each one x - lr * (2*x). Plotting the bowl and marking where the ball sits after each step makes the downhill walk visible.






The takeaway is in the spacing of the dots: high on the right wall the slope is steep, so the steps are large; as the ball nears the bottom the slope flattens, the gradient shrinks toward 0, and the steps get tiny. The ball glides in and settles near x = 0 — the minimum — without ever being told where it was. That is gradient descent: follow the negative gradient, and the shape of the valley guides you to the bottom.

title: Gradients
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A gradient is the slope generalised: it points in the direction of steepest increase, so its negative points downhill — the way that decreases the function fastest.
One gradient-descent step is new = old - learning_rate * gradient: nudge the value a small step opposite the gradient to shrink the function.
For f(x) = x^2 the gradient is 2*x, the minimum is at x = 0, and there the gradient is 0, so steps stop moving.
Repeating the step walks toward the minimum; the learning rate sets the step size, and too large a rate overshoots instead of settling.

title: Gradients
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
The heart of learning is one descent step. Complete descent_step(x, grad, lr) so it moves x a small step against the gradient: x - lr * grad. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The rule is new = old - learning_rate * gradient. In code that is x - lr * grad. When grad is 0, lr * grad is 0, so x stays put — which is why a flat spot (the minimum) stops the walk.



Show solution

def descent_step(x, grad, lr):
    return x - lr * grad

run_tests([
    ("step from 10", descent_step(10, 4, 0.1), 9.6),
    ("negative move", descent_step(0, 5, 0.1), -0.5),
    ("zero gradient = no move", descent_step(3, 0, 0.1), 3),
])
x - lr * grad steps opposite the gradient. From x = 10 with grad = 4 and lr = 0.1 that is 10 - 0.1 * 4 = 9.6. When grad = 0 the step is 0, so x does not move — the ball has reached flat ground.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now wire in the gradient for a specific bowl. For f(x) = x^2 the gradient at x is 2*x. Write step_x_squared(x, lr) that does one descent step on this bowl: compute the gradient 2*x, then return x - lr * (2 * x). Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The gradient of f(x) = x^2 is the derivative 2*x — in code, 2 * x. The step then reuses Exercise 1’s rule: x - lr * grad. At x = 0 the gradient is 0, so the value stays at the minimum.



Show solution

def step_x_squared(x, lr):
    grad = 2 * x
    return x - lr * grad

run_tests([
    ("from 5, lr 0.1", step_x_squared(5, 0.1), 4.0),
    ("from 4, lr 0.5", step_x_squared(4, 0.5), 0.0),
    ("already at minimum", step_x_squared(0, 0.1), 0.0),
])
The gradient 2 * x plugged into x - lr * grad gives x - lr * (2 * x). From x = 5, lr = 0.1: 5 - 0.1 * 10 = 4.0. From x = 4, lr = 0.5: 4 - 0.5 * 8 = 0.0. At x = 0 the gradient is 0, so the step leaves x at the minimum.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Start from some x (say 10) and apply step_x_squared a few times in a loop, printing x after each step. Show the value marching toward 0. Then try a much larger learning rate and describe what changes.
Rubric — a good answer:

Loops step_x_squared several times from a starting x, reassigning x each time, and prints the value as it shrinks toward 0.
Reports that with a small rate x settles toward the minimum at 0.
Tries a larger rate and comments on the difference (faster, or overshooting and bouncing if the rate is too big).



Show one possible answer

def step_x_squared(x, lr):
    grad = 2 * x
    return x - lr * grad

# A small, stable rate: x glides down to the minimum.
x = 10.0
for i in range(6):
    x = step_x_squared(x, 0.1)
    print(f"step {i + 1}: x = {round(x, 4)}")

print("---")

# Too large a rate: each step overshoots and the value bounces outward.
x = 10.0
for i in range(6):
    x = step_x_squared(x, 1.1)
    print(f"big-rate step {i + 1}: x = {round(x, 4)}")
With lr = 0.1 the value shrinks toward 0 and settles — the ball rolls into the bottom of the bowl. With lr = 1.1 each step jumps past the minimum to a point farther up the opposite wall, so x grows in size and bounces outward instead of settling. That is overshooting: the step size matters as much as the direction.

title: Gradients
text: Check yourself

title: Gradients
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You now have the engine of learning: read the gradient, step the opposite way, repeat. On a one-input bowl that is the whole story. Real models, though, are deep stacks of functions, and to get the gradient of the error with respect to a buried weight you need to chain slopes together through every layer. Next we meet exactly that tool — the chain rule (Ch8) — which turns a single derivative into the backpropagation that trains real networks.

title: Dot products & similarity
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Dot products & similarity
text:
The hook
Two friends rate how much they like each movie genre on a scale of 0 to 5: action, comedy, romance. Ana is [5, 1, 0] — loves action, mild on comedy, cold on romance. Bo is [4, 2, 0] — close to Ana. Cleo is [0, 1, 5] — the near-opposite, all-in on romance. Who shares taste with whom?
You can feel the answer, but a recommender system needs a number. The dot product is that number. It takes two vectors, multiplies their matching entries, and adds everything up into a single score. When two taste vectors point the same way, that score is big and positive — they are similar. When they point in unrelated directions, the score sits near zero. This one operation is how a model measures “how alike are these two things?”

You’ll need from before: a vector as a row of numbers that is also a point and an arrow with a direction (M2 Ch2). That is the one tool we carry in.

title: Dot products & similarity
section: Multiply the pairs, add them up
text:
Multiply the pairs, add them up
The dot product of two vectors is the sum of their paired products:
\[a \cdot b = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + \cdots + a_n b_n\]
Walk through the matching entries, multiply each pair, and add. A concrete one:
\[[1, 2, 3] \cdot [4, 5, 6] = 1 \times 4 + 2 \times 5 + 3 \times 6 = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32\]
The result is always a single number, never another vector. Two more to fix the pattern: \([2, 2] \cdot [3, 3] = 6 + 6 = 12\), and \([1, 0] \cdot [0, 1] = 0 + 0 = 0\).

What the number means
The size and sign of that number carry the meaning:

Big positive dot — the vectors point the same way. Each pair pulls in the same direction, so the products pile up. These two are similar.
Near zero — the vectors are unrelated (at right angles). Where one is large the other is small, so the products cancel or never grow. The genres they care about barely overlap.
Negative — the vectors point in opposite directions. One liking what the other dislikes drags the sum below zero.

Back to the friends. \(\text{Ana} \cdot \text{Bo} = 5\times4 + 1\times2 + 0\times0 =
22\) — a big positive score, shared taste. \(\text{Ana} \cdot \text{Cleo} =
5\times0 + 1\times1 + 0\times5 = 1\) — near zero, almost nothing in common. The dot product ranked the friendships for us, with one multiply-and-add.






Note🧱 For Java developers





The dot product is a loop of multiply-and-add — an accumulator over two arrays:
double dot = 0.0;
for (int i = 0; i < a.length; i++) {
    dot += a[i] * b[i];   // multiply the pair, add to the running total
}
Two related notes. First, a raw dot product grows with the length of the vectors, so longer vectors score higher even when their direction matches no better. To compare directions fairly, divide by both lengths — that is cosine similarity, the dot normalised to the range −1 to 1. We mention it so the name is not a surprise later; this chapter stays with the plain dot product.
Second, you rarely write that loop in production. Libraries like NumPy (and the BLAS kernels beneath them) vectorize it into native, cache-aware code, so you call a @ b or np.dot(a, b) and get speed a hand-rolled loop will not match. You learn the loop to understand the idea; you call the library to run it fast.

title: Dot products & similarity
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s see taste as vectors. We plot Ana, Bo, and Cleo as bars across the three genres, then compute each pair’s dot product. The picture and the numbers should agree on who is alike.






Ana and Bo share a shape — both lean hard into action — while Cleo leans the other way into romance. The dot product turns that visual match into a score:






The numbers match the picture: Ana · Bo = 22 is far larger than Ana · Cleo = 1, so a recommender would call Ana and Bo the close pair. One multiply-and-add per genre, summed, became a similarity score.

title: Dot products & similarity
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

The dot product multiplies matching entries and adds them: \(a \cdot b = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + \cdots + a_n b_n\), always a single number. Example: \([1, 2, 3] \cdot [4, 5, 6] = 32\).
A big positive dot means the vectors point the same way — similar; near zero means unrelated; negative means opposite.
Treat each thing as a vector of features and the dot product becomes a similarity score — that is how we compare two people’s movie tastes.
The same multiply-and-add powers recommenders and the inside of every matrix product; cosine similarity is this dot product normalised by length.

title: Dot products & similarity
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Build the dot product for two 3-element vectors: multiply each matching pair and add the three products. The first two pairs are written for you. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The first two pairs follow the pattern a[index] * b[index]. The third pair uses index 2: a[2] * b[2]. Add it to the two terms already there.



Show solution

def dot(a, b):
    return a[0] * b[0] + a[1] * b[1] + a[2] * b[2]

run_tests([
    ("dot([1,2,3],[4,5,6])", dot([1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]), 32),
    ("dot([1,0,0],[0,1,0])", dot([1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0]), 0),
])
Each matching pair is multiplied and the three products are added: 1*4 + 2*5 + 3*6 = 4 + 10 + 18 = 32. The second test gives 0 because the two vectors never have a non-zero entry in the same slot.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now use the sign to answer a yes/no question: do these two people share taste? Write aligned(a, b) that returns True when the dot product is positive (the vectors point broadly the same way) and False otherwise. The dot is computed for you; complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

You want True only when dot is strictly greater than zero. A comparison like dot > 0 is itself a True/False value you can return directly. Note that a dot of exactly 0 is not greater than 0, so it returns False.



Show solution

def aligned(a, b):
    dot = sum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
    return dot > 0

run_tests([
    ("shared taste", aligned([1, 2], [3, 4]), True),
    ("opposite", aligned([1, 0], [-1, 0]), False),
    ("unrelated", aligned([1, 0], [0, 1]), False),
])
dot > 0 evaluates to a boolean, so we return it straight. [1, 2]·[3, 4] = 11 is positive (True); [1, 0]·[-1, 0] = -1 is negative (False); and [1, 0]·[0, 1] = 0 is not greater than zero, so unrelated tastes return False.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Here are three taste vectors:
amir = [4, 0, 1]
bina = [3, 1, 0]
chen = [0, 2, 5]
Find which two of the three are most similar by dot product. Compute the dot product of all three pairs, print them, and name the closest pair.
Rubric — a good answer:

Computes the dot product for all three pairs (amir·bina, amir·chen, bina·chen).
Prints each pair’s score so the comparison is visible.
States which pair has the largest dot product and is therefore most similar.



Show one possible answer

def dot(a, b):
    return sum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))

amir = [4, 0, 1]
bina = [3, 1, 0]
chen = [0, 2, 5]

scores = {
    "amir·bina": dot(amir, bina),
    "amir·chen": dot(amir, chen),
    "bina·chen": dot(bina, chen),
}
for pair, score in scores.items():
    print(f"{pair} = {score}")

best = max(scores, key=scores.get)
print(f"Most similar pair: {best}")
amir·bina = 12 is the largest (against amir·chen = 5 and bina·chen = 2), so amir and bina share the most taste — both lean into the first genre, which is where the products pile up.

title: Dot products & similarity
text: Check yourself

title: Dot products & similarity
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now turn two vectors into a single similarity score: multiply the pairs, add them up, and read the sign. That score sits at the heart of recommenders, search, and every entry of a matrix product. Next we switch from linear algebra to calculus and meet derivatives = sensitivity (Ch6) — how much an output changes when you nudge an input, the idea that lets a model learn.

title: Matrices as transformations
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
The hook
Last chapter a vector was a point on the grid — an arrow from the origin to a spot. Now picture a whole sheet of those points, like a printed grid on a rubber sheet. A matrix is a way to grab that sheet and move it: pull it wider, press it flatter, spin it, or flip it over. Every point rides along to a new spot.
That is the whole idea of this chapter. A 2x2 matrix is not a table of numbers to memorise — it is an action on space. Feed it a point, and it hands you back a moved point. Before any of the mechanics, hold that picture: a matrix moves things.

You’ll need from before: a vector as a point/arrow on the x-y grid, and reading off its two coordinates [x, y] (M2 Ch2). That’s the only tool we carry in.

title: Matrices as transformations
section: What a matrix does, before how
text:
What a matrix does, before how
Say you have a point [x, y] and a 2x2 matrix written as two rows:
[[a, b],
 [c, d]]
Applying the matrix to the point produces a new point:
[a*x + b*y,
 c*x + d*y]
Read it as a recipe with two lines. The first output number comes from the top row [a, b] combined with the point: a*x + b*y. The second output number comes from the bottom row [c, d]: c*x + d*y. Two rows in, two coordinates out. The point has moved.
What kind of move depends on the four numbers:

Stretch / squash — a diagonal matrix like [[2, 0], [0, 1]] doubles the x-direction and leaves y alone, so space widens. [[1, 0], [0, 0.5]] presses the y-direction flat.
Do nothing — the identity matrix [[1, 0], [0, 1]] returns every point unchanged. Try it on [5, -2]: 1*5 + 0*(-2) = 5 and 0*5 + 1*(-2) = -2, so you get [5, -2] back. It is the “multiply by 1” of matrices.
Flip / rotate — other numbers reflect points across an axis or spin them around the origin (you’ll meet rotation properly in Ch4).

Let’s check the recipe on one point. Take the stretch [[2, 0], [0, 3]] and the point [1, 1]:
first  = 2*1 + 0*1 = 2
second = 0*1 + 3*1 = 3
So [1, 1] moves to [2, 3] — pushed right and up. The matrix moved the point, and the four numbers decided where.






Note🧱 For Java developers





A 2x2 matrix is a double[][] — a nested array {{a, b}, {c, d}} (in Python, a nested list [[a, b], [c, d]]). Applying it to a length-2 vector is a small loop of multiply-and-add: for each output row, sum row[k] * v[k] across the columns. You could hand-write that loop for a 2x2, and for a 3x3 you’d nest another. In practice a library (NumPy here, the way you’d reach for a BLAS-backed routine on the JVM) does it for any size, fast — but the arithmetic underneath is the same multiply-add you’d write yourself.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Pictures make this concrete. Take the four corners of the unit square — (0,0), (1,0), (1,1), (0,1) — and apply the stretch matrix [[2, 0], [0, 1]] to each corner. The matrix doubles every x-coordinate and leaves y unchanged, so the square should widen into a rectangle. We plot the corners before (blue) and after (orange) so you can see the move.






The takeaway is in the picture: the blue square became a wider orange rectangle. The matrix did not touch the y-coordinates (the shape is the same height), but it doubled every x-coordinate (twice as wide). That is what “a matrix moves space” looks like — each corner rode the matrix to its new spot, and the whole shape stretched with them.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A 2x2 matrix is an action on space: feed it a point and it hands back a moved point — stretched, squashed, rotated, or flipped.
Applying [[a,b],[c,d]] to [x,y] gives [a*x + b*y, c*x + d*y] — top row makes the first coordinate, bottom row makes the second.
A diagonal matrix like [[2,0],[0,1]] scales each direction on its own — here, doubling x and leaving y, which widens a square into a rectangle.
The identity matrix [[1,0],[0,1]] leaves every point unchanged — the “multiply by 1” of matrices.

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Complete apply(matrix, v) so it applies a 2x2 matrix to a 2-vector. The first output coordinate is filled in for you; write the second using the bottom row [m[1][0], m[1][1]]. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The second coordinate mirrors the first, but with the bottom row of the matrix: m[1][0]*v[0] + m[1][1]*v[1].



Show solution

def apply(m, v):
    return [m[0][0]*v[0] + m[0][1]*v[1], m[1][0]*v[0] + m[1][1]*v[1]]
Each output coordinate pairs one row of the matrix with the whole vector: top row makes the first number, bottom row makes the second. For [[2,0],[0,3]] on [1,1] that is [2*1 + 0*1, 0*1 + 3*1] = [2, 3].



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
A horizontal stretch scales only the x-direction. Write stretch_x(v, factor) so it multiplies the x-component by factor and leaves the y-component alone — exactly what the diagonal matrix [[factor, 0], [0, 1]] would do. Complete the returned list.









If you’re stuck

You want a two-item list. The first item is v[0] * factor; the second is v[1], untouched. A factor of 1 should give the point back unchanged.



Show solution

def stretch_x(v, factor):
    return [v[0] * factor, v[1]]
Only the x-component is scaled, so [3, 4] with factor 2 becomes [6, 4] — twice as far right, same height. A factor of 1 leaves the point where it was, the horizontal version of the identity.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). The matrix [[-1, 0], [0, 1]] flips points across the y-axis: it negates x and keeps y. Apply it (by hand or in code) to a couple of points such as [3, 2] and [1, 4], then describe in a sentence what happened to each point and to the shape overall.
Rubric — a good answer:

Computes the moved point for at least two inputs, e.g. [3, 2] → [-3, 2].
Notes that the x-coordinate’s sign flipped while y stayed the same.
Describes the move as a mirror reflection across the y-axis (left becomes right).



Show one possible answer

def apply(m, v):
    return [m[0][0]*v[0] + m[0][1]*v[1], m[1][0]*v[0] + m[1][1]*v[1]]

flip = [[-1, 0], [0, 1]]
for point in ([3, 2], [1, 4]):
    print(point, "->", apply(flip, point))
Each point’s x-coordinate changes sign while y is untouched: [3, 2] → [-3, 2] and [1, 4] → [-1, 4]. The whole shape is mirrored across the y-axis — what was on the right is now the same distance to the left, like a reflection in a vertical mirror.

title: Matrices as transformations
text: Check yourself

title: Matrices as transformations
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You now see a matrix as a move: a recipe that takes a point and returns a moved point, stretching or flipping space. Next we ask what happens when you do two moves in a row — stretch then rotate — which leads to matrix multiplication, intuitively (Ch4): combining two transformations into one.

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
text:
The hook
“You need to be great at math to do AI.” You don’t. You need a little math, and mostly you need an intuition for it — a feel for what’s going on — which is far easier and far more useful than memorising formulas.
Here is the whole secret of this module: every idea starts as a picture. You’ll see a vector, see a slope, see a curve flatten out — and only then meet the symbols, which become labels for things you already understand. By the end you’ll assemble a single artificial neuron, the tiny unit that deep learning stacks by the million.

You’ll need from before: NumPy arrays and vectorized thinking (M1 Ch12). That’s the only tool we carry in.

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
section: The little math AI actually uses
text:
The little math AI actually uses
Three small areas cover almost everything in this course:

Linear algebra — vectors and matrices: how to represent data (a row of numbers) and transform it. This is how a model holds and moves information.
Calculus — derivatives and gradients: how an output changes when you nudge an input. This is how a model learns — by nudging itself downhill.
Probability & statistics — chance, distributions, averages: how to reason about uncertain data and summarise it.

That’s it. No proofs, no exam tricks — the parts that show up when you build things.

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
section: A relationship is a line you can draw
text:
A relationship is a line you can draw
Most of the math here is about relationships between numbers. Take a simple one we’ll reuse: the tip tends to grow with the bill. Plot a few restaurant bills against their tips and the relationship jumps out — it’s roughly a line.






Your eye already drew the line through those points. Finding that line — and measuring how well it fits — is a surprising amount of what machine learning does. Later chapters give you the tools: vectors to hold the numbers, a slope (a derivative) to describe the line, and a gradient to improve it.

🔬 Going deeper (optional): the model in M0 Chapter 1 did exactly this in higher dimensions — it found a boundary between flower species instead of a line through tips. Same idea, more numbers.

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
text:
The road to one neuron
Each chapter adds one small piece, and the final chapter snaps them together into a neuron:

Vectors & matrices (Ch2–5) — hold data and measure similarity.
Derivatives & gradients (Ch6–8) — the slope that tells a model which way to improve, and the chain rule that powers learning.
Probability & statistics (Ch9–14) — reason about uncertain data.
NumPy linear algebra → one neuron (Ch15–16) — do it in code, then build the forward and backward pass of a single neuron by hand.

You don’t need to hold all of that now. You need to start — with a picture.

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

AI needs a little math: some linear algebra, some calculus, some probability — and mostly intuition for it.
Every idea here starts as a picture, then meets the symbols.
Much of machine learning is finding and measuring a relationship — often a line through data.
The module builds, piece by piece, toward one artificial neuron.

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
A line is tip = rate * bill. Complete predict_tip so it applies a tip rate to a bill. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

A line through the origin is one multiplication: the bill times the rate, bill * rate.



Show solution

def predict_tip(bill, rate):
    return bill * rate
bill * rate is the straight-line relationship: double the bill and the predicted tip doubles too.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
To measure how well a line fits, you compare predictions to reality. Write abs_error(predicted, actual) — the size of the gap, always positive. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The gap is predicted - actual, but you want its size regardless of sign. The built-in abs(...) makes any number non-negative.



Show solution

def abs_error(predicted, actual):
    return abs(predicted - actual)
predicted - actual can be positive or negative; abs(...) returns its size, so an over-estimate and an under-estimate of the same gap count the same.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Pick a tip rate and use predict_tip to predict tips for a few bills, then compare to these real tips with abs_error. Which rate makes the gaps smallest? Print your numbers.
Rubric — a good answer:

Tries at least two different rates and predicts tips for several bills.
Uses abs_error to measure the gap for each, and reports the totals.
States which rate fit best — the smallest total gap.



Show one possible answer

def predict_tip(bill, rate):
    return bill * rate

def abs_error(predicted, actual):
    return abs(predicted - actual)

bills = [10, 20, 30, 40]
real_tips = [2, 3, 5, 6]

for rate in (0.12, 0.15):
    total = sum(abs_error(predict_tip(b, rate), t) for b, t in zip(bills, real_tips))
    print(f"rate {rate}: total gap {round(total, 2)}")
The rate with the smaller total gap fits these tips better — a hand-cranked version of what “training a model” automates with calculus in the chapters ahead.

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
text: Check yourself

title: Why math for AI (and how little you need to start)
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You’ve seen the plan and the spirit: a little math, lots of pictures, building to one neuron. Next we meet the first piece — vectors: how a row of numbers becomes a point and an arrow you can reason about.

title: Notebooks, the IDE & Colab
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Notebooks, the IDE & Colab
text:
The hook
You’ve written real Python over thirteen chapters — and every line of it ran right here, in a notebook inside your browser, with nothing to install. That’s one place to write code. There are a few others, and choosing well saves you hours.
Think of it like a kitchen. A microwave is fast and needs no setup — great for a quick snack. A full stove is more work to set up but right for a real meal. A restaurant kitchen has equipment you’d never buy for home. None is “better”; you pick by the job. Writing Python is the same: the same language runs in a browser notebook, a code editor on your laptop, or a cloud machine with a GPU. This chapter is the what and when of those places.

You’ll need from before: that you import packages and run cells here (Ch1), and that each project keeps its packages in an environment you install (Ch13).

title: Notebooks, the IDE & Colab
section: The same Python, in different places
text:
The same Python, in different places
Here’s the idea to hold onto: a place to write code is not a different language. for, def, and import mean the same thing everywhere. What changes between places is three practical things:

Install — how much you set up before the first line runs.
Cost — whether it’s free (₹0) or costs money.
Compute — how much hardware you can reach, especially a GPU (a chip that trains models far faster than a laptop’s processor).

With that lens, here are the four places you’ll meet in this course.

1. An in-browser notebook (where you are now)
A notebook is a page of cells — small blocks you run one at a time — mixed with prose. The one you’re reading runs Python in the browser itself through a runtime called Pyodide: zero install, ₹0, nothing to break. It’s the best place to learn and to try a quick idea, which is why the whole of M1 lives here.


2. A local code editor / IDE
An IDE (Integrated Development Environment) is an app on your own machine for writing code — VS Code and PyCharm are common ones. Your code lives in files on your disk, runs against the virtual environment you set up in Ch13, and the editor adds tools: autocomplete, a debugger, and search across many files. This is the home of a real project — anything with more than one file that you’ll keep and grow.


3. Jupyter notebooks
Jupyter is the notebook format the browser page imitates, run on your own machine. Same cells-plus-prose feel, but with your local environment and files behind it. It shines for data exploration: load a table, plot it, tweak a cell, re-run, repeat — keeping the story and the output side by side.


4. A free cloud notebook with a GPU
When a task needs more muscle than a laptop — training a neural network, say — you open a notebook on a cloud machine that has a free GPU. Google Colab and Kaggle both give you this at ₹0. You’ll use them from Module 5 on; your laptop never has to do the heavy lifting.

💡 On a free cloud notebook (not here). This page runs in your browser, so there’s no GPU to switch on right here — but the workflow elsewhere is short. On Google Colab you open the notebook in Colab, then go to Runtime → Change runtime type and choose a GPU; the same notebook now runs on a free cloud GPU. Kaggle is a similar free option: create a notebook, then turn on a GPU accelerator in its settings panel. You write the same Python either way — only the machine under it changes.







Note🧱 For Java developers





A notebook runs cell by cell, each cell sharing one live session — closer to a JShell REPL than to a compiled program: there’s no build step, and state from an earlier cell is still around. Your edit–compile–run loop in IntelliJ maps onto a local IDE here: VS Code or PyCharm is the “real project” home in both worlds, with a debugger and project-wide search. The rule of thumb carries over — explore and demo in a notebook, but build and ship anything multi-file in the IDE.




⚠️ Watch out: because notebook cells share one session, running them out of order can give confusing results (a variable from a cell you ran earlier lingers). When in doubt, re-run from the top.

title: Notebooks, the IDE & Colab
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
You can’t fabricate a GPU here, but you can ask the running Python where it lives. The cell below reports the current runtime — proof that “the same Python” is a real, inspectable thing. Press Run.






The exact strings depend on the build, but the point stands: this is ordinary Python, reporting on itself. Open the same notebook in Colab with a GPU and the identical code runs — the platform line would change, your Python would not.






Same language, three answers — the choice is about the job, not the syntax.

title: Notebooks, the IDE & Colab
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

An in-browser notebook, a local IDE, Jupyter, and a cloud notebook are all places to run the same Python — the language doesn’t change.
They differ in install, cost, and compute: the browser is zero-install and ₹0; a local IDE owns your files; a cloud notebook reaches a free GPU.
Pick by the job: a browser notebook to learn or try a quick idea, a local IDE for a real multi-file project, Colab or Kaggle when you need a GPU.
Notebook cells share one session, so re-run from the top if results look off.

title: Notebooks, the IDE & Colab
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete where_to_run so it picks a place based on whether the task needs a GPU. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

When no GPU is needed, the answer is the place you’re in now — the in-browser notebook, which is free. The expected text is exactly "in-browser (₹0)".



Show solution

def where_to_run(needs_gpu):
    if needs_gpu:
        return "Colab (free GPU)"
    return "in-browser (₹0)"

run_tests([
    ("gpu work goes to Colab", where_to_run(True), "Colab (free GPU)"),
    ("no gpu stays in browser", where_to_run(False), "in-browser (₹0)"),
])
When needs_gpu is True the function returns early with the Colab answer; otherwise it falls through to the in-browser, ₹0 option.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write recommend(needs_gpu, real_project) using if/elif/else: GPU work wins first, then a real project, otherwise the in-browser notebook. Complete the marked lines.









If you’re stuck

The elif branch handles a real project — that’s the "local IDE". The final else is the everyday case, "in-browser notebook". Note needs_gpu is checked first, so a GPU task never reaches the other branches.



Show solution

def recommend(needs_gpu, real_project):
    if needs_gpu:
        return "Colab or Kaggle"
    elif real_project:
        return "local IDE"
    else:
        return "in-browser notebook"

run_tests([
    ("gpu wins", recommend(True, True), "Colab or Kaggle"),
    ("project -> IDE", recommend(False, True), "local IDE"),
    ("otherwise notebook", recommend(False, False), "in-browser notebook"),
])
The branches are checked top to bottom: needs_gpu first, so GPU work always routes to Colab or Kaggle; then a real project goes to the local IDE; everything else stays in the in-browser notebook.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). In two or three sentences, describe where you will write code for the rest of this course and why. Name at least two of the four places and the kind of task each is for.
Rubric — a good answer:

Names at least two of the four places (in-browser notebook, local IDE, Jupyter, cloud notebook).
Ties each named place to a task (quick learning, a real project, or GPU work).
Mentions that the choice is about install/cost/compute, not a different language.



Show one possible answer

plan = {
    "learning M1–M4": "in-browser notebook (₹0, nothing to install)",
    "my own project": "local IDE (VS Code, files on my machine)",
    "training a model in M5+": "Colab or Kaggle (free GPU)",
}
for task, place in plan.items():
    print(f"{task}: {place}")
“I’ll keep learning here in the browser because it’s ₹0 and there’s nothing to set up. When I start my own multi-file project I’ll move to VS Code so I get a debugger and my files on disk. Once I need to train a model in Module 5 I’ll open a Colab or Kaggle notebook for the free GPU — same Python throughout, with different machines under it.”

title: Notebooks, the IDE & Colab
text: Check yourself

title: Notebooks, the IDE & Colab
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You now know the four places you’ll write Python — and that picking one is about the job (quick learning, a real project, or needing a GPU), never about a different language. That closes the M1 lesson chapters. Next come the module quiz, which checks the whole of your Python foundation, and the data-cleaning capstone, where you turn the messy habits.csv into a tidy table with the functions you’ve built.

title: A NumPy preview
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: A NumPy preview
text:
The hook
Suppose you want to double every step count in a week, or count how many days you hit your goal. With a plain Python list you reach for a loop — write it, track an index or a running total, append to a new list. NumPy lets you say what you want to the whole column at once: steps * 2 doubles every value, and steps >= 8000 tests every day in one go. No loop, no bookkeeping.
That “do the maths on the entire array at once” idea is called vectorized thinking, and it’s the foundation of the data and AI tools coming in M2 and M3. This chapter is your first taste.

You’ll need from before: loops and conditions (Ch2) — the very thing NumPy lets you stop writing by hand — and lists (Ch4), which is what an array is built from.

title: A NumPy preview
text:
What is a NumPy array?
NumPy is Python’s numerical-computing library. Its core type is the array: an ordered container of numbers that all share one type. You import the library under the customary short name and build an array from a list:
import numpy as np

steps = np.array([7000, 8200, 9000, 6500, 8000])
A list and an array look similar, but an array is built for maths on the whole collection. That difference is the whole point of this chapter.

title: A NumPy preview
text:
Element-wise operations (no loop needed)
Do arithmetic on an array and the operation applies to every element, giving back a new array of the same length:
steps * 2        # every value doubled
steps + 100      # 100 added to every value
steps >= 8000    # a True/False for every value
The last one returns a boolean array — the result of testing each element against 8000. Compare that with a list, where steps * 2 would repeat the list and steps >= 8000 would be an error. With an array, the operation reaches every element for you — this is what “vectorized” means.

title: A NumPy preview
text:
Summary methods
An array carries methods that reduce all its values to one number:
steps.mean()     # the average
steps.sum()      # the total
steps.max()      # the largest value
One note for later: steps.mean() gives back a NumPy float, not a plain Python float. It prints and compares like a number, so for everyday use you won’t notice the difference.

title: A NumPy preview
text:
Boolean masking
A boolean array can select elements. Put the test inside the square brackets and you get back only the elements where it was True:
steps[steps >= 8000]      # the values that met the goal
(steps >= 8000).sum()     # how many met it (True counts as 1)
steps >= 8000 builds the mask; steps[...] keeps the matching values; .sum() on the mask counts the Trues, because True behaves as 1 and False as 0. When you want a plain Python int from that count, wrap it: int((steps >= 8000).sum()).






Note🧱 For Java developers





A NumPy array is closer to a typed numeric buffer than to int[] plus a loop. In Java you’d write for (int i = 0; i < a.length; i++) b[i] = a[i] * 2;. In NumPy a * 2 expresses the same intent in one line, and the library runs the per-element work in compiled code — closer in spirit to bulk or SIMD-style math than to element-by-element iteration. The boolean mask a[a >= 8000] replaces the filter-into-a-new-list loop entirely.

title: A NumPy preview
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
A week of step counts, handled without a single loop. Press Run.






steps * 2 reaches every value; steps >= 8000 tests every day at once and gives back [False  True  True False  True]. No index, no loop.






The mask steps >= goal does double duty: .sum() counts the Trues (three days) and steps[mask] keeps the matching values. Wrapping the count in int(...) gives a plain Python integer, handy when exact equality matters.

title: A NumPy preview
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A NumPy array (np.array([...])) holds numbers of one type and is built for maths on the whole collection.
Element-wise operations like arr * 2 and arr >= 8000 apply to every element at once — no Python loop.
Summary methods arr.mean(), arr.sum(), arr.max() reduce the whole array to a single value.
A boolean mask selects (arr[arr >= 8000]) and counts (int((arr >= 8000).sum())) the matching elements.

title: A NumPy preview
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete array_mean so it builds an array from values and returns its average. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The average is the mean. An array has a method with that exact name — arr.mean(). The grader compares numbers with a tolerance, so a NumPy float is fine here.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def array_mean(values):
    arr = np.array(values)
    return arr.mean()

run_tests([
    ("two values", array_mean([8000, 9000]), 8500.0),
    ("all the same", array_mean([5000, 5000, 5000]), 5000.0),
])
arr.mean() averages every element of the array. [8000, 9000] averages to 8500.0; three equal values average to that same value.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write count_active(steps, goal) — build an array and return, as a plain int, how many step counts are at least goal. Use a boolean mask. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The mask is arr >= goal. Counting its True values is (arr >= goal).sum(), and int(...) around it returns a plain Python integer.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def count_active(steps, goal):
    arr = np.array(steps)
    return int((arr >= goal).sum())

run_tests([
    ("two of three", count_active([7000, 8200, 9000], 8000), 2),
    ("none qualify", count_active([100, 200], 8000), 0),
    ("both qualify", count_active([8000, 9000], 8000), 2),
])
arr >= goal builds a boolean mask; .sum() counts the Trues because True acts as 1; int(...) returns a plain Python integer. With goal 8000, the first list has two qualifying days, the second none, the third two.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Use a boolean mask to select the step values that meet a goal and print them. Build an array, make the mask, and index with it.
Rubric — a good answer:

Builds an array with np.array(...) and a goal value.
Uses a boolean mask to index the array: arr[arr >= goal].
Prints the selected values and runs on a mix of above- and below-goal days.



Show one possible answer

import numpy as np

def active_values(steps, goal):
    arr = np.array(steps)
    return arr[arr >= goal]

print(active_values([7000, 8200, 9000, 6500, 8000], 8000))   # [8200 9000 8000]
arr >= goal is a boolean mask, and arr[mask] keeps only the elements where the mask is True. Here three days meet the 8000 goal, so those three values print.

title: A NumPy preview
text: Check yourself

title: A NumPy preview
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You’ve met arrays and vectorized thinking — describing maths over a whole column instead of looping element by element. Next chapter, Environments & packages (Ch13), steps back from code to cover how libraries like NumPy get installed and isolated with venv and pip. After M1, NumPy returns in force: it underpins the tables and tensors of M2 (data handling) and M3 (the maths of machine learning), where every operation you saw here scales up to real datasets.

title: Clean, readable code
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Clean, readable code
text:
The hook
You write code once, but you (and others) re-read it many times. The reader you should write for is a tired beginner — maybe yourself, three weeks from now — opening the habits cleaner and trying to understand it fast.
Compare two lines that do the same thing on the habits data:
c = sum(1 for x in d if x >= 8000)      # what is c? what is d? why 8000?
active_days = sum(1 for steps in day_steps if steps >= GOAL)
The second line reads like a sentence. Clarity beats cleverness every time, and this chapter is the handful of habits that get you there.

You’ll need from before: writing and calling your own functions (Ch3) — the main tool for keeping code small and avoiding repetition.

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Descriptive names
A name is a tiny piece of documentation. s and x make the reader decode the code; steps and active_days let them read it.

Name a value for what it holds: steps, not s; active_days, not x.
A function name is what it does: average_steps, not calc.
Short names are fine for short lives — a loop index i, or n for a count in a two-line function. The longer a value lives, the more its name should say.

You don’t need comments to explain a good name — the name already did it.

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Small, single-purpose functions
A function should do one thing you can name. If you can’t name it without saying “and”, it’s probably two functions. Small functions are easier to read, to test, and to reuse.
def is_active(steps):
    """A day is active when it reaches the step goal."""
    return steps >= GOAL
That one idea — “is this day active?” — now has a name you can use anywhere.

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Name your constants
A bare number sitting in the middle of code is a hard-coded number: the reader has to guess what it means, and if it appears five times you have to change all five. Give it a name in UPPER_CASE, once, at the top:
GOAL = 8000          # daily step target

def is_active(steps):
    return steps >= GOAL
Now GOAL explains itself, and moving the target to 10000 is a one-line change.

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Comments explain WHY, not WHAT
Good code already says what it does. A comment earns its place by saying why — the reason a reader can’t see from the code alone.
steps = int(raw.replace(",", ""))   # bad: restates the code
steps = int(raw.replace(",", ""))   # good: some rows store steps as "8,200"
If a comment only repeats the line, delete it and improve the names instead.

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Don’t repeat yourself (DRY)
If you copy a piece of logic, every future fix has to be made in every copy — and one will get missed. Put the logic in a function (Ch3) and call it. That single function becomes the one place the rule lives.
active_days = sum(1 for steps in day_steps if is_active(steps))
best_streak = max(steps for steps in day_steps if is_active(steps))
Both lines lean on the same is_active rule, so “what counts as active” is defined exactly once.






Note🧱 For Java developers





Python’s style conventions differ from Java’s, and the linters enforce them:

Names and functions use snake_case (active_days, average_steps), not camelCase.
You don’t write getters/setters; read and assign attributes directly, and reach for @property only if you later need computed access.
A docstring (the """...""" under a def) is Python’s Javadoc — it documents the function and shows up in help().
Constants are UPPER_CASE by convention — Python has no final keyword, so the capitals are the signal “treat this as fixed”.

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Here is a “before” that counts active days and the percentage active — with cryptic names, a hard-coded 8000 in two places, and the same test copied twice. Press Run.






Now the “after”: descriptive names, one named constant, and a small is_active helper used in both places. It computes the identical result.






Both cells print (3, 60) — same answer, but the second one you can read once and trust. The rule “≥ 8000” now lives in exactly one place (GOAL + is_active), so changing the target is a single edit instead of a hunt.

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Descriptive names (steps, active_days) turn code into something you read, not decode.
Keep functions small and single-purpose, and replace hard-coded numbers with a named constant like GOAL = 8000.
Comments explain WHY, not what the code already says.
DRY: put repeated logic in one function and call it, so a rule is defined once.

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Replace the hard-coded 8000 with a named constant so the goal has a name. Fill in STEP_GOAL, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The target is 8000. Put that value after STEP_GOAL =, so the comparison steps >= STEP_GOAL checks against the named goal.



Show solution

STEP_GOAL = 8000

def is_active(steps):
    return steps >= STEP_GOAL
STEP_GOAL names the target once; is_active reads against that name, so the number 8000 no longer floats unexplained inside the comparison.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write one small, well-named function: average_steps(day_steps) returns the mean of a list of step counts. Guard the empty case so it returns 0 instead of dividing by zero. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

An empty list has length 0, and sum([]) / len([]) would divide by zero. Test for the empty list first — not day_steps is True when the list is empty.



Show solution

def average_steps(day_steps):
    if not day_steps:
        return 0
    return sum(day_steps) / len(day_steps)
The guard if not day_steps returns 0 for the empty list; otherwise sum() / len() is the mean. The name and docstring say exactly what the function does.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Rewrite this cryptic snippet for readability — without changing what it computes. It counts how many days hit the step target and is hard to follow:
def f(l):
    n = 0
    for v in l:
        if v >= 8000:
            n = n + 1
    return n
Rubric — a good answer:

Uses descriptive names for the function, parameter, and loop value.
Replaces the hard-coded 8000 with a named constant.
Pulls the “is this day active?” test into a small helper and reuses it (DRY).
Returns the same count as the original for the same input.



Show one possible answer

STEP_GOAL = 8000   # daily step target

def is_active(steps):
    """A day is active when it reaches the step goal."""
    return steps >= STEP_GOAL

def count_active_days(day_steps):
    """How many days reached the step goal."""
    return sum(1 for steps in day_steps if is_active(steps))

print(count_active_days([8200, 5100, 9300, 6000, 8000]))   # 3
Same count as f, but the names tell the story, STEP_GOAL explains the number, and the active-day rule lives once in is_active.

title: Clean, readable code
text: Check yourself

title: Clean, readable code
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now write code a beginner can re-read: clear names, small functions, named constants, WHY-comments, and no copied logic. Next chapter — Pythonic data idioms — builds on this with the idiomatic patterns that make Python code both shorter and clearer, so readable and concise stop being a trade-off.

title: Reading a traceback
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Reading a traceback
text:
The hook
You run your habits cleaner, and the screen fills with red text — a wall of file paths, arrows, and line numbers. It looks like the program exploded. It didn’t. That wall is a traceback, and it’s the most useful gift Python gives you: a step-by-step record of exactly how it reached the error, ending with what the error was. Learn to read it and a scary wall becomes a map straight to the bug.

You’ll need from before: what an exception is and how an uncaught one stops the program (Ch7), and that values have types (Ch1).

title: Reading a traceback
text:
What a traceback is
A traceback is what Python prints when an exception is not caught. (Caught it with try/except last chapter? Then there’s no traceback — you handled it.) An uncaught exception stops the program, and on the way out Python prints the trail of calls that led to the failure.
The single most important habit: read it bottom-up. The very last line is the punchline; everything above it is the story of how you got there.

title: Reading a traceback
section: The last line: type and message
text:
The last line: type and message
The bottom line of every traceback has the same shape:
ExceptionType: message
The part before the colon is the exception type — NameError, ValueError, and friends. The part after is the message, a human-readable hint. Read this line first, always. It usually tells you most of what you need.

title: Reading a traceback
section: The lines above: the call stack
text:
The lines above: the call stack
Above that last line is the call stack — the chain of function calls that were in progress when the error happened, printed most recent call last. So the frame nearest the bottom (directly above the error line) is where the error actually occurred; the frames above it are who called whom to get there.
Here is a complete traceback. Read it from the bottom:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "clean.py", line 12, in <module>
    summary = average_steps(rows)
  File "clean.py", line 7, in average_steps
    return total / len(rows)
ZeroDivisionError: division by zero
Walk it bottom-up:

Last line — ZeroDivisionError: division by zero. The type is ZeroDivisionError; the message says you divided by zero.
