Frame directly above — File "clean.py", line 7, in average_steps, on return total / len(rows). That’s the buggy line: len(rows) was 0.
Frame above that — line 12 called average_steps(rows). That’s how you reached the bug — useful context, but the fix lives on line 7.

The bug: average_steps was handed an empty list. Read bottom-up, three lines told you the type, the message, and the exact line to fix.

title: Reading a traceback
text:
The common types
You’ll meet the same handful again and again. The type name alone often points straight at the cause:

NameError — you used a name that doesn’t exist (often a typo, or a variable used before it’s defined).
TypeError — an operation got the wrong type, like "3" + 4.
KeyError — a dict key isn’t there, like row["stpes"].
ValueError — the right type but a bad value, like int("8h").
IndexError — a list index is out of range, like rows[99] on a short list.
ZeroDivisionError — you divided by zero.







Note🧱 For Java developers





A Python traceback is Java’s stack trace — with the reading order flipped. Java prints the exception type and message first, then frames top-down; Python prints frames first and the ExceptionType: message last, so you read Python bottom-up. NameError/TypeError/KeyError are runtime errors much like NullPointerException or ClassCastException. And remember from Ch7: Python has no checked exceptions, so nothing is declared in a signature — the traceback at runtime is your full source of truth about what went wrong and where.

title: Reading a traceback
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
We can’t let a cell raise an uncaught error here (it would stop the page), so we catch each error and print the same two facts the bottom line of a traceback shows: the type name and the message. Press Run.






Each line shows type(e).__name__ (the exception type, like ValueError) and the message — exactly what you’d read off the bottom of a traceback, without crashing the page.
You can also capture the whole traceback as a string with the traceback module, then print it on your own terms:






traceback.format_exc() returns the same multi-line text Python would have printed — captured safely in a string. The last line is the type and message, right where you’ve learned to look.

title: Reading a traceback
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A traceback is what an uncaught exception prints — the trail of calls that led to the error.
Read it bottom-up. The last line is ExceptionType: message; read that first.
The lines above are the call stack, most recent call last; scan them for your file and line to find the bug.
The type name alone often names the cause — NameError, TypeError, KeyError, ValueError, IndexError, ZeroDivisionError.

title: Reading a traceback
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
The last line of a traceback is ExceptionType: message. Complete error_type so it returns the type name of an exception object. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

type(exc) gives the class; its .__name__ attribute is the bare string, like "ValueError". That’s the same word that starts a traceback’s last line.



Show solution

def error_type(exc):
    return type(exc).__name__
type(exc) is the exception’s class and .__name__ is its name as a string — the exact text before the colon on a traceback’s bottom line.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write failure_type(func) that calls func() inside a try/except. If it raises, return the exception’s type name (a string); if it doesn’t raise, return "no error". This reports the punchline a traceback would have shown. Complete the marked lines.









If you’re stuck

In the except, you already named the exception e, so its type name is type(e).__name__. The final return runs only when no exception was raised — that’s the "no error" case.



Show solution

def failure_type(func):
    try:
        func()
    except Exception as e:
        return type(e).__name__
    return "no error"
If func() raises, control jumps to the except and returns the caught type’s name. If it returns normally, the except is skipped and the function falls through to "no error".



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Read this traceback, then in a comment name (a) the exception type and (b) the line number in the buggy file you would fix.
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "report.py", line 18, in <module>
    print(mood_label(row))
  File "report.py", line 9, in mood_label
    return LABELS[row["mood"]]
KeyError: 'tired'
Rubric — a good answer:

Reads the last line to name the type: KeyError.
Identifies the buggy line as line 9 (the frame nearest the error, in your file report.py), not line 18 (that line only called the function).
Notes the cause in a word: the dict LABELS has no key 'tired'.



Show one possible answer

# (a) Exception type: KeyError
# (b) Line to fix: line 9 -> LABELS[row["mood"]]
# Why: read bottom-up. The last line is "KeyError: 'tired'", so 'tired'
# is a mood with no entry in LABELS. The frame directly above the error is
# report.py line 9 -- that's the buggy line in our code. Line 18 only
# called the function. Fix: add 'tired' to LABELS or use LABELS.get(...).
The bottom line gives the type and the missing key; the nearest stack frame in your own file gives the line. Line 18 is context, not the fix.

title: Reading a traceback
text: Check yourself

title: Reading a traceback
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now turn a wall of red text into a one-line diagnosis: read bottom-up, name the type, find your line. That closes out errors. Next we move from scripts to structure — the next chapter, enough OOP to be useful — bundling data and the functions that work on it into classes you can reuse.

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text:
The hook
Enough hand-typing rows. The real habits.csv — all 35 days — is sitting right next to this page, and now you’ll load it. When you do, you’ll see something important: real data is messy. Some step counts have commas (7,500), some sleep values carry a stray h (8h), some moods are padded (ok), and a few fields are blank. Loading the file is step one; the rest of the module makes it clean.

You’ll need from before: types and string-to-number conversion (Ch1), and dicts (Ch4) — because each CSV row will arrive as a dict.

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text:
Reading a CSV
Open a file with open(...), ideally inside a with block so it closes itself:
with open("data/habits.csv") as f:
    text = f.read()
But you rarely want the raw text — you want rows. The csv module’s DictReader reads each row into a dict keyed by the column headers:
import csv

with open("data/habits.csv") as f:
    rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))

rows[0]   # {'date': '2026-01-01', 'steps': '7500', 'sleep_hours': '7.5', 'mood': 'good'}
Two things to notice: each row is exactly the dict shape from Chapter 4 (row["steps"]), and every value is a string — '7500', not 7500 — so you’ll convert with int()/float() before any maths (Chapter 1).

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text:
Working with JSON
JSON is a text format for structured data, used everywhere data is sent between programs. The json module converts both ways:
import json

text = json.dumps({"steps": 7500, "mood": "good"})   # object -> JSON text
obj = json.loads(text)                                # JSON text -> object
dumps (“dump string”) serialises; loads (“load string”) parses it back.

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text:
Writing a file
csv.DictWriter writes dict rows back out — useful for saving a cleaned table:
with open("clean.csv", "w", newline="") as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=["date", "steps"])
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerows(some_rows)






Note🧱 For Java developers





with open(...) as f: is Python’s try-with-resources — the file closes automatically at the end of the block. DictReader and the json module replace the BufferedReader/parsing and Jackson/Gson boilerplate you’d reach for in Java, in a couple of lines.




⚠️ Watch out: in the browser, files live in an in-memory filesystem. The habits file was loaded into it for this page, and anything you write lives there too — but it vanishes when you close the tab. That’s perfect for learning, and exactly how the in-browser runner stays ₹0.

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Load the real file, look at it, round-trip a row through JSON, and write a small subset back. Press Run.






Look closely at those rows — steps of '7,500', a blank field, a sleep_hours of '8h'. That mess is real, and cleaning it is the next few chapters.












You read a real file, transformed it, and wrote a new one — entirely in the browser’s in-memory filesystem.

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

csv.DictReader reads each row into a dict keyed by the header; every value is a string, so convert before doing maths.
json.dumps turns a Python object into JSON text; json.loads turns it back.
csv.DictWriter writes dict rows back out to a file.
In Pyodide, files live in an in-memory filesystem — great for learning, but writes disappear when the tab closes.

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
A CSV row arrives as a dict of strings. Complete the marked line so steps_as_int reads the steps field and converts it to a whole number. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

You read a dict field by its key. The steps live under the key "steps", and int(...) turns that string into a number.



Show solution

def steps_as_int(row):
    return int(row["steps"])
row["steps"] is the string "7500"; wrapping it in int(...) gives the number 7500.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write total_steps(rows) — sum the steps across a list of dict rows, converting each from text to an int. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Inside the loop you want this row’s steps as a number — int(row["steps"]) — added to the running total.



Show solution

def total_steps(rows):
    total = 0
    for row in rows:
        total += int(row["steps"])
    return total
Each row’s "steps" string is converted to an int and added to total, which the function returns at the end.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Read the real data/habits.csv with csv.DictReader, and report two numbers: how many rows it has, and how many of those have a missing (blank) steps value. Print both.
Rubric — a good answer:

Opens data/habits.csv with csv.DictReader and counts the rows.
Counts rows whose steps field is the empty string "".
Prints both numbers and runs without error.



Show one possible answer

import csv

with open("data/habits.csv") as f:
    rows = list(csv.DictReader(f))

missing = sum(1 for r in rows if r["steps"] == "")
print("Rows:", len(rows))
print("Rows with missing steps:", missing)
DictReader gives the rows; the count of those whose steps is "" tells you how much patching the cleaning step will need — a real first look at data quality.

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text: Check yourself

title: Reading & writing files (CSV/JSON)
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now load the messy habits.csv, move data through JSON, and write files — the start and end of every data task. You also got your first honest look at the mess. Next we make code fail safely on bad rows with try/except, so one bad value doesn’t crash the whole job.

title: Data structures
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Data structures
text:
The hook
Until now a “day” has been a tuple like ("2026-01-01", 7500). That works, but day[1] is a riddle — was that steps or sleep? A real day has four fields, and you’d much rather write day["steps"]. Python gives you four built-in containers, each suited to a different job. Choosing the right one makes your code read almost like a sentence.

You’ll need from before: loops (Ch2) and functions (Ch3).

title: Data structures
text:
Four containers, four jobs
list — an ordered, changeable sequence. Your go-to for a collection of things, like every day’s steps:
steps = [7500, 8200, 5400]
steps.append(9100)   # add to the end
steps[0]             # 7500  (index from 0)
len(steps)           # 4
dict — a mapping of keys to values: a record of named fields. The natural shape for one row of data:
day = {"date": "2026-01-01", "steps": 7500, "mood": "good"}
day["steps"]         # 7500  (read by name, not position)
day["mood"] = "ok"   # update a field
tuple — like a list, but fixed (immutable): you can’t change it after you make it. Good for a small, fixed group, or for returning several values at once:
location = (12.97, 77.59)   # (latitude, longitude)
location[0]                 # 12.97
# location[0] = 0           # would raise an error — tuples can't change
set — an unordered collection of unique items. Perfect for “what are the distinct values?” and fast “is this in here?” checks:
moods = {"good", "ok", "good"}   # duplicates collapse
moods                            # {"good", "ok"}
"bad" in moods                   # False

Which one when?



You want…
Use a…




an ordered list of things you’ll add to
list


a record with named fields (a data row)
dict


a fixed group, or to return several values
tuple


the unique values, or fast membership tests
set









Note🧱 For Java developers





The rough equivalents: list ≈ ArrayList, dict ≈ HashMap, set ≈ HashSet, tuple ≈ an immutable record or fixed array. Two freedoms feel new: you don’t declare element types (a list can hold mixed types), and dict/set literals are built right into the language with {} and [] rather than constructors.

title: Data structures
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s remodel the habits data the readable way: each day a dict, all days a list, and the distinct moods a set. Press Run.






day["steps"] says what it means, the set removes the duplicate "good" for free, and the loop reads each record by name. Compare that to juggling day[1].

title: Data structures
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

list — an ordered, changeable sequence; index with [i], grow with append.
dict — a record of named fields; read by key (day["steps"]) — the natural shape for a data row.
tuple — a fixed, immutable ordered group; handy for returning several values.
set — unordered unique items; ideal for distinct values and membership tests.

title: Data structures
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the marked line so get_steps reads the "steps" field from a day dict. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

You read a dict field by its key in square brackets: day["steps"].



Show solution

def get_steps(day):
    return day["steps"]
day["steps"] looks up the value stored under the key "steps" — the readable alternative to remembering a position.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write distinct_moods(days) — return the set of distinct mood values across a list of day dicts. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

A set grows with .add(...). Each day’s mood is d["mood"], so add that to moods. Duplicates are dropped automatically.



Show solution

def distinct_moods(days):
    moods = set()
    for d in days:
        moods.add(d["mood"])
    return moods
moods.add(d["mood"]) puts each mood into the set; because a set keeps only unique items, the repeated "good" appears once.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Build count_moods(days) that returns a dict mapping each mood to how many days had it — a tally. Print it for a list you make up.
Rubric — a good answer:

Uses a dict as the tally and a loop over the days.
Handles a mood seen for the first time (start its count at 0 or use dict.get).
Returns the dict and runs on a list with at least one repeated mood.



Show one possible answer

def count_moods(days):
    tally = {}
    for d in days:
        mood = d["mood"]
        tally[mood] = tally.get(mood, 0) + 1
    return tally

print(count_moods([{"mood": "good"}, {"mood": "ok"}, {"mood": "good"}]))
# {'good': 2, 'ok': 1}
tally.get(mood, 0) returns the current count (or 0 the first time a mood is seen), and adding one updates it — a dict used as a running tally.

title: Data structures
text: Check yourself

title: Data structures
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now hold data in the right shape — records as dicts, collections as lists, unique values as sets. Next we transform these collections concisely with comprehensions, turning multi-line loops into a single readable expression.

title: Control flow
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Control flow
text:
The hook
In Chapter 1 you looked at a single day of habits data. But a real question — “how many days did I reach 8,000 steps?” — can’t be answered from one row. You have to go through every day and decide for each whether it counts.
That pairing is the whole of this chapter:

loops go through the data (for, while), and
conditions decide what to do for each item (if, elif, else).


You’ll need from before: types and comparisons (Ch1) — especially that a check like steps >= 8000 gives back a True/False.

title: Control flow
text:
Indentation is the syntax
Before the keywords, one rule that catches every newcomer: in Python, indentation decides what belongs to what. The indented lines under an if or a for are its block; when the indentation stops, the block ends.
if steps >= 8000:
    print("active")     # indented -> inside the if
print("done")           # not indented -> always runs






Note🧱 For Java developers





Where Java uses { } to group statements, Python uses indentation — there are no braces, and the indentation isn’t optional style, it’s the grammar. A few more shifts:

Conditions need no parentheses: if steps >= 8000: (note the colon), not if (steps >= 8000) {.
“else if” is spelled elif.
The data loop is a for-each — for day in days: — not a C-style for (int i = 0; i < n; i++). Stick to 4 spaces per level and never mix tabs and spaces.

title: Control flow
Control flow
text:
Repeating: for and while
A for loop walks every item in a collection — exactly what you want for rows of data:
for day in days:
    print(day)
A while loop repeats as long as its condition stays true — useful when you don’t know the count in advance:
while not done:
    ...      # keep going until something sets done = True
The real power comes from putting an if inside a for: walk the rows, and decide something for each.

title: Control flow
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
A handful of days from the habits data (steps already converted to int, as in Chapter 1). We’ll label each day and count the active ones. Press Run.






The accumulator pattern — start a counter at 0, add 1 inside the loop when a condition holds — is one you’ll reach for constantly.






The while keeps stepping forward until either the data runs out or a day falls below 7,000 — here it stops at the third day (5,400 steps), giving a streak of 2.

title: Control flow
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Indentation defines blocks in Python — no braces; be consistent (4 spaces).
if / elif / else take the first branch whose condition is true.
for walks every item in a collection; while repeats while a condition holds.
A for with an if inside — plus a counter — is the core pattern for counting or filtering rows of data.

title: Control flow
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the condition so label_day returns "active" for days that reach 8,000 steps and "low" otherwise. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

“Reaches 8,000” means at least 8,000: the comparison steps >= 8000 gives the True/False the if needs.



Show solution

def label_day(steps):
    if steps >= 8000:
        return "active"
    return "low"
When steps >= 8000 is true the function returns "active" straight away; otherwise it falls through to "low".



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write count_active(days, goal) — loop over the (date, steps) days and count how many reach goal. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

You’re using the accumulator pattern from the worked example: when a day is active, increase the counter by one with count += 1.



Show solution

def count_active(days, goal):
    count = 0
    for date, steps in days:
        if steps >= goal:
            count += 1
    return count
The loop visits every day; the if lets only the active ones increase count, which the function returns at the end.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Compute the longest run of consecutive active days (≥ 8,000 steps) in a list of (date, steps) days. Print your answer for a list you make up.
Rubric — a good answer:

Loops once through the days, tracking a current run and the best run seen.
Resets the current run to 0 on a non-active day.
Reports the longest run as a number, and the code runs.



Show one possible answer

days = [("a", 9000), ("b", 8200), ("c", 5000), ("d", 8100), ("e", 8800), ("f", 8800)]

best = run = 0
for date, steps in days:
    if steps >= 8000:
        run += 1
        best = max(best, run)
    else:
        run = 0
print("Longest active streak:", best)   # 3
Each active day extends the current run and may beat best; a low day resets run to 0. The longest streak here is the final three days.

text: Check yourself

title: Control flow
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now walk through data and make decisions row by row — the engine of almost every data task. So far we’ve repeated that logic inline; next we package it into functions you can name, reuse, and test, so the same step works on any day’s data.

title: A 10-minute history of AI
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: A 10-minute history of AI
text:
The hook
Generative AI feels like it arrived overnight — ChatGPT reached the public in late 2022 and the world changed its mind about computers in a year. But the first artificial neuron was described in 1943, before the transistor existed. AI is roughly eighty years old. Its story is not a straight climb; it is a series of bold promises, crushing disappointments (“AI winters”), and a handful of breakthroughs that changed everything.
Here is the ten-minute version.

You’ll need from before: the family tree from Chapter 4 — AI ⊃ ML ⊃ deep learning ⊃ generative AI. We’ll watch those rings appear, in order, over time.

title: A 10-minute history of AI
text:
The story, in five acts
Act 1 — The idea (1943–1958). In 1943, Warren McCulloch and Walter Pitts wrote down a simple mathematical model of a neuron. In 1950, Alan Turing asked “can machines think?” and proposed a test for it. In 1956, a summer workshop at Dartmouth College gave the field its name: artificial intelligence. Then, in 1958, Frank Rosenblatt built the Perceptron — a machine that learned to tell patterns apart from examples. Optimism ran high.
Act 2 — The first winter (1969–1980s). In 1969, Marvin Minsky and Seymour Papert proved that a single-layer perceptron could not learn even simple patterns (famously, the “exclusive-or”). The limitation was real, the disappointment sharp, and funding collapsed. This was the first AI winter — a recurring pattern where hype outruns results and the money leaves.
Act 3 — Learning returns (1986–2010). In 1986, a method called backpropagation (popularised by Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams in Nature) let networks with many layers learn — sidestepping the 1969 limitation. Neural networks revived; by the 1990s they could read handwritten digits. Meanwhile, a different, rule-and-search kind of AI hit a milestone: in 1997, IBM’s Deep Blue beat world chess champion Garry Kasparov. (Deep Blue searched moves by rules — it did not learn, a useful reminder that “AI” is broader than “machine learning.”)
Act 4 — The deep-learning boom (2012–2017). In 2012, a deep network called AlexNet (Krizhevsky, Sutskever, and Hinton) won the ImageNet image-recognition contest by a wide margin. Three things had finally come together: big datasets, fast GPUs, and deeper networks. Deep learning went from fringe to mainstream almost overnight. In 2016, DeepMind’s AlphaGo beat Go champion Lee Sedol — a game long thought too intuitive for computers.
Act 5 — Transformers and generative AI (2017–today). In 2017, eight researchers at Google published “Attention Is All You Need,” introducing the transformer — the architecture behind nearly every modern language model. Scaled up on enormous text, transformers became foundation models: trained once, then adapted to countless tasks. GPT-3 (2020) showed how capable they were; ChatGPT (2022) put one in everyone’s hands. The current frontier extends this with models trained to generate images and audio, and reasoning models trained to work through problems step by step.

🔬 Going deeper (optional): notice the rhythm — an idea, a wave of hype, a winter, then a breakthrough that makes the old promise real. Today’s excitement is the warmest summer yet, but the pattern is worth remembering.

title: A 10-minute history of AI
section: Let’s build it (worked example): the timeline as data
text:
Let’s build it (worked example): the timeline as data
History is easier to reason about as data. We’ll put the milestones in a list and ask questions of it. Press Run.












Seventy-nine years. The “overnight” success was almost a century in the making.

title: A 10-minute history of AI
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

AI is about eighty years old — the artificial neuron dates to 1943.
Progress came in booms and winters: hype, disappointment, then a breakthrough that delivered on the old promise.
The 2012 deep-learning boom (AlexNet) and the 2017 transformer are the two milestones that most directly enabled today’s generative AI.
“AI” is broader than “machine learning”: Deep Blue (1997) was AI that searched by rules, with no learning at all.

title: A 10-minute history of AI
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. We’ll reuse the timeline data from the worked example.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the marked line so event_year returns the year a named event happened (or None if it isn’t in the timeline). Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The loop has already unpacked each entry into year and event. When event matches, which of those two should the function return?



Show solution

timeline = [
    (1958, "Rosenblatt's Perceptron"),
    (2012, "AlexNet wins ImageNet"),
    (2017, "The transformer"),
]

def event_year(timeline, name):
    for year, event in timeline:
        if event == name:
            return year
    return None
When the event matches, return its year; if the loop finishes with no match, the event isn’t in the timeline, so return None.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write events_in_era(timeline, start, end) — return the list of event names whose year is between start and end, inclusive. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

“Between start and end, inclusive” is two comparisons joined with and: the year is at least start and at most end. Python lets you chain them: start <= year <= end.



Show solution

timeline = [
    (1958, "Perceptron"),
    (1986, "Backpropagation"),
    (2012, "AlexNet"),
    (2017, "Transformer"),
    (2022, "ChatGPT"),
]

def events_in_era(timeline, start, end):
    names = []
    for year, event in timeline:
        if start <= year <= end:
            names.append(event)
    return names
start <= year <= end is True only for years inside the era, so only those event names are collected — in the order they appear in the timeline.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Pick one milestone from this chapter and argue, in three sentences, why it mattered — what became possible after it that was not possible before.
Rubric — a good answer:

Names a real milestone and its (correct) year from the chapter.
States clearly what it unlocked (e.g. the transformer → scalable language models).
Connects it to something you’ve seen, used, or read about since.



Show one possible answer

“The 2017 transformer mattered because it let networks pay ‘attention’ to all parts of a text at once, which made training on enormous datasets practical. Before it, language models struggled to keep track of long context. Every chatbot I’ve used since — including the sentiment demo in Chapter 4 — descends from that one idea.”

title: A 10-minute history of AI
text: Check yourself

title: A 10-minute history of AI
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You’ve placed AI in time: an eighty-year arc of booms, winters, and breakthroughs, arriving at the generative models of today. That’s the end of the “what and why.” The final chapter of this module is the “how” of this school — how to move through the tiers, exercises, quizzes, and capstones, and earn your certificate.

title: What is AI, really?
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: What is AI, really?
text:
The hook
Open your phone’s photo app and search “dog.” It finds every dog photo you’ve ever taken — including dogs it has never seen, in your friend’s pictures. Nobody sat down and wrote instructions like “if the image has fur and four legs and a wet nose, it’s a dog.” That rule would be hopeless: it would flag cats, miss dogs in costumes, and break on a photo taken in the dark.
Instead, the app was shown hundreds of thousands of labelled photos — this is a dog, this is not — and it worked out the pattern on its own. That is the whole idea behind modern AI, and it is exactly what you did in Chapter 1: you showed a program labelled flowers, and it learned to name the species.

You’ll need from before: Chapter 1’s loop (load → train → predict → measure) and the word model — a function learned from examples.

title: What is AI, really?
text:
AI, in plain language
Artificial intelligence is getting computers to do tasks that normally need human intelligence — recognising a face, understanding a sentence, deciding which move to play, predicting tomorrow’s demand, writing a paragraph.
For decades, the only way to make a computer do anything was to write the rules by hand: spell out every step, every if-this-then-that. That works for taxes and traffic lights. It falls apart for “is this a dog?”, because nobody can write down, in code, every rule for what a dog looks like.
The breakthrough was to stop writing the rules and start learning them:

Traditional programming: you write the rules; the computer follows them.
Machine learning: you show examples; the computer works out the rules.

Machine learning (ML) is the engine under almost everything called “AI” today. A model is the learned rule. Training is the act of learning it from labelled examples. You met all three words in Chapter 1 — here they are again, named.

Learned behaviour, not a hand-written rule
The important phrase is learned behaviour. The iris model’s rule for telling species apart was never typed by a person; it was shaped by the data. Change the data and you change the behaviour. This is powerful — and it is the source of AI’s biggest weakness.

⚠️ Watch out: a model is only as good as its examples. If the training photos were all of golden retrievers in daylight, the app will struggle with a black dog at night. Worse, a model can be confidently wrong — it outputs an answer with no sense of “I’m unsure.” Treat its predictions as informed guesses, not facts.



Everyday AI you already use
Each of these learned its behaviour from data, not from hand-written rules:

Autocomplete finishing your sentence — learned from mountains of text.
Recommendations (“you might also like…”) — learned from what people watched.
Spam filters — learned from messages people marked as spam.
Voice assistants turning speech into text — learned from recorded speech.

We’ll sort out how these relate — AI vs ML vs deep learning vs generative AI — in the next chapter. First, let’s watch a program learn a rule from data.

title: What is AI, really?
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Here is the whole idea in a few lines: a spam filter that learns which words signal spam, instead of us writing them down. Press Run.












The program was never told that “free” means spam. It noticed it, by counting across the labelled examples. Now we use what it learned on a message it has never seen:






That is machine learning in miniature: behaviour (which messages are spam) learned from examples, then applied to new data. A real spam filter does the same thing with millions of messages and far cleverer counting — but the shape is identical.

title: What is AI, really?
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

AI is getting computers to do tasks that normally need human intelligence.
Its modern engine is machine learning: learn the rule from labelled examples instead of writing it by hand.
A model’s behaviour is learned from data — so it is only as good as that data, and it can be confidently wrong.
You have now seen it twice: the iris model and the spam demo both learned a rule no one typed out.

title: What is AI, really?
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the marked line so count_with_word counts how many messages contain a given word. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

You want the messages that contain the word. Python’s in checks membership: "free" in "free lunch" is True.



Show solution

def count_with_word(messages, word):
    return sum(1 for m in messages if word in m)
word in m is True when the message contains the word; summing those Trues counts the matches.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write classify(message, spam_words) — return "spam" if the message contains any spam word, otherwise "ham". Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The loop only reaches its body when a spam word is in the message. So at that point, what label should the function hand back?



Show solution

def classify(message, spam_words):
    for word in spam_words:
        if word in message:
            return "spam"
    return "ham"
If any spam word is found, return "spam" immediately; if the loop finishes without a match, the message is "ham".



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Find the weakness in our keyword approach: write a message that is clearly spam to a human but that classify would label "ham" (it uses none of the spam words). Then explain, in two sentences, how learning from more labelled examples would help catch it.
Rubric — a good answer:

Gives a genuinely spammy message that the keyword list misses.
Explains that more (and more varied) labelled spam would teach the model new signals — words, phrasing, patterns — beyond a short fixed list.
Notes that no fixed keyword list can keep up; learning from data is what scales.



Show one possible answer

print(classify("urgent: verify your account to avoid suspension", ["free", "win"]))
# -> 'ham', even though a human reads it as spam
“This message uses none of our two keywords, so the classifier misses it. If we showed the model thousands of labelled messages, it would learn that words like urgent, verify, and suspension also signal spam — far more than we could ever list by hand, which is exactly why learning from data beats fixed rules.”

title: What is AI, really?
text: Check yourself

title: What is AI, really?
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now say what AI is: computers doing intelligence-like tasks by learning rules from examples. But “AI” is a big word that hides several nested ideas — machine learning, deep learning, and the generative AI behind today’s chatbots. Next we draw the family tree, with one concrete example for each branch.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
The hook
You are about to train a real machine-learning model — in this browser tab, with nothing installed. No account, no download, no setup. Scroll to the first code block, press Run, and in a few seconds a model will sort flowers it has never seen before, and tell you how often it gets them right.
The flowers are irises. Picture three species laid out on a table. For each flower someone measured four things: the length and width of its petals and of its sepals (the green leaf-like parts under the petals). Given those four numbers, can a computer learn to name the species? That is the whole task — and by the end of this page you will have built the thing that does it.

By the end of this chapter you’ll be able to train a classifier, call it on new data, and read its accuracy.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
The idea, in plain language
Start with the word that does the heavy lifting: model.
A model is a function that you learn from examples instead of writing by hand. You do not sit down and code the rule “if the petals are long and narrow, it’s species B.” Instead you show the computer many flowers whose species you already know, and it works out the rule for you.
Three words describe the whole cycle:

Training (also called fitting): you hand the model a pile of examples where the answer is known — here, flowers labelled with their species — and it adjusts itself until its guesses match those known answers reasonably well.
Predicting: you give the trained model a new flower it has not seen, and it outputs a species.
Accuracy: you check how often it is right. If you test it on 40 flowers and it names 38 correctly, that is 38/40 = 0.95, or 95% accuracy.

One habit matters from day one: you test on flowers the model did not train on. A model can score well on examples it has already memorised and still fail on new ones, the way reciting answers you’ve seen is not the same as understanding. So we split the data into a training set (to learn from) and a test set (to grade on).
That is the entire plan. The code below carries it out in five short steps.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
These blocks run Python in your browser through Pyodide. The first run downloads the runtime, which takes a few seconds; after that it is instant. Press Run on each block in order.






X holds the four measurements for every flower; y holds the species, written as a number (0, 1, or 2) because models work with numbers.






We keep a quarter of the flowers aside as the test set. random_state=0 makes the split the same every time, so your numbers match this page.






LogisticRegression is a small, well-understood classifier — a good first model. fit is the training step. max_iter=200 gives it enough rounds to settle.












That percentage is the share of test flowers the model named correctly. You built a model, trained it, used it on new data, and graded it — the full loop.

🔬 Going deeper (optional): try changing test_size to 0.5 and re-running from Step 2. With fewer training flowers, does the accuracy move? That tension — more data to learn from versus more data to grade on — comes back often.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A model is a function learned from labelled examples, not a rule you write by hand.
Training (fitting) adjusts the model on known answers; predicting runs it on new inputs.
Accuracy is the fraction of predictions that match the truth — always measured on data the model did not train on.
You did all of this in the browser, at zero cost and zero install.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
Practice
Each exercise below is auto-graded by run_tests, the shared Larnix grader. It is loaded for you from lib/grader.py — you do not paste or define it.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the one marked line so the model is trained. Replace ____ with the call that fits model on the training data, then press Run.









Show solution

model.fit(X_train, y_train)
fit is the training step: it takes the inputs X_train and their known answers y_train. After it runs, model.predict works on new data.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write accuracy(y_true, y_pred) — the fraction of predictions that match the truth, as a number between 0 and 1. Complete the marked line.









Show solution

def accuracy(y_true, y_pred):
    correct = sum(1 for a, b in zip(y_true, y_pred) if a == b)
    return correct / len(y_true)
a == b is True when a prediction matches the truth; summing those Trues counts the matches, and dividing by the number of labels gives the fraction. This is what accuracy_score computed for you in Step 5.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈15 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Starting from the worked example, try to beat 95% accuracy on the test set. Ideas: raise max_iter, or swap LogisticRegression for another classifier such as from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier.
Rubric — a good solution:

Trains on X_train/y_train only and reports accuracy on the held-out X_test/y_test (no testing on training data).
States the change you made and the accuracy you reached.
Reaches at least the worked example’s baseline, ideally higher.



Show one possible solution

from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
print(f"k-NN accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, knn.predict(X_test)):.2%}")
A nearest-neighbour classifier often does well on Iris. Other approaches are fine too — the point is to change one thing, measure honestly on the test set, and compare.

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text: Check yourself

title: Train your first model — in your browser
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You have run the complete machine-learning loop — load, split, train, predict, measure — on a classic dataset, without installing anything. Every later module reuses this loop on bigger data and stronger models. Next, we look at what the accuracy number does not tell you, and when it can mislead.

title: About Larnix
text:
Artificial intelligence is reshaping how we work, learn, and create — yet for most people it stays a black box, gated behind expensive courses, pricey hardware, and walls of jargon. We think that is backwards. Understanding the technology shaping your future should not depend on your budget or your laptop.
So Larnix is free, open, and built on one belief: the fastest way to understand something is to build it. Not to watch someone else build it — to build it yourself, line by line, until it clicks.




Build first, theory as you go. Every chapter has you run real code and see a real result, then explains why it works. You learn by doing, the way you actually remember.
One idea at a time. Short, single-topic chapters in plain language. New terms are introduced in everyday words before the technical name appears.
It runs where you are. Beginner chapters execute Python right in your browser, with nothing to install. The GPU-heavy modules run on free cloud tiers, and any lesson that touches a paid service shows a free alternative.
Zero to frontier. Seventeen modules take you from “what is AI” through classical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and large language models — ending with you pretraining your own small LLM.




The goal is not a certificate you forget. It is the genuine ability to read, build, and reason about modern AI systems — to train and evaluate models honestly, to explain how a transformer works because you wrote one, and to ship a project you are proud to show. In short: from curious beginner to someone who can build.



Larnix is being built in the open, and it is early. Right now the platform is complete and proven end to end on the first full chapter — Train your first model, in your browser — and new modules are being authored from there. You can start that chapter today, for free; follow the roadmap to see what is coming next.
If that mission speaks to you, the best first step is the smallest one: train your first model.

title: Learn how AI works — by building it.
text:
✦ Free forever · runs in your browser · no signup
Learn how AI works — by building it.

Most courses explain AI. Larnix has you build it. Train your first model in the next twenty minutes — right here in this browser tab — and keep going, one idea at a time, until you’ve built your own language model. No setup, no fees, no maths-degree required.


Train your first model ▸ See the full journey


The journey: zero to frontier

Seventeen modules carry you from “what is AI” to building and shipping real systems. Each chapter is short, plain-spoken, and runnable. Begin at M0 today — the rest unlock as the curriculum grows.




M0
Orientation

Train a real model in twenty minutes: load data, fit a classifier, and watch it sort flowers it has never seen. Your first taste of the whole loop. 🟢 Beginner ▸ Available



M1
Python for AI

Enough Python to handle data — variables, functions, files, clean code — built from zero, then a data-cleaning capstone on a deliberately messy dataset. 🟢 Beginner ▸ Available



M2
Math You Actually Need

The little math AI uses — vectors, derivatives, probability — pictures before symbols, building to the math of one neuron, gradient-checked by hand. 🟡 Intermediate ▸ Available



M3
Data & Tooling

Load, clean, explore, and plot real data with NumPy, Pandas, and Matplotlib — then run a full exploratory analysis on a dataset you have never seen. 🟡 Intermediate ▸ Available



M4
Classical ML

The workhorses behind real products — regression, trees, ensembles — and the honest question every practitioner asks: did it actually learn? 🟡 Intermediate Soon



M7
Transformers & LLMs

Ever wondered what is inside a chatbot like ChatGPT? Build the attention mechanism and the transformer yourself, step by step — nothing hand-waved. 🔴 Advanced Soon



M11
Build your own LLM

The headline capstone: pretrain a small language model end to end, on a free GPU. The thing experts do — you will do it too. 🔴 Advanced Soon



M16
Career-ready

Turn what you built into a career — portfolio projects, production MLOps, and interview practice that hiring managers respect. 🟡 Intermediate Soon



Your first model is twenty minutes away.
It runs in this browser tab, costs nothing, and needs no account. Curious what you can build? There is only one way to find out.
Start Chapter 1 ▸

title: What you need & what it costs
text:
The hook
You trained a machine-learning model in Chapter 1. Here is the bill for it:
₹0.
No card, no trial, no “first month free.” Learning to drive does not start with buying a car — you borrow one for the lesson. Larnix is built the same way: the expensive machinery is borrowed, for free, right when you need it, and most of the time you need nothing but the browser tab you are already in.
This chapter is the honest receipt. By the end you will know exactly what you need, what each tool costs, and where the one optional spend in the whole course shows up — so money is never the reason you stop.

You’ll need from before: nothing but Chapter 1’s “press Run and it works” experience. We build straight on that.
crumbs: Ch2 — What you need & what it costs

title: What you need & what it costs
text:
What you actually need
For the part of the course you are in now — Modules 0 through 10 — the full list of requirements is:

A web browser (the one you’re reading this in).
An internet connection to load each page.

That’s it. The code runs inside the browser through a technology called Pyodide, which is Python compiled to run on the web. There is nothing to install, no account to create, and no payment anywhere.
Two free tools appear later, and only when a lesson actually needs them:

A free Google account, so you can open the heavier Module 5+ notebooks on Google Colab or Kaggle — both give you a real GPU at no cost.
Ollama, a free program that runs language models on your own computer, used in the later language-model modules. Also free.

You do not need either of these today. When the time comes, the chapter that uses them walks you through the free setup.

The three compute tiers
Every chapter declares where its code runs. There are exactly three tiers, and you can read which one you’re in from the badge at the top of each chapter:









Tier
What runs it
Cost
First appears




browser
Your browser (Pyodide)
₹0
Module 0 (here)


colab
A free Colab/Kaggle GPU
₹0 (free tier)
Module 5


gpu
A rented GPU, billed by the second
a few hundred ₹ (optional)
Module 11 only



Modules 0–10 use only the first two tiers, so they are ₹0 end to end. The third tier appears exactly once.


The one optional spend, named honestly
The Module 11 capstone trains a small language model from scratch. Doing that at full size is the only place where renting a GPU — a few hundred rupees for a short session — can help. It is optional: the same capstone ships a smaller-model path that runs on a free GPU, so you can complete the course for ₹0 even there.

⚠️ Watch out: when a later lesson shows a paid API (say, a hosted model), it is always optional and always paired with a free fallback — a local Ollama model or a free tier. If a chapter ever seems to demand a key to continue, that is a bug; the free path is the real path.

title: What you need & what it costs
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
We’ll compute the bill ourselves. The cells below run in your browser — pressing Run on them costs, fittingly, ₹0.






The runtime says Emscripten / wasm32: that is Python running inside your browser as WebAssembly. No server did this work; your own tab did.












That last number is a what-if, not a promise: you choose the rate and the hours. The exercises below let you compute your own.

title: What you need & what it costs
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Modules 0–10 cost ₹0 — a browser is the entire setup; there is nothing to install or pay for.
When a GPU is needed (Module 5 onward), it comes from a free tier (Colab or Kaggle).
Any paid API in a lesson is optional and always paired with a free fallback — keys are never required to learn.
The only optional spend is the Module 11 capstone (a few hundred rupees, approximate), and even it has a free smaller-model path.

title: What you need & what it costs
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the marked line so total_cost adds up the cost of every tool. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Each item is a pair (name, cost). The loop already unpacks it into name and cost — you need only the value being summed.



Show solution

def total_cost(tools):
    return sum(cost for name, cost in tools)
cost is the number in each (name, cost) pair; sum(...) adds them. A toolkit of all zeros totals 0.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write gpu_estimate(hours, rate_per_hour) — the cost of renting a GPU is the time times the rate. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Cost by time means one multiplication: how many hours, times the price per hour.



Show solution

def gpu_estimate(hours, rate_per_hour):
    return hours * rate_per_hour
Renting is billed by time, so the cost is hours * rate_per_hour. Zero hours costs nothing; fractional hours are fine because it’s plain multiplication.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Plan your own Module 11 budget. Pick a realistic hourly GPU rate (look one up), choose how many hours you’d spend, and use gpu_estimate to compute the cost. Then write two sentences: what it would cost you, and how you’d do the capstone for ₹0 instead.
Rubric — a good answer:

Uses gpu_estimate with a rate you state and reports the number.
Names the ₹0 alternative (the free smaller-model path on a free GPU).
Is honest that the rate is an estimate that changes over time.



Show one possible answer

my_rate = 35   # ₹/hour — an example; check a current provider's rate
my_hours = 4
print("My M11 estimate:", gpu_estimate(my_hours, my_rate), "₹")
“At ₹35/hour for 4 hours, a full-size run would cost me about ₹140. I could instead train the smaller model on a free Colab/Kaggle GPU and finish the capstone for ₹0 — the rented GPU only buys speed and scale, not the learning.”

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
text: Beginner Browser Stable
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
text:
The hook
People say “AI,” “machine learning,” “deep learning,” and “generative AI” as if they were the same thing. They are not — but they are not rivals either. They nest, like a set of Russian dolls. The chatbot you may have used is a piece of generative AI, which is a kind of deep learning, which is a kind of machine learning, which is a kind of AI:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ AI — machines doing intelligence-like tasks  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ ML — learning the rules from examples   │  │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐  │  │
│  │  │ DL — many-layered neural networks │  │  │
│  │  │  ┌────────────────────────────┐  │  │  │
│  │  │  │ GenAI — creates new content │  │  │  │
│  │  │  └────────────────────────────┘  │  │  │
│  │  └──────────────────────────────────┘  │  │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

You’ll need from before: the idea of a model (Ch1) and learned behaviour — rules learned from examples instead of written by hand (Ch3).
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
section: The four rings, with one example each
text:
The four rings, with one example each
AI is the outermost ring: any technique that makes a computer do something we’d call intelligent. That includes machine learning — but also older, rule-based systems. Example: a chess program that searches moves using rules a person wrote. It is AI, but nothing about it is learned.
Machine learning (ML) sits inside AI: instead of writing the rules, you learn them from examples. Example: the iris classifier (Ch1) and the spam filter (Ch3). Both found their rules in data. Most are not neural networks at all.
Deep learning (DL) sits inside ML: machine learning that uses neural networks with many layers. Its trick is learning its own features straight from raw data — pixels, audio samples, characters — so you don’t hand-engineer them. Example: the photo app that recognises a dog, or speech turned into text.
Generative AI (GenAI) sits inside DL: deep-learning models built to create new content rather than label existing content. Example: a chatbot writing a paragraph, or a model painting an image from a description.

Classify vs generate — the key split
The deepest two rings differ in what they produce:

A classifier (discriminative) picks a label from a fixed set: spam/ham, dog/cat, which of three iris species.
A generator produces something new that wasn’t in the options: a fresh sentence, an unseen image, a snippet of audio.

Let’s make “generate” concrete with a toy you can read in full.
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
section: Let’s build it (worked example): a baby generator
text:
Let’s build it (worked example): a baby generator
A real text generator uses an enormous neural network. The idea, though, fits in a few lines: learn which word tends to follow which, then walk that chain to produce a new sentence. Press Run.












That sentence was created, not chosen from a list — it may even be one that was never in the corpus. That is the essence of “generative.” Swap the toy chain for a deep neural network trained on much of the internet, and you have a modern language model. Same shape, vastly more scale.
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
section: Feel it: real generative-adjacent AI, in your browser
text:
Feel it: real generative-adjacent AI, in your browser
The toy above is readable but tiny. Here is a real neural network — the kind behind deep learning — running entirely in your browser, no Python and no server. Type a sentence and it judges the sentiment. The first click downloads a small model (it needs the internet once); after that it runs offline.

  Sentiment demo — a deep-learning model judging your text. This is a taste, not a graded exercise.
  Your sentence
  This course is free and I learned a lot.
  Analyse sentiment
  
  Try to fool it — a tricky sentence can make it confidently wrong, exactly as Chapter 3 warned.




🔬 Going deeper (optional): the model ran on your device. Nothing you typed was sent to a server — a real example of the ₹0, privacy-friendly path this course prefers.
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

AI ⊃ ML ⊃ DL ⊃ GenAI — each is a special case of the one outside it.
ML learns rules from examples; DL is ML with many-layered neural networks that learn their own features from raw data.
GenAI is deep learning that creates new content, rather than labelling existing content.
A classifier picks from fixed options; a generator makes something new.
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. We’ll model the family tree as a dictionary that maps each ring to the one outside it.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the marked line so parent returns the ring directly outside a given one (or None for the top). Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

A dictionary’s .get(key) returns the value, or None when the key is missing — exactly the behaviour you need for the outermost ring.



Show solution

parent_of = {"ML": "AI", "DL": "ML", "GenAI": "DL"}

def parent(node):
    return parent_of.get(node)
.get returns the parent if it exists and None otherwise, so parent("AI") is None without a special case.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write is_kind_of(node, ancestor) — True if ancestor is node itself or any ring outside it. This is the “is GenAI a kind of AI?” question. Complete the line that steps outward.









If you’re stuck

Each turn of the loop should move one ring outward. You already wrote the function that finds the ring outside a node in Exercise 1 — use the same parent_of.get.



Show solution

parent_of = {"ML": "AI", "DL": "ML", "GenAI": "DL"}

def is_kind_of(node, ancestor):
    while node is not None:
        if node == ancestor:
            return True
        node = parent_of.get(node)
    return False
The loop walks outward — GenAI → DL → ML → AI — returning True if it ever lands on ancestor, and stopping at None (past the outermost ring) otherwise.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Pick a real AI system you’ve used — a chatbot, a photo search, a recommendation feed, a voice assistant — and place it precisely in the tree. Name the innermost ring it belongs to, and justify each level outward.
Rubric — a good answer:

Names the most specific ring correctly (e.g. a chatbot → GenAI).
Justifies each outer ring (it’s GenAI, so it’s DL, so it’s ML, so it’s AI).
Distinguishes whether the system mainly classifies or generates.



Show one possible answer

“A chatbot is GenAI: it generates new sentences. That makes it deep learning (a large neural network), which makes it machine learning (it learned from huge amounts of text, not hand-written rules), which makes it AI (it does a task we’d call intelligent). It mainly generates rather than classifies.”
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
text: Check yourself
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI — a family tree
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now place any “AI” you meet in its ring, and tell a classifier from a generator. These ideas didn’t arrive all at once — they piled up over seventy years of false starts and breakthroughs. Next, a ten-minute history: how we got from a single artificial neuron to today’s generative models.
crumbs: Ch4 — AI vs ML vs DL vs GenAI

title: How this school works
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: How this school works
text:
The hook
Without being told, you have already used almost every feature of this school. In the last five chapters you ran real code, were marked by an auto-grader, took a quiz, and met “review cards.” This short chapter names those parts, so from here on you can use them on purpose — and shows you how to track your way to a certificate.

You’ll need from before: nothing new. This is a map of the building you’re already standing in.

title: How this school works
text:
The parts of the school

Difficulty tiers
Every chapter wears a badge for its level:

🟢 Beginner — assumes no prior machine learning; gentle pace.
🟡 Intermediate — builds on earlier modules; introduces real tooling.
🔴 Advanced — assumes solid fundamentals; production or frontier depth.

All of M0 is 🟢. Later modules climb. You can always read the badge before you commit your time.


The shape of every chapter
You’ve now seen the same rhythm five times — that’s deliberate (it’s called the Varsity contract):

a hook (an analogy or a working demo first),
a plain-language explanation of one idea,
at least one runnable example you can press Run on,
a Key Takeaways box, and
a few graded exercises with hidden solutions.

Knowing the shape means you always know where you are in a chapter.


Code that runs, and graders that check it
Every code block runs in your browser, at ₹0 (Chapter 2). The exercises are checked by an auto-grader — the run_tests you’ve been using — which gives instant pass/fail feedback. Each exercise hides a solution walkthrough you should open only after a genuine try.


Quizzes and capstones
Your understanding is checked three ways, in increasing size:

Exercises — small, inside each chapter, auto-graded.
Quizzes — a 2–3 question quick check per chapter, plus a longer module quiz at the end of each module.
Capstones — one build-something project per module, graded by a rubric. Every capstone must report a number (“did you measure it?”). A result you did not measure cannot score well, however polished the write-up.



Spaced review, tracks, and the certificate
Each chapter seeds a few review cards — short question/answer prompts drawn from its Key Takeaways — so you can come back and beat the forgetting curve. As you finish modules, you progress along a learning track; completing a track’s quizzes and capstones is what earns a certificate. (The review scheduler and certificate are part of how the school is designed to grow; the cards are being seeded for every chapter as it ships.)

title: How this school works
section: Let’s build it (worked example): track your progress
text:
Let’s build it (worked example): track your progress
Let’s make “progress” concrete. We’ll list the foundation modules with a flag for whether you’ve finished each, and compute how far along you are. Press Run.












One module down. The same idea — a little data plus a little code — is how a progress page or a certificate check works under the hood.

title: How this school works
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Read the tier badge (🟢/🟡/🔴) to know a chapter’s level before you start.
Every chapter follows the same shape: hook → explanation → runnable example → Key Takeaways → graded exercises.
Your understanding is checked at three sizes: exercises → quizzes → capstones, and every capstone must report a number.
Review cards help you remember, and finishing modules moves you along a track toward a certificate.

title: How this school works
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the marked line so percent_complete returns the fraction of modules finished, as a number from 0 to 1. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

A fraction is a part divided by a whole: the number done divided by the total number of modules, which is len(modules).



Show solution

def percent_complete(modules):
    done = sum(1 for name, completed in modules if completed)
    return done / len(modules)
Dividing the completed count by the total number of modules gives a fraction from 0.0 (none done) to 1.0 (all done).



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write next_module(modules) — return the name of the first module that isn’t finished, or None if every module is done. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The loop stops at the first module where completed is False. At that point, which of the two unpacked values is the module’s name?



Show solution

def next_module(modules):
    for name, completed in modules:
        if not completed:
            return name
    return None
Return the name of the first unfinished module; if the loop never finds one, every module is done, so return None.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Write your own learning plan, in three or four sentences: which track or modules you’ll do first, roughly when, and why — what you want to be able to build by the end.
Rubric — a good plan:

Names specific modules or a track (not “everything”).
States a rough schedule you could actually keep.
Connects to a concrete goal (a project, a job, a skill) you care about.



Show one possible plan

plan = [
    ("M0 — Orientation", "this week"),
    ("M1 — Python for AI", "next two weeks"),
    ("M3 — Data & Tooling", "the month after"),
]
for module, when in plan:
    print(f"{when}: {module}")
“I’ll finish M0 this week, then spend two weeks on M1 so I can clean data with my own functions, and follow with M3 to run a real EDA — because my goal is to analyse my city’s open transport data and publish one honest chart.”
