title: How this school works
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
That’s the whole of Module 0: you’ve run a model, confirmed the ₹0 path, learned what AI is and how its pieces nest, placed it in history, and mapped how to learn here. Next comes the module quiz and the M0 capstone — your first build-something — after which Module 1 begins teaching the Python you’ll use for everything that follows.

title: Variables & types
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Variables & types
text:
The hook
Here is one row from the habits file we’ll clean and study across this whole module:
date        steps   sleep_hours   mood
2026-01-01  7500    7.5           good
Four values, and not one of them is the same kind of thing: 2026-01-01 is text, 7500 is a whole number, 7.5 has a decimal point, and good is a label. Before you can clean, count, or chart any of this, you need to know what kinds of values Python holds — and how to turn one kind into another. That is this chapter.

You’ll need from before: only the experience of pressing Run on a code cell in Module 0.

title: Variables & types
text:
Variables: names for values
A variable is a name bound to a value. You create one with =:
steps = 7500
That’s it — no type to declare, no semicolon. Python looks at the value 7500 and works out that steps is a whole number. This is called dynamic typing: the type follows the value, and the same name can later hold something else entirely.






Note🧱 For Java developers





In Java you’d write int steps = 7500; — you declare the type up front, end with a semicolon, and the compiler stops you if you later assign a String to steps. Python does none of that:

No type declaration — steps = 7500 is enough.
No semicolons, no braces; indentation defines blocks (you’ll see that next chapter).
A name can be rebound to a different type: steps = 7500 then steps = "lots" is legal. The cost is that type mistakes surface when the code runs, not when it compiles — so the habit of checking types (below) earns its keep.

title: Variables & types
text:
The four core types
Most data you’ll meet is one of four basic types:

int — a whole number: 7500, 0, -3.
float — a number with a decimal point: 7.5, 3.0, -0.25.
str — text (“string”), written in quotes: "good", "2026-01-01".
bool — a truth value: True or False.

You can always ask Python a value’s type with type(...). Arithmetic works as you’d expect, and comparisons produce booleans:
type(7500)        # <class 'int'>
7500 / 2          # 3750.0  (dividing with / always gives a float)
7500 >= 8000      # False   (a comparison yields a bool)
Strings have their own toolkit you’ll lean on when cleaning text — for example "  Good  ".strip().lower() turns a padded, capitalised label into the tidy "good". More on that when we reach the messy mood column.

title: Variables & types
section: Data from files arrives as text
text:
Data from files arrives as text
This is the single most useful fact in the chapter. When you read a CSV (Chapter 6), every value comes in as a string — even 7500. So "7500" + "100" would glue text into "7500100", not add to 7600. To do maths, you first convert:

int("7500") → 7500
float("7.5") → 7.5
str(7500) → "7500"

Getting types right is step zero of working with data.

title: Variables & types
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s take that habits row exactly as a file would hand it to us — all strings — and give each value its proper type. Press Run.






Notice the payoff: once steps is an int, steps / 2 and steps >= 8000 work. Left as the string "7500", neither would.

title: Variables & types
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A variable is a name bound to a value; Python infers its type from the value (dynamic typing) — no declarations.
The four core types are int, float, str, bool; check any value with type(x).
Data read from files arrives as strings — convert with int() or float() before doing arithmetic.
Comparisons (>=, ==, …) produce booleans (True/False).

title: Variables & types
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the marked line so as_int turns a string of digits into a whole number. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

There’s a built-in that turns a string into a whole number; you used it in the worked example: int(...).



Show solution

def as_int(text):
    return int(text)
int("7500") reads the digits and returns the whole number 7500, whose type is int.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write met_goal(steps_text, goal) — convert the string steps_text to a number and return whether it reaches the goal (a bool). Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

“Reaches the goal” means at least the goal: steps >= goal. A comparison like that already gives back True or False.



Show solution

def met_goal(steps_text, goal):
    steps = int(steps_text)
    return steps >= goal
After converting the text to an int, the comparison steps >= goal evaluates to the boolean the function returns.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). In the real habits file, one steps value is written "7,500" — with a comma. Try int("7,500") and read the error. Then describe, in two sentences, what goes wrong and one way you could fix the string before converting it.
Rubric — a good answer:

Correctly states that int("7,500") raises a ValueError (the comma isn’t a digit).
Proposes removing the comma first, e.g. "7,500".replace(",", "") → "7500".
Connects this to why data needs cleaning before analysis.



Show one possible answer

cleaned = "7,500".replace(",", "")   # "7500"
print(int(cleaned))                  # 7500
“int(\"7,500\") raises a ValueError because a comma is not a digit, so Python can’t read the whole thing as a number. Removing the comma first with .replace(\",\", \"\") gives \"7500\", which converts cleanly — a tiny example of the cleaning every real dataset needs.”

title: Variables & types
text: Check yourself

title: Variables & types
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now name values, tell their types apart, and convert text into numbers — the groundwork for everything we do with data. Next we use these values to make decisions: if, for, and while, driven by the rows of the habits file.

title: Functions
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Functions
text:
The hook
In Chapter 2 you wrote the “is this day active?” check right inside the loop. But you’ll want that exact check again — for another month of data, in a chart, in your capstone. Copying the same lines around is how a small bug ends up in five places.
A function fixes this: write the logic once, give it a name, and call it wherever you need it. You’ve already used functions — int(), len(), run_tests() — so the idea is familiar. This chapter you write your own.

You’ll need from before: loops and if/elif/else (Ch2), and comparisons returning True/False (Ch1).

title: Functions
text:
Defining a function
A function is a named block with inputs (parameters) and an output (whatever you return):
def label_day(steps):
    """Label a day by its step count."""
    if steps >= 8000:
        return "active"
    elif steps >= 6000:
        return "ok"
    return "low"

def starts the definition; label_day is the name; steps is a parameter.
The line in quotes is a docstring — a one-sentence description of what the function does.
return hands a value back to whoever called the function. The moment a return runs, the function stops.

You call it by name, passing an argument:
label_day(8200)   # -> "active"

⚠️ Watch out: a function with no return gives back the special value None. If a function “does nothing,” check that you actually returned the result.







Note🧱 For Java developers





def label_day(steps): replaces String labelDay(int steps) { … } — no return type, no parameter types, no braces (indentation again). A bare return or no return yields None, Python’s null-like value. Functions are first-class: you can store one in a variable or pass it to another function, which you’ll use later with tools like map and sort.

title: Functions
text:
Default argument values
A parameter can have a default — a value used when the caller doesn’t give one:
def met_goal(steps, goal=8000):
    """Did the day reach the goal? Goal defaults to 8000."""
    return steps >= goal

met_goal(8200)        # uses the default goal of 8000 -> True
met_goal(5000, 4000)  # overrides the goal -> True
Defaults keep the common case short while still allowing flexibility.

title: Functions
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s turn Chapter 2’s inline logic into reusable functions, then have one function call another. Press Run.






summarise doesn’t repeat the labelling rules — it calls label_day. Change the rule in one place and every caller gets the fix. That is the whole point.

title: Functions
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

def names a reusable block with parameters; you call it by name with arguments.
return hands a value back; a function with no return gives back None.
A parameter can have a default value (goal=8000) for the common case.
Functions let you write logic once and reuse it — the cure for copy-paste bugs (DRY: don’t repeat yourself).

title: Functions
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the marked line so is_missing returns True only for an empty string — the way a blank entry shows up in data. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

“Empty string” is the value "" — two quotes with nothing between. Compare value to it with ==.



Show solution

def is_missing(value):
    return value == ""
value == "" is True only for the empty string; a space " " is one character, so it’s False — a distinction that matters when you clean real data.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write clean_mood(text) — tidy a mood label by removing surrounding spaces and lowercasing it, so "  Good ", "good", and "GOOD" all become "good". This is a real cleaning step for the habits data. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Two string methods, chained: .strip() removes the spaces, and .lower() lowercases. You can write them one after another: text.strip().lower().



Show solution

def clean_mood(text):
    return text.strip().lower()
.strip() removes the surrounding spaces and .lower() makes it lowercase; chaining them tidies the label in one expression.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Write a function clean_steps that takes a steps value as text — which might contain a comma like "7,500" or be blank "" — and returns it as an int, using 0 for a blank. Test it on a few values.
Rubric — a good answer:

Removes a comma before converting (.replace(",", "")).
Handles the blank case without crashing (returns 0).
Uses return and runs on at least three different inputs.



Show one possible answer

def clean_steps(text, default=0):
    """Convert a steps string to an int, tolerating commas and blanks."""
    text = text.strip()
    if text == "":
        return default
    return int(text.replace(",", ""))

for value in ["7,500", "8200", ""]:
    print(repr(value), "->", clean_steps(value))
The function tidies the text, returns the default for a blank, and strips a comma before converting — three of the messes you’ll meet in the habits file, handled in one reusable place.

title: Functions
text: Check yourself

title: Functions
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
Functions are the unit of reuse — you now write logic once and call it anywhere, and you’ve built your first real cleaning helpers (clean_mood, clean_steps). Next we look at the containers that hold your data — lists, dicts, sets, and tuples — so those functions have something rich to work on.

title: Comprehensions
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Comprehensions
text:
The hook
In Chapter 4 you kept writing the same three-step ritual: make an empty list, loop over the data, append something each time. It’s so common that Python has a one-line shorthand for it — the comprehension:
step_counts = [d["steps"] for d in days]
Read aloud, that’s “the steps, for each day in days.” Same result as a loop, but it fits on one line and says what it produces.

You’ll need from before: loops (Ch2) and the data structures — lists and dicts (Ch4).

title: Comprehensions
text:
The list comprehension
Here is the loop you’d write, and the comprehension that replaces it:
# the loop
counts = []
for d in days:
    counts.append(d["steps"])

# the comprehension — same result, one line
counts = [d["steps"] for d in days]
The shape is [expressionforitemincollection]. The expression (d["steps"]) is evaluated for each item and collected into a new list.

Add a filter with if
Put an if at the end to keep only some items:
active_dates = [d["date"] for d in days if d["steps"] >= 8000]
“The date, for each day, if that day reached 8,000 steps.” Filtering and transforming, in one readable line.


The dict comprehension
The same idea builds a dictionary — supply a key: value pair:
by_date = {d["date"]: d["steps"] for d in days}
That maps each date to its step count — handy for looking a day up later.

⚠️ Watch out: comprehensions are for clear transforms. If yours needs several conditions or nested loops, a plain for loop is easier to read — and easier to debug. Reach for the one-liner only when it stays a one-thought line.







Note🧱 For Java developers





A comprehension is Python’s built-in version of stream().map(...).filter(...) .collect(...). Because it’s syntax rather than a chain of method calls, the map and filter read in one expression — but the same advice applies: keep each one small and single-purpose.

title: Comprehensions
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Three transforms of the habits days — a map, a filter, and a dict — each in one line. Press Run.






Each line states its intent: the steps, the active dates, each date mapped to its steps. That readability is the whole reason comprehensions exist.

title: Comprehensions
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A list comprehension [expr for x in items] replaces the empty-list / loop / append ritual with one line.
Add an if filter: [expr for x in items if condition] keeps only some items.
A dict comprehension {key: value for x in items} builds a mapping.
Use them for clear transforms; fall back to a for loop when the logic gets complicated.

title: Comprehensions
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the comprehension so all_steps returns a list of each day’s steps. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The expression to collect is each day’s steps, read by key: d["steps"].



Show solution

def all_steps(days):
    return [d["steps"] for d in days]
d["steps"] is evaluated for each day and collected into the new list, in order.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write active_dates(days, goal) — return a list of the dates of days whose steps reach goal, using a filtered comprehension. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The filter (if d["steps"] >= goal) is already written; you only need the value to collect, which is the day’s date: d["date"].



Show solution

def active_dates(days, goal):
    return [d["date"] for d in days if d["steps"] >= goal]
The if keeps only days that reach the goal; for each of those, the expression d["date"] collects the date.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Build mood_by_date(days) that returns a dict mapping each day’s date to its mood, using a dict comprehension. Print it for a list you make up.
Rubric — a good answer:

Uses a dict comprehension {...: ... for d in days}.
Maps d["date"] to d["mood"].
Returns the dict and runs on a list of at least two days.



Show one possible answer

def mood_by_date(days):
    return {d["date"]: d["mood"] for d in days}

print(mood_by_date([
    {"date": "2026-01-01", "mood": "good"},
    {"date": "2026-01-02", "mood": "ok"},
]))
# {'2026-01-01': 'good', '2026-01-02': 'ok'}
The dict comprehension reads each day once and pairs its date with its mood — a lookup table built in a single line.

title: Comprehensions
text: Check yourself

title: Comprehensions
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
Comprehensions let you transform data in one clear line — you’ll reach for them constantly with NumPy and Pandas later. Next we get data in and out of files, reading and writing the CSV and JSON your habits data actually lives in.

title: Errors & exceptions
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Errors & exceptions
text:
The hook
One bad value can sink an entire script. In the habits file, sleep_hours has a stray "8h" and steps has "7,500". The moment your code hits int("8h"), it raises an error — and if nothing catches it, the loop stops dead, even though the other 34 rows were perfectly fine.
Real data always has bad values. Robust code expects them and keeps going. try/except is how you do that.

You’ll need from before: converting strings to numbers (Ch1) and reading rows from a file (Ch6) — the exact place bad values show up.

title: Errors & exceptions
text:
What is an exception?
An exception is an error raised while your program runs — dividing by zero, converting "8h" to an int, looking up a key that isn’t there. If nothing catches it, the exception stops the program and prints a traceback (the report we’ll learn to read in the next chapter).
A few you’ll meet constantly:

ValueError — a value is the wrong kind, like int("8h").
KeyError — a dict key doesn’t exist, like row["stpes"] (a typo).
ZeroDivisionError — dividing by zero.

title: Errors & exceptions
text:
try / except
Put the risky code in a try block and the recovery in an except block:
try:
    n = int(value)     # might raise ValueError
except ValueError:
    n = 0              # recover: use a safe default instead
If int(value) succeeds, the except is skipped. If it raises a ValueError, Python jumps to the except and your program carries on.

Catch the specific exception
Always name the exception you expect. A bare except: catches everything — including bugs you’d want to know about, like a misspelled variable:
try:
    n = int(value)
except ValueError:    # good: only bad-number errors
    n = 0

⚠️ Watch out: except: or except Exception: with no thought hides real bugs. Catch the narrowest exception that makes sense (ValueError here), so a typo still surfaces loudly instead of silently becoming 0.



Reading the error message
Add as e to get the exception object, whose text explains what went wrong:
try:
    int("8h")
except ValueError as e:
    print("Could not convert:", e)
You can also raise your own when input is invalid: raise ValueError("steps cannot be negative").






Note🧱 For Java developers





try/except is Python’s try/catch; except ValueError as e: is catch (NumberFormatException e). The big difference: Python has no checked exceptions — you’re never forced to declare or catch anything, so catching is a choice you make where recovery makes sense. finally works exactly as in Java.

title: Errors & exceptions
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Clean a column of messy step values without letting one bad entry stop the loop. Press Run.






int("7,500"), int("8h"), and int("") each raise a ValueError, so they land in the except and become None — while "7500" and "8200" convert cleanly. The loop survives all five rows.






The message — invalid literal for int() with base 10: ‘8h’ — is the same text you’ll see at the bottom of a traceback next chapter.

title: Errors & exceptions
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

An exception is a runtime error; if uncaught it stops the program.
try/except runs risky code and recovers if it fails — so one bad row doesn’t crash the whole job.
Catch the specific exception (except ValueError) so you don’t hide unrelated bugs.
except … as e gives you the error message; raise lets you signal bad input from your own code.

title: Errors & exceptions
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the except so safe_int returns None when a value can’t be converted. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

int("8h") raises a ValueError — the wrong kind of value. That’s the exception to catch.



Show solution

def safe_int(text):
    try:
        return int(text)
    except ValueError:
        return None
int() raises ValueError for unparsable text; the except ValueError catches exactly that and returns None instead of crashing.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write clean_steps(text) — strip a comma, convert to an int, and fall back to 0 if it still can’t be parsed. This is the robust version of the habits cleaner. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The task says “fall back to 0” — so when conversion fails, the except should return 0.



Show solution

def clean_steps(text):
    try:
        return int(text.replace(",", ""))
    except ValueError:
        return 0
Removing the comma fixes "7,500"; anything still unconvertible ("8h", "") raises ValueError, caught to return 0 — so the cleaner never crashes.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Write count_bad(values) that goes through a list of step strings and returns how many could not be converted to an int. Use try/except and test it on a mixed list.
Rubric — a good answer:

Loops over the values with a try/except ValueError.
Increments a counter only in the except branch.
Returns the count and runs on a list with both good and bad values.



Show one possible answer

def count_bad(values):
    bad = 0
    for v in values:
        try:
            int(v)
        except ValueError:
            bad += 1
    return bad

print(count_bad(["7500", "8h", "8200", ""]))   # 2
Each value is attempted in the try; only the failures reach the except, where bad grows. Here "8h" and "" fail, so the count is 2.

title: Errors & exceptions
text: Check yourself

title: Errors & exceptions
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now keep a job running over messy data, recovering from bad values instead of crashing on them. But when an error isn’t something you expected, Python hands you a traceback — and that report is your best debugging tool. Next chapter, we learn to read one and find the bug fast.

title: A little OOP
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: A little OOP
text:
The hook
You’ve been using objects since Chapter 1. When you write name.strip() or rows.append(row), that name and that rows are objects — each carries some data (the text, the list of items) and a set of behaviours that act on it (.strip(), .append()). The dot means “ask this object to do something with its own data”.
A class lets you make your own such bundles. As you reach for libraries later (NumPy arrays, pandas DataFrames, model objects), you’ll meet classes constantly — this chapter is enough to read them with confidence. We’ll wrap one row of the habits data in a small Day object.

You’ll need from before: defining functions with parameters and defaults (Ch3) and the dict/list way of holding a row of data (Ch4) — a class is a tidier home for that same data plus the functions that use it.

title: A little OOP
text:
An object bundles data + behaviour
So far a day of habits data has lived as a dict: {"date": "2026-01-02", "steps": 8200}. That holds the data, but the logic that works on it (is this an active day?) floats around in separate functions. An object keeps both in one place: the values and the behaviour that belongs with them.
A class is the blueprint; an instance is one object built from it. Calling the class — Day(...) — builds an instance, the same way list() builds a new list.

title: A little OOP
section: Defining a class with __init__ and methods
text:
Defining a class with __init__ and methods
class Day:
    """One day of habits data."""
    def __init__(self, date, steps):
        self.date = date
        self.steps = steps

    def is_active(self, goal=8000):
        return self.steps >= goal

class Day: starts the blueprint. The triple-quoted line is its docstring.
__init__ is the constructor: Python runs it once, automatically, when you create an instance. Its job is to store the starting attributes on the object.
is_active is a method — a function that belongs to the class.

An attribute is data stored on the object (self.date, self.steps). A method is behaviour you call on it (d.is_active()).

What self is
Every method’s first parameter is self: the particular instance the method was called on. When you write d.is_active(), Python passes d in as self, so self.steps reads that day’s steps. You never pass self yourself — the dot does it for you.


A default method argument
is_active(self, goal=8000) gives goal a default (Ch3, same idea). Call d.is_active() and it compares against 8000; call d.is_active(5000) to use your own goal for that one call.






Note🧱 For Java developers





class is your class; __init__ is the constructor; self is this — except Python makes it the explicit first parameter of every method instead of an implicit keyword. Differences worth noting: there are no access modifiers (public / private) and no type declarations on attributes — you set self.steps and it exists. You also rarely write getters/setters; reading d.steps directly is normal and idiomatic, and a property can be added later without changing callers.

title: A little OOP
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Build one Day from a habits row, then read its attribute and call its method. Press Run.






Day("2026-01-02", 8200) runs __init__, which stores the two attributes. Then d.date and d.steps read them back, and d.is_active() runs the method against the default goal of 8000 — 8200 >= 8000 is True.






Passing 6000 overrides the default for that call. Each day is its own instance with its own self.steps, so the same method gives a different answer per object.

title: A little OOP
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

An object bundles data (attributes) with behaviour (methods) — you’ve used this all along with strings and lists.
A class is the blueprint; calling it (Day(...)) builds an instance, and __init__ runs once to set that instance’s starting attributes.
self is the instance a method is acting on; self.steps reads that object’s own attribute. You read attributes and call methods with the dot: d.steps, d.is_active().
A method can take a default argument (goal=8000), used when the caller leaves it out.

title: A little OOP
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete __init__ so a StepCounter stores its step count as an attribute. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

__init__ receives the value as the parameter steps. Assign that parameter to the attribute — the right-hand side is the parameter name itself.



Show solution

class StepCounter:
    """Holds a single day's step count."""
    def __init__(self, steps):
        self.steps = steps
__init__ runs when you write StepCounter(7500); assigning self.steps = steps copies the argument onto the instance, so StepCounter(7500).steps reads back 7500.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Complete the is_active method so it returns True when the day’s steps reach the goal. Fill in the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Read this day’s steps with self.steps, then compare to goal with >=. The method should return that comparison directly.



Show solution

class Day:
    """One day of habits data, with an activity check."""
    def __init__(self, date, steps):
        self.date = date
        self.steps = steps

    def is_active(self, goal=8000):
        return self.steps >= goal
self.steps is this instance’s own count; self.steps >= goal is a boolean, so the method returns True or False. Leaving goal out uses the default 8000.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Design a small Tally class that adds up steps across several days: an __init__ that starts the running total at 0, an add(self, steps) method that adds to it, and a total attribute you can read. Build one and add a few values.
Rubric — a good answer:

__init__ sets a starting attribute (e.g. self.total = 0).
add updates self.total using the argument passed in.
You create an instance, call add a couple of times, and read the total back.



Show one possible answer

class Tally:
    """A running total of steps across days."""
    def __init__(self):
        self.total = 0

    def add(self, steps):
        self.total += steps

t = Tally()
t.add(8200)
t.add(5000)
print(t.total)   # 13200
__init__ starts self.total at 0; each add call updates that one instance’s attribute, so after adding 8200 and 5000 the total reads 13200.

title: A little OOP
text: Check yourself

title: A little OOP
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now read the classes that libraries hand you — construct one, read its attributes, call its methods — and write a small class of your own to keep data and behaviour together. Next chapter steps back to a skill that makes all of this readable to the next person (including future you): Clean, readable code.

title: Pythonic data idioms
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
The hook
Your habits data often arrives as separate columns: one list of dates, another of steps. To work with them you need each date sitting next to its own step count. You could reach for range(len(dates)) and index both lists by hand — but Python has cleaner tools for exactly this. zip pairs the two lists, enumerate numbers the rows for you, and dict.get reads a value with a fallback when a key is missing. These small idioms are what make data code short and readable.

You’ll need from before: lists, tuples, and dicts (Ch4), and the loop-shaped thinking behind comprehensions (Ch5).

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
Pairing two lists with zip
zip walks several lists together and hands you one item from each at a time:
dates = ["2026-06-01", "2026-06-02"]
steps = [7500, 8200]
list(zip(dates, steps))   # [('2026-06-01', 7500), ('2026-06-02', 8200)]
Each result is a tuple — dates[0] paired with steps[0], then dates[1] with steps[1]. zip stops at the shorter list, so mismatched lengths never raise; the extra tail is dropped. zip returns a lazy iterator, so wrap it in list(...) when you want to see or reuse the pairs.

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
Numbering items with enumerate
When you want a counter alongside each item, enumerate beats tracking an index variable yourself:
for i, step in enumerate(steps, start=1):
    print(i, step)        # 1 7500 \n 2 8200
enumerate(items) yields (index, item) pairs starting at 0; pass start=1 for human-friendly 1-based numbering.

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
Tuple unpacking
Both zip and enumerate hand back tuples, and Python lets you unpack a tuple straight into named variables in one step:
pair = ("2026-06-01", 7500)
date, steps = pair          # date = "2026-06-01", steps = 7500
That same move works in a for loop header, which is why the loops above read so cleanly:
for date, steps in zip(dates, step_counts):
    print(date, "->", steps)
No temporary pair variable, no pair[0] / pair[1] — the names say what each slot means.

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
Safe lookups with dict.get
Indexing a missing key (row["mood"]) raises KeyError. dict.get lets you ask for a value and name a fallback for when it’s absent:
row = {"date": "2026-06-01", "steps": 7500}
row.get("mood", "unknown")   # "unknown" — the key is missing, so the default
row.get("steps", 0)          # 7500 — the key exists, so its value
This is the safe way to read optional columns from messy habits rows.






Note🧱 For Java developers





These idioms replace index-based loops you’d write by reflex in Java. zip plus a for date, steps in … loop does the work of a for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) that reaches into two arrays by index; enumerate(items, start=1) is the same loop when you genuinely need the counter. Tuple unpacking (date, steps = pair) drops the temp variables you’d otherwise pull out with pair.getKey() / pair.getValue(). And row.get("mood", "unknown") is Map.getOrDefault(key, fallback) — one call, no containsKey check first.

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Pair the dates with their step counts, number the days, and read a mood that may be missing. Press Run.






zip(dates, steps) yields (date, count) pairs; enumerate(..., start=1) wraps each pair with a 1-based counter; the loop header unpacks both at once into day, date, and count.






The mood key is missing, so get returns the fallback "unknown" instead of raising KeyError; steps is present, so its real value comes back.

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

zip(a, b) pairs two lists element-by-element into tuples, stopping at the shorter one — ideal for lining up parallel columns.
enumerate(items, start=1) gives you a counter and the item together, so you never hand-manage an index.
Tuple unpacking (date, steps = pair, or for date, steps in pairs:) names each slot and drops temporary variables.
dict.get(key, default) reads a value safely, returning the fallback instead of raising KeyError when the key is missing.

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete the safe lookup so mood_or_unknown returns the row’s mood, or "unknown" when that key is missing. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The fallback is the second argument to get. The task asks for the string "unknown" when no mood is recorded.



Show solution

def mood_or_unknown(row):
    return row.get("mood", "unknown")
dict.get returns the value for "mood" when it exists, and the second argument — "unknown" — when it doesn’t, so the lookup never raises KeyError.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write pair_days(dates, steps) so it returns a list of (date, steps) tuples, using zip to line the two lists up. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

zip(dates, steps) yields the pairs lazily; wrap it in list(...) to return them as an actual list of tuples.



Show solution

def pair_days(dates, steps):
    return list(zip(dates, steps))
zip walks both lists together, producing (date, steps) tuples; list(...) collects them. Two empty lists produce no pairs, so the result is [].



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Write print_days(dates) that prints a numbered list of days, 1-based, using enumerate. Test it on a small list of dates.
Rubric — a good answer:

Loops over the dates with enumerate(dates, start=1).
Unpacks the counter and the date in the loop header (for i, date in …).
Prints each line as the number followed by its date, and runs on a sample list.



Show one possible answer

def print_days(dates):
    for i, date in enumerate(dates, start=1):
        print(f"{i}. {date}")

print_days(["2026-06-01", "2026-06-02", "2026-06-03"])
# 1. 2026-06-01
# 2. 2026-06-02
# 3. 2026-06-03
enumerate(dates, start=1) pairs each date with a 1-based counter; the loop header unpacks that pair into i and date, so each line numbers a day without any manual index bookkeeping.

title: Pythonic data idioms
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now line up parallel columns, number rows, unpack tuples, and read optional keys safely — the everyday idioms that keep data code short. Next chapter is A NumPy preview (chapter 12), where these same lists become arrays you can do math on all at once, without writing the loop yourself.

title: Environments & packages
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Environments & packages
text:
The hook
You’ve imported numpy and used csv without a thought — but where do those packages come from, and what happens when two projects need different versions of the same one? Project A wants pandas 1.5, project B wants pandas 2.3. Install one and you break the other.
The fix every real project uses is a virtual environment: a private box of packages, one per project, that can’t clash with anything else. This chapter is the what and why of environments and packages — the habits that keep a project runnable tomorrow and on someone else’s machine.

You’ll need from before: that you import packages to use them (Ch6, Ch12), and that code runs in the browser here via Pyodide (Ch2).

title: Environments & packages
section: Packages, and where Python looks for them
text:
Packages, and where Python looks for them
A package is reusable code someone else wrote — numpy, pandas, csv. When you import one, Python searches a list of folders (sys.path) for it. The cell below is a runnable look at that search path and what’s available right now, in this browser runtime:






In the browser, packages live in Pyodide’s in-memory runtime, and the tool that adds more is micropip — the browser counterpart of pip. On your own computer, the tool is pip, and you keep each project’s packages separate with a virtual environment.

title: Environments & packages
section: Virtual environments, on your own machine
text:
Virtual environments, on your own machine
A virtual environment (venv) is a folder holding one project’s own Python and its own packages. You create one, “activate” it, and any pip install from then on goes into that project alone. When you’re done, you record the exact versions in a requirements.txt so anyone can rebuild the same setup.

💡 On your own computer (this page runs in the browser, so the commands below are for your real machine, not here). These are real commands; the pip freeze output is from an actual run:
python -m venv .venv             # create the isolated environment
source .venv/bin/activate        # turn it on  (Windows: .venv\Scripts\activate)
pip install wrapt==1.16.0        # install into THIS project only
pip freeze                       # list exact installed versions
# -> wrapt==1.16.0
pip freeze > requirements.txt    # save them so the project is reproducible

Anyone who later runs pip install -r requirements.txt in their own fresh venv gets the very same versions — the project runs the same on every machine.






Note🧱 For Java developers





A venv plays the role of an isolated dependency set per project — think of requirements.txt as Python’s pom.xml/build.gradle dependency list, and pip/micropip as Maven/Gradle fetching from a registry (PyPI). The big difference: there’s no compile step and no central target/ — packages install straight into the active environment, which is why isolating that environment matters.




⚠️ Watch out: pinning exact versions (==2.3.0) is what makes a project reproducible. An unpinned pip install pandas grabs whatever is newest that day, so the same code can behave differently next month.

title: Environments & packages
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
You won’t run pip in the browser, but the requirements.txt format is plain text you can build and read with the Python you already know. Here we turn a few packages into pinned requirement lines. Press Run.






Each line is name==version — the same shape pip freeze printed in the transcript above. Reading and writing this format is all you need to keep a project pinned.

title: Environments & packages
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A package is reusable code you import; Python finds it on sys.path, and pip (or micropip in the browser) installs more.
A virtual environment gives each project its own packages, so versions can’t clash between projects.
A requirements.txt of name==version lines pins exact versions, making a project reproducible on any machine.
Pin versions deliberately — unpinned installs grab whatever is newest and can change behaviour later.

title: Environments & packages
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈5 min) 🟢
Complete pin so it builds a requirements.txt line from a package name and version. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

You want the text name, then ==, then version. An f-string makes this readable: f"{name}=={version}".



Show solution

def pin(name, version):
    return f"{name}=={version}"
The f-string drops name and version into the name==version shape that pip reads — exactly what a requirements.txt line looks like.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈10 min) 🟡
Write parse_requirement(line) — split a requirements.txt line like "pandas==2.3.0" into a (name, version) tuple. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

"pandas==2.3.0".split("==") returns the list ["pandas", "2.3.0"]. Unpacking that into name, version gives you the two pieces.



Show solution

def parse_requirement(line):
    name, version = line.split("==")
    return name, version
line.split("==") breaks the text at the == into a two-item list, which unpacks straight into name and version.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). In two or three sentences, explain to a teammate why your project should ship a pinned requirements.txt rather than asking everyone to pip install pandas with no version. Then write the three requirement lines your project would pin (pick real packages and versions).
Rubric — a good answer:

Explains that pinning gives everyone the same versions, so the project is reproducible and doesn’t break when a package updates.
Mentions that a virtual environment keeps those packages from clashing with other projects.
Lists three name==version lines.



Show one possible answer

requirements = [
    "numpy==2.5.0",
    "pandas==2.3.0",
    "matplotlib==3.9.0",
]
print("\n".join(requirements))
“Pinning exact versions means everyone installs the same packages, so the code runs the same on my machine, yours, and the server — and an upstream update next month can’t silently change our results. We install them into a per-project virtual environment so they never clash with another project’s versions.”

You now know how real projects keep their packages isolated and pinned — the habit that makes code reproducible. The last Python chapter looks at where you write code in the first place: notebooks, an editor, and free cloud GPUs via Colab — and when to reach for each.

title: Vectors & geometry
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Vectors & geometry
text:
The hook
You already use vectors without the name. A location on a map is “3 right, 4 up” from some corner. That pair of numbers is a vector: a short list that names a point. Draw an arrow from the origin (0, 0) to that point and you have the same vector as a picture — a direction and a distance.
This is the recurring idea of the module: data becomes vectors. A movie rated on “funny” and “scary” is the vector [funny, scary]. A pixel’s red, green, blue values are the vector [r, g, b]. Once data is a list of numbers, geometry — points, arrows, distances — becomes a way to reason about it.

You’ll need from before: the spirit of Ch1 — picture first, symbols second. No new tools; we draw arrows and count squares.

title: Vectors & geometry
section: A vector is a point and an arrow
text:
A vector is a point and an arrow
Write a 2D vector as [x, y]. Two readings of the same thing:

As a point: go x along the horizontal axis and y up the vertical axis. The vector [2, 1] lands at the spot two right and one up.
As an arrow: draw a straight line from the origin (0, 0) out to that point. The arrow has a direction (where it points) and a length (how far).

Both readings carry the same information. We pick whichever is handier for the job.

Adding vectors — tip to tail
To add [1, 2] and [3, 4], add matching slots: the x’s together, the y’s together.
[1, 2] + [3, 4] = [1 + 3, 2 + 4] = [4, 6]
That is the componentwise rule. The picture: place the second arrow so its tail starts at the tip of the first. Walk along the first arrow, then along the second, and you arrive at [4, 6] — the same place the rule predicts.


Scaling a vector — stretch or shrink
Multiply a vector by a single number (a scalar) and every component scales by it:
2 * [3, 1] = [6, 2]
The arrow keeps its direction but changes length — 2 stretches it to twice as long, 0.5 shrinks it by half, and a negative number flips it to point the other way.


How long is a vector?
The length (or magnitude) of [x, y] is the straight-line distance from the origin to the point. The arrow is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs x and y, so the Pythagorean theorem gives:
magnitude([x, y]) = sqrt(x*x + y*y)
For [3, 4] that is sqrt(9 + 16) = sqrt(25) = 5.0. The “3 across, 4 up” arrow is exactly 5 units long.






Note🧱 For Java developers





In Java you would reach for a fixed-size double[] or a record Vec2(double x, double y) — the size is known and the type is rigid. Here a vector is a plain Python list like [3.0, 4.0]; you index it with v[0] and v[1], and you don’t need a separate Point class — the same list is the point and the arrow.
One difference to expect: there is no operator overloading doing the math for you, so element-wise addition is written out by hand ([a[0] + b[0], a[1] + b[1]]) in this chapter. Later, NumPy automates exactly this — a + b on arrays adds componentwise for you — but writing it by hand first makes the rule concrete.

title: Vectors & geometry
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s draw two vectors as arrows from the origin and see their directions and lengths.






The takeaway from the picture: each vector is an arrow leaving the origin. [2, 1] points mostly to the right and a little up; [1, 3] points mostly up and a little right. The arrow for [1, 3] reaches farther from the origin — its length is sqrt(1 + 9) ≈ 3.16, against sqrt(4 + 1) ≈ 2.24 for [2, 1]. The numbers in the list and the arrow on the grid are two views of one object.
We can confirm those lengths in code:

title: Vectors & geometry
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A 2D vector [x, y] is a list of numbers that names a point — and an arrow from the origin to that point.
Add vectors componentwise ([1, 2] + [3, 4] = [4, 6]); the picture is tip-to-tail.
Scaling by a number stretches or shrinks the arrow (and a negative flips it), keeping its line.
The length of [x, y] is sqrt(x*x + y*y) — so magnitude([3, 4]) = 5.0.

title: Vectors & geometry
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Complete vector_add so it returns the componentwise sum of two 2-element vectors. The first slot is filled in; replace ____ with the second, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The second slot mirrors the first: where the first is a[0] + b[0], the second adds the y components, a[1] + b[1].



Show solution

def vector_add(a, b):
    return [a[0] + b[0], a[1] + b[1]]

run_tests([
    ("[1,2] + [3,4]", vector_add([1, 2], [3, 4]), [4, 6]),
    ("[0,0] + [5,-2]", vector_add([0, 0], [5, -2]), [5, -2]),
])
Each output slot is the sum of the matching input slots — the componentwise rule, and the tip-to-tail picture in numbers.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write magnitude(v) — the length of a 2D vector as a float, using sqrt(v[0]^2 + v[1]^2). In Python, raising to the 0.5 power takes a square root, so write the expression with ** 0.5. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Square each component, add them, then take the square root by raising the sum to the 0.5 power: (v[0] * v[0] + v[1] * v[1]) ** 0.5. Mind the parentheses — ** 0.5 binds tightly, so the whole sum must be wrapped.



Show solution

def magnitude(v):
    return (v[0] * v[0] + v[1] * v[1]) ** 0.5

run_tests([
    ("length of [3,4]", magnitude([3, 4]), 5.0),
    ("length of [0,0]", magnitude([0, 0]), 0.0),
    ("length of [6,8]", magnitude([6, 8]), 10.0),
])
[3, 4] gives sqrt(9 + 16) = sqrt(25) = 5.0. The grader compares floats with a small tolerance, so the result reads as exactly 5.0.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Scale a vector by a number, then describe what happens to its length. Pick a vector like [3, 4], multiply it by a scalar such as 2, compute the magnitude before and after, and print both.
Rubric — a good answer:

Scales a vector by a number componentwise (each slot times the scalar).
Computes the magnitude before and after, and prints both numbers.
States the pattern in words: scaling by k multiplies the length by k (for a positive k).



Show one possible answer

def magnitude(v):
    return (v[0] * v[0] + v[1] * v[1]) ** 0.5

def scale(v, k):
    return [v[0] * k, v[1] * k]

v = [3, 4]
k = 2
scaled = scale(v, k)
print(f"{v} has length {magnitude(v)}")
print(f"{scaled} has length {magnitude(scaled)}")
print(f"length scaled by {k}: {magnitude(scaled) / magnitude(v)}")
[3, 4] has length 5.0; [6, 8] has length 10.0. Scaling the arrow by 2 doubled its length while keeping its direction — multiplying a vector by k multiplies its length by k.

title: Vectors & geometry
text: Check yourself

title: Vectors & geometry
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
A vector holds one piece of data as a point and an arrow, and you can add, scale, and measure it. Next we step up from a single arrow to whole grids of numbers — matrices as transformations: how a table of numbers can move, rotate, and reshape the vectors from this chapter.

title: Matrix multiplication, intuitively
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Matrix multiplication, intuitively
text:
The hook
Think of a matrix as an instruction for moving points around: stretch, flip, rotate. Now suppose you want to do two of them in a row — “stretch the picture wide, then flip it upside down.” You could carry each point through the first instruction and then the second. Or you could fold both instructions into one matrix and do them together in a single step.
That folding-together is exactly what matrix multiplication is. Multiplying two matrices means composing their transformations: the product is the single matrix that does one transformation and then the other. Before we touch a formula, hold onto that picture — a product is “do this, then that.”

You’ll need from before: a matrix as a transformation that moves points, and how a 2x2 matrix acts on a vector (M2 Ch3). That’s the one tool we carry in.

title: Matrix multiplication, intuitively
section: One entry at a time: row dot column
text:
One entry at a time: row dot column
Here is the whole computation, built from a piece you already half-know. To multiply two matrices you walk across a row of the first and down a column of the second, multiply the pairs, and add them up. That add-up-the-products step is a dot product:
\[[a, b] \cdot [c, d] = a \times c + b \times d\]
A concrete one: \([1, 2] \cdot [3, 4] = 1 \times 3 + 2 \times 4 = 3 + 8 = 11\). Two more to fix the pattern: \([2, 0] \cdot [5, 7] = 10 + 0 = 10\), and \([1, 1] \cdot [1, 1] = 1 + 1 = 2\). Each one collapses two pairs of numbers into a single number.
For two 2x2 matrices \(A\) and \(B\), the product \(C = A \cdot B\) fills in each entry this way: the entry in row \(i\), column \(j\) of \(C\) is the dot product of row \(i\) of \(A\) with column \(j\) of \(B\).
\[
A = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix},\quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} e & f \\ g & h \end{bmatrix},\quad
A \cdot B = \begin{bmatrix} a e + b g & a f + b h \\ c e + d g & c f + d h \end{bmatrix}
\]
Top-left of the answer comes from the top row of \(A\) and the left column of \(B\). Top-right comes from the top row of \(A\) and the right column of \(B\). Four entries, four little dot products — that is the entire rule.

Why this is “do one, then the other”
When you apply matrix \(A\) to a vector you get a new vector; apply \(B\) to that and you get a third. Doing both, \(B(A v)\), is the same as building the combined matrix once and applying it a single time. So a sequence of transformations becomes a single multiplication — which is why a model can squeeze many layers of “move the data this way” into compact matrix math.






Note🧱 For Java developers





In code, a 2x2 (or N x N) multiply is plain nested loops: an outer loop over the rows of A, an inner loop over the columns of B, and an innermost accumulate that sums A[i][k] * B[k][j]. Three loops, an O(n^3) shape — the same triple loop you would write for any grid-by-grid combine in Java.
That is also why libraries like NumPy (and the BLAS kernels under it) exist. For a 2x2 the loop is fine, but real models multiply matrices with thousands of rows and columns, millions of times. NumPy hands that inner loop to optimized, cache-aware native code, so you write A @ B and get speed you would never reach hand-rolling the loops yourself. You learn the loop to understand it; you call the library to run it fast.

title: Matrix multiplication, intuitively
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s see two transformations stack. Take a unit square, apply transformation \(A\) (a horizontal stretch), then apply transformation \(B\) (a vertical flip) to the result. Watching the square change twice makes “compose” concrete.






The square widens first (transformation \(A\)), then the wide square flips below the axis (transformation \(B\)). Two instructions, applied in order — and that ordered pair of moves is precisely what one matrix product captures.
Now the punchline: folding “A then B” into one matrix is the product \(B \cdot A\) (the second transformation sits on the left). Applying that single combined matrix lands every point in the same place as doing \(A\) and then \(B\).






Both paths send \((1, 1)\) to the same point. The combined matrix is not a shortcut you have to trust — it is the two transformations, multiplied into one.

title: Matrix multiplication, intuitively
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

One entry of a matrix product is a dot product: a row of the first matrix with a column of the second, \([a, b] \cdot [c, d] = a \times c + b \times d\).
For 2x2 matrices, \(C[i][j]\) is row \(i\) of \(A\) dotted with column \(j\) of \(B\) — four entries, four small dot products.
Multiplying matrices composes transformations: the product is the single matrix for doing one and then the other.
Order matters — \(A \cdot B\) and \(B \cdot A\) can differ, because doing the moves in a different order can land points in a different place.

title: Matrix multiplication, intuitively
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Every entry of a product is a dot product, so start there. Complete dot(row, col) for two 2-element lists: multiply the matching pairs and add them. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The first pair is already there: row[0] * col[0]. The second pair follows the same shape with index 1: row[1] * col[1]. Add the two together.



Show solution

def dot(row, col):
    return row[0] * col[0] + row[1] * col[1]
Each pair is multiplied — row[0]*col[0] and row[1]*col[1] — and the two products are added. That single number is what fills one slot of a matrix product.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now build the full product. Write matmul2(A, B) for two 2x2 matrices, returning a 2x2 result where result[i][j] is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B. You may reuse a dot of a row and a column. Complete the marked lines.









If you’re stuck

cols[1] is column 1 of B. The top-right entry is row 0 of A dotted with that column: dot(A[0], cols[1]). The other three entries are already filled in to match.



Show solution

def matmul2(A, B):
    def dot(row, col):
        return row[0] * col[0] + row[1] * col[1]

    cols = [[B[0][0], B[1][0]], [B[0][1], B[1][1]]]
    return [
        [dot(A[0], cols[0]), dot(A[0], cols[1])],
        [dot(A[1], cols[0]), dot(A[1], cols[1])],
    ]

run_tests([
    ("identity leaves B", matmul2([[1, 0], [0, 1]], [[2, 3], [4, 5]]), [[2, 3], [4, 5]]),
    ("double then ones", matmul2([[2, 0], [0, 2]], [[1, 1], [1, 1]]), [[2, 2], [2, 2]]),
])
Pulling out the columns of B first makes each entry a clean dot(row, col). Multiplying by the identity copies B straight through, and doubling then summing ones gives all 2s — both match the entry-by-entry rule.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈4 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Show with one example that order matters: pick two 2x2 matrices A and B for which A·B and B·A are different, compute both products, and print them side by side.
Rubric — a good answer:

Chooses two specific 2x2 matrices and computes both A·B and B·A.
Prints both results so the difference is visible.
States in a sentence that the two differ, so order is not interchangeable.



Show one possible answer

def matmul2(A, B):
    def dot(row, col):
        return row[0] * col[0] + row[1] * col[1]

    cols = [[B[0][0], B[1][0]], [B[0][1], B[1][1]]]
    return [
        [dot(A[0], cols[0]), dot(A[0], cols[1])],
        [dot(A[1], cols[0]), dot(A[1], cols[1])],
    ]

A = [[1, 1], [0, 1]]
B = [[1, 0], [1, 1]]
print("A·B =", matmul2(A, B))
print("B·A =", matmul2(B, A))
print("Different — so the order of two transformations is not interchangeable.")
Here A·B = [[2, 1], [1, 1]] but B·A = [[1, 1], [1, 2]]. The same two matrices in the other order land points differently, which is what “order matters” means.

title: Matrix multiplication, intuitively
text: Check yourself

title: Matrix multiplication, intuitively
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now read a matrix product two ways: mechanically, as rows dotted with columns, and meaningfully, as one transformation followed by another. That dot product at the heart of every entry is about to get its own spotlight. Next we look at dot products & similarity (Ch5) — the same multiply-and-add, used to measure how alike two vectors are.

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
text:
The hook
Picture driving with the accelerator. Press it a little and your speed climbs a little; press it a little on a steep hill and your speed barely moves. The same nudge, two different responses. That responsiveness — how much the output changes when you nudge the input a tiny bit — is exactly what a derivative measures. A derivative is sensitivity.
There is a second picture for the same idea: the slope of a curve at a point. On a straight line the steepness never changes, so its slope is one number. On a curve the steepness keeps shifting, so you zoom in on one point until the curve looks straight, and read the slope right there. That local slope is the derivative.

You’ll need from before: a relationship you can draw as a line (M2 Ch1 — the “predict the tip from the bill” line) and reading a pair of numbers as a point (x, y) (M2 Ch2). That’s all we carry in.

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
section: A straight line: slope is rise over run
text:
A straight line: slope is rise over run
Recall the tip line from Chapter 1: tip = rate * bill. Pick any two points on a straight line, (x1, y1) and (x2, y2). The slope is how far you climb divided by how far you walk across:
slope = rise / run = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1)
If a 10% tip line passes through (0, 0) and (100, 10), its slope is (10 - 0) / (100 - 0) = 0.1. That slope is the sensitivity: nudge the bill up by ₹1 and the predicted tip rises by ₹0.10, anywhere on the line. On a straight line the derivative is the same value at every point — the line’s single, constant slope.

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
section: A curve: zoom in until it looks straight
text:
A curve: zoom in until it looks straight
A curve bends, so its steepness changes as you move along it. Take f(x) = x². Near x = 1 it climbs gently; near x = 3 it climbs steeply. There is no single slope for the whole curve — there is a slope at each point.
Here is the move that makes a derivative concrete. Zoom in on one point of a smooth curve. Zoom far enough and the little piece you see looks like a straight segment. Measure that segment’s slope and you have the derivative at the point. The derivative answers: right here, if I nudge x a tiny bit, how fast does f(x) respond?
We can turn “zoom in and measure” into arithmetic. Take a tiny step h to either side of x and measure the rise over the run between those two nearby points — a central difference:
numeric_derivative ≈ (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2h)
The run from x - h to x + h is 2h, and the rise is the change in f across it. With a small h (we use h = 1e-5), this estimate is close for a smooth curve and exact for a straight line, because a line has the same slope across any run. In prose: for f(x) = x² the true derivative is 2x (slope 2 at x = 1, slope 6 at x = 3), and the numeric step lands right next to those values — but we will keep our auto-graded checks on straight lines, where the arithmetic is exact.






Note🧱 For Java developers





Think of the derivative as the limit of rise-over-run as the run shrinks toward zero — the formal lim h→0 (f(x+h) - f(x)) / h you may have met once. There are two ways to get it in code. Numeric differentiation is what we do here: pick a tiny h and divide, like a finite-difference approximation — a few floating-point operations, no calculus rules. Symbolic differentiation instead applies the rules of calculus to the formula itself, returning a new formula (the derivative of x² is 2*x), the way a computer-algebra library would.
Modern deep-learning frameworks use a third route, automatic differentiation (autograd): as your code computes a value it records each operation, then walks that record backward applying known per-operation derivatives, giving exact slopes without you writing any by hand. PyTorch’s autograd and JAX’s grad do this; the central difference below is the hand-cranked version that helps you check them.

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s see the derivative as a local slope. We plot f(x) = x² and, at x = 1, draw the straight tangent line whose slope is the derivative there (2 × 1 = 2). Where the curve and the line touch, they head in the same direction — the line is the curve’s “looks straight when you zoom in” twin at that point.






Read the picture: the orange line rises 2 units for every 1 unit across, so the curve’s slope at that point is 2 — that is the derivative of x² at x = 1. Slide the touch-point to the right and the tangent tilts up more steeply (slope 2x grows), which is the curve telling you it is getting more sensitive to x. The derivative is not one number for the whole curve; it is a number you read off at each point.

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A derivative measures sensitivity — how much the output changes when you nudge the input a tiny bit. It is the slope of the curve at a point.
On a straight line the slope is constant: rise / run = (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1).
On a curve the slope changes; zoom in until the point looks straight and read the slope there.
You can estimate a derivative numerically with a central difference, (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2h), for a tiny h — exact on a line, close on a curve.

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
The slope of a line is rise over run. Complete slope(p1, p2) where each point is a pair (x, y). Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Rise is the change in y (p2[1] - p1[1]); the run is the matching change in x, p2[0] - p1[0]. Divide rise by run.



Show solution

def slope(p1, p2):
    rise = p2[1] - p1[1]
    run = p2[0] - p1[0]
    return rise / run
For (0,0) to (2,6) the rise is 6 and the run is 2, so the slope is 6 / 2 = 3.0 — climb 3 for every 1 across.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now estimate a derivative at a point with the central difference. Write the body of numeric_derivative(f, x, h=1e-5) so it returns (f(x+h) - f(x-h)) / (2h). We test it on straight lines and a constant, where the slope is exact.









If you’re stuck

The rise is f(x + h) - f(x - h) and the run between those two points is 2 * h. Divide the rise by the run: (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2 * h).



Show solution

def numeric_derivative(f, x, h=1e-5):
    return (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2 * h)
The two sample points are x - h and x + h, a run of 2h apart. For the line 2x + 1 the rise across that run is 2 * (2h), so the division returns 2.0 regardless of h. A constant function never rises, so its slope is 0.0.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Use numeric_derivative on the curve f(x) = x * x at a couple of points and compare each estimate to the exact slope 2x. Print the point, the estimate, and 2x so you can see how close they are.
Rubric — a good answer:

Defines f(x) = x * x and reuses your numeric_derivative.
Estimates the slope at at least two different x values (e.g. x = 1 and x = 3).
Compares each estimate to 2 * x and notes that they are close (the estimate is not exact on a curve, unlike on a line).



Show one possible answer

def numeric_derivative(f, x, h=1e-5):
    return (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2 * h)

def f(x):
    return x * x

for x in (1.0, 3.0):
    estimate = numeric_derivative(f, x)
    exact = 2 * x
    print(f"x={x}: estimate {round(estimate, 6)} vs 2x = {exact}")
At x = 1 the estimate sits next to 2, and at x = 3 next to 6. On a curve the central difference is an approximation — very close for a tiny h, but not exact the way it is on a straight line.

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
text: Check yourself

title: Derivatives = sensitivity
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now read a derivative two ways: as sensitivity (how hard the output reacts to a nudge) and as a local slope (rise over run, zoomed in). That single number tells a model how a change to one input would move its output. Next, in Ch7 — Gradients, we collect a derivative for every input at once into one vector, the gradient, that points in the direction of steepest change — the compass a model follows downhill when it learns.

title: The chain rule
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: The chain rule
text:
The hook
Picture a pair of gears. You turn the first gear; it drives a second gear; the second gear moves the hand of a dial. If the first gear turns the second twice as fast, and the second turns the dial three times as fast, then turning your hand moves the dial six times as fast — 2 × 3. The two ratios multiply.
That is the whole of the chain rule. A model rarely does one thing to an input; it does a step, then a step on top of that — a composition f(g(x)): first the inner step g, then the outer step f on its result. To find how sensitive the final output is to a nudge in x, you multiply the local slopes:
(f ∘ g)'(x) = f'(g(x)) * g'(x) — the outer slope times the inner slope.
A small change in x is scaled by the inner step’s slope, and that change is scaled again by the outer step’s slope. Two scalings in a row multiply. This single move — ripple a nudge through composed steps by multiplying each local slope — is the engine of backpropagation, the algorithm that trains every neural network.

You’ll need from before: a derivative read as a local slope / sensitivity (M2 Ch6 — nudge the input, see how fast the output responds) and the idea that a slope is the number a model follows to improve (M2 Ch7 — gradients). That’s all we carry in.

title: The chain rule
section: A two-step chain: predict, then measure the error
text:
A two-step chain: predict, then measure the error
Here is the two-step pipeline a model lives in. Step 1 (inner): turn an input x into a prediction. Step 2 (outer): turn that prediction into an error. To learn, the model needs to know how the error responds to a nudge in x — but x only touches the error through the prediction. The chain rule stitches the two steps together.
Take a concrete, exact case. Let the inner step double its input,
u = g(x) = 2 * x, so the inner slope is g'(x) = 2 (every step in x moves u twice as far).
Let the outer step square its input,
y = f(u) = u * u, so the outer slope is f'(u) = 2 * u (from Ch6: the slope of u² is 2u).
The chain rule multiplies them, evaluating the outer slope at the inner value u:
dy/dx = f'(u) * g'(x) = (2 * u) * 2 = 4 * u = 8 * x.
Read it as numbers. At x = 3 the inner step gives u = 6, so the outer slope is 2 * 6 = 12 and the inner slope is 2; their product is 12 * 2 = 24. A tiny nudge to x near 3 moves y about 24 times as far. The composed slope is never one of the two slopes alone — it is the two multiplied.
The same arithmetic, stripped to its core, is a one-line helper:
chain(outer_slope, inner_slope) = outer_slope * inner_slope
so chain(2, 3) = 6, chain(5, 0) = 0 (a flat outer step kills the whole signal — a zero anywhere in the product zeroes it), and chain(-1, 4) = -4 (a downhill outer step flips the sign). That tiny multiply, repeated through every step of a deep network, is backpropagation.






Note🧱 For Java developers





Think of a composition as a Function pipeline: x -> g -> u -> f -> y, the same shape as g.andThen(f). Each stage has a local gain — its derivative — saying how much it amplifies a small wobble passing through it. The chain rule says the pipeline’s total gain is the product of the per-stage gains, exactly like multiplying conversion factors down a unit chain (m/s × s = m).
This is precisely what reverse-mode automatic differentiation (autodiff) — the “backward pass” / backpropagation in PyTorch’s autograd or JAX’s grad — does. The forward pass records each operation and its local slope; the backward pass walks that record in reverse, multiplying the local slopes together to get the derivative of the final output with respect to each input. No symbolic calculus at runtime, no hand-derived formulas: a stack of recorded steps, multiplied back to front.

title: The chain rule
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s see the chain rule produce the right slope. We plot the composed function y = (2x)² and, at x = 1, draw the straight tangent line whose slope we get by multiplying the two local slopes. At x = 1 the inner step gives u = 2, so the inner slope is 2 and the outer slope is 2u = 4; their product is 4 * 2 = 8, which is 8 * x at x = 1. As a cross-check, the cell also prints the central-difference derivative from Ch6 — built with no calculus rules — and the two agree.






Read the picture: the orange tangent rises 8 units for every 1 across, and it hugs the curve’s direction right at the touch-point — so 8 is genuinely the slope of (2x)² at x = 1. We did not differentiate the squared expression directly; we multiplied the inner slope 2 by the outer slope 4. The printed central difference lands on the same 8, confirming the product is the true sensitivity. Multiply the local slopes, and the composition’s slope falls out.

title: The chain rule
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

The chain rule finds the derivative of a composition f(g(x)) by multiplying the outer derivative by the inner derivative: (f ∘ g)'(x) = f'(g(x)) * g'(x).
It works because a nudge is scaled twice — once by the inner slope, once by the outer slope — and two scalings in a row multiply.
Concretely, for u = 2x (slope 2) and y = u² (slope 2u), dy/dx = (2u) * 2 = 8x; at x = 3, u = 6 and dy/dx = 24.
This multiplying of local slopes through composed steps is the engine of backpropagation — how real networks learn.

title: The chain rule
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
The chain rule multiplies slopes. Complete chain(outer_slope, inner_slope) so it returns their product. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The chain rule is a single multiplication — the outer step’s slope times the inner step’s slope: outer_slope * inner_slope.



Show solution

def chain(outer_slope, inner_slope):
    return outer_slope * inner_slope
chain(2, 3) is 2 * 3 = 6. A 0 anywhere in the product gives 0 — a flat step blocks the signal — and a negative slope flips the sign, so chain(-1, 4) = -4.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now use the chain rule on a real composition. For y = (2x)² the inner step is u = 2x (slope 2) and the outer step is u² (slope 2u), so dydx = (2 * (2 * x)) * 2 = 8 * x. Write the body of dydx(x). Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The inner value is u = 2 * x. The outer slope is 2 * u, the inner slope is 2, and the chain rule multiplies them: (2 * (2 * x)) * 2. That is the same as 8 * x.



Show solution

def dydx(x):
    return (2 * (2 * x)) * 2
At x = 3 the inner step gives u = 6, the outer slope is 2 * 6 = 12, and the inner slope is 2, so dydx = 12 * 2 = 24. The expression equals 8 * x, so it is 0 at x = 0 and -16 at x = -2.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Check your chain-rule dydx against the central-difference derivative from Ch6, applied to (2x)² at a point. Print the chain-rule slope, the numeric slope, and confirm they are close.
Rubric — a good answer:

Defines the composition y = (2 * x) ** 2 (or inner then outer) and reuses your chain-rule dydx.
Estimates the slope at a chosen x with a central difference (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2 * h) for a tiny h.
Prints both numbers and notes they agree (the chain rule matches the numeric slope).



Show one possible answer

def composed(x):
    return (2 * x) ** 2

def dydx(x):
    return (2 * (2 * x)) * 2          # chain rule: 8 * x

def numeric_derivative(f, x, h=1e-5):
    return (f(x + h) - f(x - h)) / (2 * h)

for x in (3.0, -2.0):
    chain_slope = dydx(x)
    numeric_slope = numeric_derivative(composed, x)
    print(f"x={x}: chain {chain_slope} vs numeric {round(numeric_slope, 6)}")
At x = 3 both land on 24, and at x = -2 both land on -16. The chain rule (multiply the local slopes) and the hand-cranked numeric derivative agree — the product really is the composition’s sensitivity.

title: The chain rule
text: Check yourself

title: The chain rule
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now differentiate a composition by multiplying local slopes — the chain rule, (f ∘ g)'(x) = f'(g(x)) * g'(x). Stacked across many steps, that single multiply is backpropagation: a nudge ripples through composed functions, scaled by each step’s slope, so a network can trace its error back to every weight. That closes the calculus thread of this module. Next we switch gears to the third pillar — Ch9 — Probability basics — where we start reasoning about uncertain data: chance, events, and how likely things are.

title: Distributions
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Distributions
text:
The hook
Roll a fair die many times and write down the results. The 1s, 2s, 3s, 4s, 5s, and 6s come up in roughly equal piles — no number is favoured. Now measure the height of many people instead. Most cluster near the average, a few are unusually short or tall, and the extremes are rare. Both are random, but the shape of the randomness is different: the die is flat, the heights are a hump in the middle.
That shape is a distribution — a description of which values are common and which are rare. It is one of the most useful ideas in this whole course, because the numbers a model touches (pixel values, word counts, weights, errors) all have a distribution, and knowing the shape tells you what to expect. In this chapter you meet two shapes — the flat uniform and the bell-shaped normal — and you learn the one move that makes any distribution visible: draw a pile of samples and plot a histogram.

You’ll need from before: probability as the long-run fraction of times an outcome happens, between 0 and 1 (M2 Ch9). A distribution is that idea spread across every possible value at once.

title: Distributions
section: A distribution is the shape of randomness
text:
A distribution is the shape of randomness
A random quantity can land on many values. Its distribution answers, for the whole range at once: how likely is each value? Two shapes cover most of what you need early on.

Uniform — every outcome is equally likely. A fair six-sided die is uniform over 1..6: each face has probability 1/6. Drawn as a picture, a uniform distribution is flat — no value is preferred over another.
Normal (also called Gaussian, or informally a bell curve) — values cluster near a centre and grow rarer as you move away in either direction. Heights, measurement errors, and the average of many small effects tend to look normal. Drawn as a picture, it is a symmetric bell: tall in the middle, thin tails at the edges.

The contrast is the whole lesson. Uniform says “anything in range, equally.” Normal says “probably near the middle, occasionally far out.” Most real-world measurements are closer to the bell than to the flat line.

Seeing a shape: draw samples, then a histogram
You rarely get handed a distribution’s formula. Instead you get samples — actual draws — and you reconstruct the shape from them. The tool for that is a histogram: chop the value range into equal bins (buckets), then count how many samples fall in each bin and draw a bar of that height. Common values build tall bars; rare values build short ones. With enough samples, the bars trace the underlying shape — flat for uniform, a bell for normal.
Two practical notes carry through the rest of the module:

More samples, clearer shape. Ten draws look noisy; a thousand draws settle into the true shape. A histogram is an estimate, and the estimate sharpens as samples grow.
Fix the seed for reproducibility. Random draws differ every run, which makes results hard to compare or debug. Seeding the random generator (here, np.random.default_rng(0)) makes the “random” sequence the same every time, so your plot and your numbers are repeatable.







Note🧱 For Java developers





Sampling with a seed is the pattern you know from java.util.Random. In Java you write new Random(0) and every run replays the same sequence; in NumPy you write np.random.default_rng(0) and get the same guarantee — a seeded generator object you ask for draws. The difference is that default_rng hands you a whole array in one call (rng.normal(size=1000)) instead of looping nextGaussian() a thousand times, and it uses a modern, higher-quality generator than the legacy linear congruential one behind java.util.Random.
A histogram is a Map<Bucket, Integer> you have built by hand many times: walk the values, decide which bucket each falls in, increment that bucket’s count. NumPy and Matplotlib do the bucketing and counting for you, but the mental model is the same — counts per bucket.
One reason the normal distribution shows up everywhere is the central limit idea: add up (or average) many small independent random effects and the total tends toward a bell shape, almost regardless of what each individual effect looked like. Sensor noise, rounding errors, the sum of many tiny influences — they pile up into a normal. It is worth filing away now; it explains why the bell reappears across the rest of this course.

title: Distributions
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s make a bell appear from nothing but random draws. We seed a generator for reproducibility, pull 1000 samples from a normal distribution with rng.normal, and drop them into a histogram. Watch the middle bins tower over the edges.
