The takeaway is the silhouette of the bars: the tallest bins sit near 0, the middle of the distribution, and the bars shrink smoothly toward both edges, where values are rare. The printed mean lands close to 0 even though no single draw was asked to, and the most extreme draws sit a few units out on each side — the thin tails. That bell is the normal distribution made visible, built from samples alone. Re-run it and, because the seed is fixed, you get the very same bell.

title: Distributions
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A distribution is the shape that randomness takes — which values are common and which are rare across all possible outcomes.
A uniform distribution makes every outcome equally likely (a flat shape, like a fair die); a normal distribution clusters values near the middle and thins at the edges (a bell).
You see a distribution by drawing many samples and plotting a histogram — counts per bin — and the shape sharpens as samples grow.
Fix the random seed (np.random.default_rng(0)) so the draws, the plot, and the numbers are reproducible.

title: Distributions
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
A histogram bar’s height is a count, but to compare bins you often want a fraction: what share of all samples landed in this bin? Complete relative_frequency(count, total) so it divides the bin’s count by the total number of samples. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

A fraction is one division: the part over the whole, count / total. With 250 of 1000 samples in a bin, that is 250 / 1000 = 0.25.



Show solution

def relative_frequency(count, total):
    return count / total
count / total turns a raw bar height into a share of the whole. A bin holding 250 of 1000 samples has relative frequency 0.25; an empty bin (0 count) has 0.0. These fractions are what let you compare histograms with different sample sizes.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
To build a histogram bar by hand, you count how many values land inside a bin’s range. Write in_range(values, low, high) that returns how many entries of values fall within [low, high] inclusive of both ends. Use a loop or a sum with a condition. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

A value is inside the bin when it is at least low and at most high. Python lets you chain that into one comparison: low <= v <= high. The <= on both sides makes the ends inclusive.



Show solution

def in_range(values, low, high):
    count = 0
    for v in values:
        if low <= v <= high:
            count += 1
    return count
low <= v <= high is true exactly when v sits inside the bin, ends included. For [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] with low=2, high=4 it counts 2, 3, 4 → 3. When the range is a single point like [0, 0], every 0 still counts because both <= are inclusive, so [0, 0, 0] gives 3. (The same logic, vectorised, is what np.histogram runs for every bin at once.)



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Draw many samples from a uniform distribution with a fixed seed (rng = np.random.default_rng(0), then rng.uniform(size=...) or rng.integers(1, 7, size=...) for a die), plot a histogram, and describe in words how its flat shape differs from the normal’s bell you saw in the worked example.
Rubric — a good answer:

Seeds a generator and draws a sizable sample (say 1000+) from a uniform distribution, then plots a histogram with plt.hist.
Notes that the bars are roughly level across the range — every value about equally likely — instead of peaking in the middle.
Contrasts this flat shape with the normal’s bell, where the middle bins tower over the thin tails.



Show one possible answer

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)          # fixed seed → reproducible draws
samples = rng.uniform(size=2000)        # 2000 draws, each equally likely in [0, 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.hist(samples, bins=20, color="tab:orange", edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("value")
ax.set_ylabel("count (samples per bin)")
ax.set_title("2000 uniform samples → a flat shape")
plt.show()

print("min:", round(float(samples.min()), 3), "max:", round(float(samples.max()), 3))
The bars come out roughly level across [0, 1): with every value equally likely, no bin is favoured, so the histogram is a flat plateau (jagged only from sampling noise, which smooths out as samples grow). That is the visual opposite of the normal’s bell, where the central bins tower over the thin tails. Same plotting move, a different underlying shape — which is the point of a distribution.

title: Distributions
text: Check yourself

title: Distributions
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now name the shape of randomness and reveal it by sampling. But a picture is hard to compare or compute with — you want a few numbers that summarise a distribution: where its centre sits and how spread out it is. Next we meet exactly those — mean, variance & standard deviation (Ch11) — the handful of statistics that turn a whole bell into three tidy numbers.

title: Bayes, intuitively
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Bayes, intuitively
text:
The hook
You take a test for a rare disease and it comes back positive. The test is described as “99% accurate.” It feels like terrible news — your stomach drops.
But here is the surprising part: with the numbers we’ll use below, the chance you actually have the disease is about 1 in 6 — roughly 17%. You are far more likely to be fine than sick. The test did not lie, and you did not misread it. The answer is counter-intuitive because our gut ignores one thing the math refuses to ignore: how rare the disease was in the first place.
This chapter is about that correction. The tool is Bayes’ rule — a way to update a belief when evidence arrives. We’ll build it with counts of people you can picture before we write a single symbol.

You’ll need from before: the idea of a probability as a fraction between 0 and 1, and reading a conditional like “the chance of a positive given you are sick” (M2 Ch9).

title: Bayes, intuitively
section: Start with a belief, then update it
text:
Start with a belief, then update it
Before the test, what did you believe? Only that you’re a typical person. If the disease affects 1 in 100 people, your honest starting guess is “about a 1% chance.” That starting belief has a name: the prior.
Then evidence arrives — a positive test. Bayes’ rule is the recipe for combining the two: it takes your prior belief and the evidence, and returns an updated belief called the posterior — your confidence after seeing the data.
That’s the whole shape of it:

prior belief + evidence → posterior belief

The mistake our gut makes is throwing the prior away. It hears “99% accurate” and jumps straight to “99% chance I’m sick.” Bayes keeps the prior in the picture, and the prior is doing most of the work here.

title: Bayes, intuitively
text:
Picture 10,000 people, not formulas
The clearest way to see Bayes is to stop thinking in percentages and start counting actual people. This is called working in natural frequencies, and it turns a slippery probability puzzle into simple arithmetic.
Here are our numbers, which we’ll keep for the rest of the chapter:

The disease affects 1% of people (the prevalence, or base rate, is 0.01).
The test is 99% sensitive: if you truly are sick, it flags you 99% of the time.
The test has a 5% false-positive rate: if you are healthy, it still flags you 5% of the time.

Now imagine a town of 10,000 people and walk the numbers through:

Sick people: 1% of 10,000 = 100 people are actually sick. The test catches 99% of them, so it flags 99 (these are the true positives). One sick person slips through.
Healthy people: the other 9,900 are healthy. The test wrongly flags 5% of them: 5% of 9,900 = 495 (these are the false positives).
Everyone who got a positive result: 99 + 495 = 594 people.

So 594 people are staring at a positive test. How many are actually sick? Only 99 of them. The share that is truly sick is:
\[
\frac{99}{594} = 0.1667 \;\;(\text{about } 16.7\%).
\]
The other 495 — the large majority — are healthy people with a false alarm. Notice what drove the result: the healthy group is 99 times larger than the sick group, so even a small 5% slip-up on that huge group (495 people) easily outnumbers the 99 true cases. The base rate, not the test’s accuracy, is the lever.

The picture: false positives swamp the true ones
The plot below draws the two groups of positive results side by side. The false-positive bar (495) towers over the true-positive bar (99) — it is about five times taller. That single image is the whole lesson: among everyone who tested positive, the false alarms dominate, which is exactly why a positive result on a rare-disease test usually means you’re fine.






If you only remember one thing, remember the shape of those two bars: the tall red bar of false alarms is why “positive” is not the same as “sick.”

title: Bayes, intuitively
text:
The same thing, in symbols
Now the symbols are labels for things you already counted. Bayes’ rule is:
\[
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\,\cdot\,P(A)}{P(B)}.
\]
Read it with our example, where \(A\) = “you are sick” and \(B\) = “you tested positive”:

\(P(A)\) — the prior, the base rate of being sick: 0.01. (In counts: 100 of 10,000.)
\(P(B \mid A)\) — the chance of a positive given you are sick, the sensitivity: 0.99. (In counts: 99 of the 100 sick people.)
\(P(B)\) — the chance of a positive result at all, across everyone: the 594 of 10,000 who tested positive, so 0.0594.
\(P(A \mid B)\) — the posterior, the chance you are sick given the positive result. That’s what we want.

Putting the numbers in:
\[
P(\text{sick} \mid +) = \frac{0.99 \times 0.01}{0.0594}
= \frac{0.0099}{0.0594} = 0.1667.
\]
Same 16.7%. The numerator \(0.99 \times 0.01 = 0.0099\) is the true-positive share (the 99 people), and the denominator \(0.0594\) is everyone who tested positive (the 594). The formula is the same division you did by counting people — 99 / 594 — written with probabilities instead of headcounts. Counting first, symbols second.
One more useful form spells out the denominator, since \(P(B)\) is “true positives plus false positives”:
\[
P(B) = \underbrace{P(B \mid A)\,P(A)}_{\text{true positives}}
\;+\; \underbrace{P(B \mid \text{not }A)\,P(\text{not }A)}_{\text{false positives}}
= (0.99 \times 0.01) + (0.05 \times 0.99).
\]
That’s \(0.0099 + 0.0495 = 0.0594\) — the 99 and the 495, back again.

title: Bayes, intuitively
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Bayes updates a belief. Start from a prior, see evidence, land on a posterior — your confidence after the data.
The base rate matters. How common something is to begin with sets how many true cases there are to find. Ignoring it is the classic mistake.
Rare means false alarms dominate. When a disease is rare, the large healthy group produces more false positives than there are true cases — so most positive tests are false.
Count people first. Natural frequencies (99 vs 495 out of 10,000) make Bayes concrete; the formula \(P(A \mid B) = P(B \mid A)P(A) / P(B)\) is the same division written in symbols.

title: Bayes, intuitively
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Given the counts of true positives and false positives, what share of all positive results is truly sick? Complete posterior. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The total number of positive results is true_pos + false_pos. The share that is truly sick is the true positives divided by that total.



Show solution

def posterior(true_pos, false_pos):
    return true_pos / (true_pos + false_pos)
With 99 true and 495 false, that’s 99 / 594 = 0.1667. When the two counts are equal (50 and 50), the share is exactly half.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now compute the posterior straight from the three inputs, without counting people. Write bayes(prevalence, sensitivity, false_positive_rate) using Bayes’ rule with the spelled-out denominator (true positives plus false positives).









If you’re stuck

You already have the two pieces: true_pos is the true-positive share and false_pos is the false-positive share. The posterior is the true positives divided by all positives — the same true / (true + false) pattern as Exercise 1.



Show solution

def bayes(prevalence, sensitivity, false_positive_rate):
    true_pos = prevalence * sensitivity
    false_pos = (1 - prevalence) * false_positive_rate
    return true_pos / (true_pos + false_pos)
For the rare disease: true_pos = 0.01 * 0.99 = 0.0099, false_pos = 0.99 * 0.05 = 0.0495, so 0.0099 / 0.0594 = 0.1667. When prevalence rises to 0.5 the same test gives 0.9519 — the base rate, not the test, moved the answer.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Hold the test fixed (sensitivity = 0.99, false_positive_rate = 0.05) and call bayes for several prevalences — try 0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5. Print each prevalence next to its posterior, and write one sentence on what you notice.
Rubric — a good answer:

Calls bayes for at least three different prevalences with the test held fixed.
Prints each prevalence alongside its posterior in a readable way.
States the pattern: as the base rate rises, the posterior rises — a positive test means much more when the disease is common.



Show one possible answer

def bayes(prevalence, sensitivity, false_positive_rate):
    true_pos = prevalence * sensitivity
    false_pos = (1 - prevalence) * false_positive_rate
    return true_pos / (true_pos + false_pos)

for prevalence in (0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5):
    post = bayes(prevalence, 0.99, 0.05)
    print(f"prevalence {prevalence}: posterior {round(post, 4)}")
The posterior climbs from about 0.0194 at a prevalence of 0.001 up to 0.9519 at 0.5. The very same test swings from “almost certainly a false alarm” to “almost certainly real” — driven entirely by how common the disease is.

title: Bayes, intuitively
text: Check yourself

title: Bayes, intuitively
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You’ve met the one rule that turns evidence into an updated belief — and seen why the base rate quietly controls the answer. This way of thinking, prior to posterior, underpins spam filters, medical screening, and the probabilistic models later in the course. Next we’ll keep building the statistical toolkit you’ll carry into the machine-learning modules.

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text:
The hook
Suppose you want the average height of every adult in a country — all 1.4 billion of them. You cannot line them up and measure each one. So you do what every pollster, scientist, and data team does instead: you measure a few thousand people and use their average as your best guess for everyone.
Now here is the catch that this whole chapter is about. Ask a friend to go measure a different few thousand people. Their average will not match yours exactly — it will be close, but a little off. Measure a third group and you get a third answer. None of them is “wrong”; each is an honest estimate from a different handful of people. That wobble between samples is the uncertainty in your number, and the good news is that you can measure it — and shrink it — without ever counting the whole population.

You’ll need from before: the mean (the average) and the standard deviation (how spread out the values are) from M2 Ch11. We build directly on those two numbers.

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text:
Population vs sample
Two words do a lot of work here, so let’s pin them down.

Population — everyone or everything you actually care about: all adults in the country, every email a spam filter will ever see, all the customers a shop could have. The population mean (the true average) is the number you wish you knew.
Sample — the subset you can realistically collect: the 2,000 people you measured, the 500 emails you labelled. It is a stand-in for the population.

You almost never get to measure the whole population — it is too big, too expensive, or does not even exist yet (future customers). So you measure a sample and compute its mean. That sample mean is your estimate of the population mean. The entire craft of statistics grows out of one honest question: how good is that estimate?

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text:
Different samples give different answers
Imagine the true population average height is exactly 165 cm (a number nature knows but you don’t). You draw a sample of 50 people and their average comes out to 164.2 cm. A colleague draws another 50 and gets 166.1 cm. A third sample gives 165.4 cm.
Each sample is a different random handful, so each mean lands in a slightly different spot. This is sampling variability: the sample mean is itself a random quantity that changes from sample to sample. It is not a mistake or sloppy measurement — it is unavoidable, and it is exactly what “uncertainty” means here. Your single estimate is one draw from a cloud of estimates you could have gotten.
The encouraging part: that cloud has a shape. The sample means cluster around the true population mean, most of them near it and few far away — the bell from Ch10 showing up again. So while any one sample can miss, the estimates are centred on the right answer, and we can describe how tightly they cluster.

title: Sampling & uncertainty
section: The standard error: measuring the wobble
text:
The standard error: measuring the wobble
How wide is that cloud of sample means? The number that answers this is the standard error — the standard deviation of the estimate itself. (We call it the standard error to distinguish it from the standard deviation of the raw data; it is the typical size of the gap between your sample mean and the truth.)
There is a clean formula for it. If your data has standard deviation std and your sample has n values, then:
\[
\text{standard error} = \frac{\text{std}}{\sqrt{n}}
\]
Two pieces drive it, and both make intuitive sense:

More spread → more wobble. If the underlying values vary wildly (large std), any sample of them bounces around more, so estimates from different samples are further apart. The std is on top.
Bigger sample → less wobble. A larger n averages out the lucky-high and lucky-low values within each sample, so estimates settle down. The n is on the bottom — under a square root.

That square root is the key detail. To halve your uncertainty you do not need twice the data — you need four times as much, because sqrt(4) = 2. Go from 100 samples to 400 and the standard error halves; go to 900 and it shrinks to a third. This diminishing return is why polls of a few thousand people are “good enough”: past a point, more data buys you only a little more certainty.
This tightening-up as the sample grows has a name: the law of large numbers. It says the sample mean converges to the population mean as n grows. The standard error is the quantitative version of that promise — it tells you how fast the estimate settles.






Note🧱 For Java developers





If you have ever load-tested a service, you already know sampling variability. Run a benchmark for 10 requests and the average latency jumps around between runs; run it for 10,000 and the average barely moves from run to run. You were watching std / sqrt(n) shrink the whole time — more requests, a tighter estimate of the “true” mean latency. Reporting a p50 with a confidence band instead of a single number is the same instinct this chapter formalises: never quote an estimate without some sense of how much it could have wobbled.

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text:
Let’s build it (worked example)
Let’s see sampling variability. We invent a population with a known mean, then draw many samples from it, compute each sample’s mean, and plot a histogram of those means. We seed the generator (np.random.default_rng(0)) so the draws are reproducible. This cell is a demonstration, not a graded check, so randomness is fine here — but it is seeded, so you get the same picture every run.






The takeaway is in the shape of the bars: every sample produced a different mean (that is sampling variability), yet the means pile up in a narrow bell centred on the true mean of 50 (the red line). Most samples land close to the truth and few stray far — the cloud has a centre and a width. And notice the two printed spreads agree: the measured spread of the 500 sample means matches the formula std / sqrt(n) = 10 / sqrt(40) ≈ 1.58. That is the standard error — the width of the cloud — predicted from a single sample’s spread and size.

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

You estimate an unmeasurable population mean from a sample you can collect: the sample mean is your best guess for the population mean.
Different samples give different answers — sampling variability — and that wobble is the uncertainty in your estimate.
The standard error measures the wobble: standard error = std / sqrt(n) — the spread of the data divided by the square root of the sample size.
Because n sits under a square root, a bigger sample shrinks the standard error: to halve the uncertainty you need four times the data (the law of large numbers in action).

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
The sample mean is your estimate of the population mean, so start there. Complete sample_mean(sample) so it returns the average of the values. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

The average is one division: the total over the count. In Python that is sum(sample) / len(sample).



Show solution

def sample_mean(sample):
    return sum(sample) / len(sample)
sum(sample) adds the values and len(sample) counts them, so their ratio is the average. For [2, 4, 6] that is 12 / 3 = 4.0 — your single-number estimate of the population mean.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now measure the uncertainty. Write standard_error(sample) that returns the population standard deviation of the sample divided by the square root of its size — the formula std / sqrt(n). Use np.std (its default ddof=0 gives the population standard deviation) and np.sqrt. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

You need two NumPy calls. np.std(sample) gives the standard deviation (the spread), and np.sqrt(len(sample)) gives the square root of the count. Divide the first by the second: np.std(sample) / np.sqrt(len(sample)).



Show solution

import numpy as np

def standard_error(sample):
    return np.std(sample) / np.sqrt(len(sample))
For [2, 4, 6, 8] the mean is 5; the deviations -3, -1, 1, 3 square to 9, 1, 1, 9, summing to 20, so the variance is 20 / 4 = 5 and the standard deviation is sqrt(5) ≈ 2.236. Dividing by sqrt(4) = 2 gives ≈ 1.118. When every value is identical ([5, 5, 5, 5]) there is no spread, so the standard deviation — and the standard error — is 0.0: a perfectly certain estimate.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Use a seeded generator (rng = np.random.default_rng(...)) to draw one sample of size 10 and one sample of size 100 from the same population. Print each sample’s standard error and confirm that the larger sample has the smaller one — the wobble shrinks as n grows.
Rubric — a good answer:

Seeds a generator and draws a size-10 sample and a size-100 sample from the same population (same loc/scale).
Computes and prints the standard error (np.std(sample) / np.sqrt(len(sample))) for each sample.
States that the size-100 sample has the smaller standard error, connecting it to the sqrt(n) in the denominator.



Show one possible answer

import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)            # fixed seed → reproducible draws

def standard_error(sample):
    return np.std(sample) / np.sqrt(len(sample))

small = rng.normal(loc=50, scale=10, size=10)
large = rng.normal(loc=50, scale=10, size=100)

print("n=10  standard error:", round(float(standard_error(small)), 3))
print("n=100 standard error:", round(float(standard_error(large)), 3))
print("smaller sample is more uncertain:",
      standard_error(small) > standard_error(large))
The size-100 sample reports a noticeably smaller standard error than the size-10 one. Both samples come from the same population, so the difference is purely the sqrt(n) in the denominator: ten times the data divides the spread by sqrt(10) ≈ 3.16, tightening the estimate. More data, less wobble — the law of large numbers made concrete.

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text: Check yourself

title: Sampling & uncertainty
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now turn a sample into an estimate and state how uncertain that estimate is — the standard error, shrinking as the square root of the sample size. That is the last statistics idea this module needs. Next, M2 closes by putting the pieces together: we leave probability behind and head into the NumPy linear algebra that assembles everything you’ve built into a single working neuron.

title: The math of one neuron
text: Intermediate Browser Stable
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
text:
The hook
This is the chapter the whole module has been building toward. Every piece you’ve met — a dot product, a derivative, a gradient, the chain rule — clicks together here into the smallest learning machine there is: one neuron. Deep learning stacks this exact unit by the million. Understand one, and the rest is repetition.
A neuron does two small things. It takes a row of numbers and mixes them into a single score (a weighted sum). Then it bends that score into a prediction between 0 and 1 (an activation). That’s the forward pass. Learning is the backward pass: measuring how wrong the prediction was, then using the chain rule to work out how to nudge each weight to be a little less wrong next time.
We’ll do both passes by hand, in code you can read line by line, and then prove our hand-derived gradient is correct by checking it against a numeric estimate.

You’ll need from before: the dot product (Ch5), the derivative as sensitivity (Ch6), the gradient (Ch7), the chain rule (Ch8), and NumPy arrays (Ch15).
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
section: The forward pass: from inputs to a prediction
text:
The forward pass: from inputs to a prediction
A neuron holds two things it can adjust: a weight for each input (w) and a single bias (b). Given an input vector x, it computes:

The weighted sum z = w · x + b. This is the dot product from Chapter 5 — a similarity score between the weights and the input — shifted by the bias.
The activation a = σ(z), where σ (sigmoid) is 1 / (1 + e^(−z)). It squashes any real number into the range 0 to 1, so the output reads like a probability.

Let’s use one concrete example all the way through. Input x = [2, 3], weights w = [0.5, −1.0], bias b = 0.5, and the true answer we wanted is y = 1.0.






Running it: z = 0.5·2 + (−1.0)·3 + 0.5 = −1.5, the sigmoid squashes that to a ≈ 0.182426, and since we wanted y = 1.0 the squared-error loss is (0.182426 − 1.0)² ≈ 0.668428. The prediction is far too low — so the neuron has something to learn.
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
section: The loss: a number that says how wrong we are
text:
The loss: a number that says how wrong we are
To learn, the neuron needs a single number measuring its mistake. We use squared error: L = (a − y)². It’s zero when the prediction is perfect and grows as the prediction drifts from the truth. Squaring (like in Ch11’s variance) keeps it positive and punishes big misses more. Learning means changing w and b to make L smaller — and to know which way to change them, we need the gradient of L.
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
section: The backward pass: the chain rule does the work
text:
The backward pass: the chain rule does the work
The loss depends on a, which depends on z, which depends on each weight. That’s a chain — exactly Chapter 8. To get how the loss responds to weight w_i, multiply the links:
\[\frac{\partial L}{\partial w_i} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial a} \cdot \frac{\partial a}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w_i}\]
Each link is something you already know how to differentiate:

dL/da = 2(a − y) — the derivative of the squared error.
da/dz = a(1 − a) — the derivative of the sigmoid (a tidy result worth remembering: the sigmoid’s slope is its own output times one minus itself).
dz/dw_i = x_i and dz/db = 1 — the weighted sum is linear in each weight, so its slope is the matching input itself (and 1 for the bias).

Multiply the first two once to get dL/dz, then fan it out to every weight by its input. Here’s the whole backward pass:






That gives dL/dz ≈ −0.243877, so grad_w ≈ [−0.487753, −0.731630] (each is dL/dz times its input, 2 and 3) and grad_b ≈ −0.243877. The negative signs say: to reduce the loss, increase these weights and the bias — which would push the prediction up toward the target y = 1.0, exactly what we want.
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
section: Gradient checking: proving the hand-derivation is right
text:
Gradient checking: proving the hand-derivation is right
How do we know that calculus is correct and not a slip of algebra? We check it numerically. A derivative is a slope, and we can estimate any slope without calculus by nudging the input a tiny amount eps each way and measuring the rise over run: (L(p + eps) − L(p − eps)) / (2·eps). If our analytic gradient matches this numeric estimate to many decimals, it’s right.






The two gradients agree to a maximum error of about 3.27e-11 — eleven decimal places. The hand-derived chain rule is correct. That tiny leftover is rounding in the finite-difference estimate, not a mistake in the calculus.
This is not a toy check: gradient checking is exactly how practitioners catch bugs when they implement backpropagation in a real network. One neuron or one billion, the recipe is the same — forward pass, loss, chain rule backward, verify.
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A neuron’s forward pass is a dot product plus a bias (z = w·x + b) followed by an activation (a = σ(z)) that squashes the score into 0..1.
The loss (a − y)² is one number measuring how wrong the prediction is; learning means making it smaller.
The backward pass uses the chain rule to get the gradient of the loss for every weight: dL/dw_i = 2(a − y) · a(1 − a) · x_i.
A numeric gradient check (finite differences) confirms the hand-derived gradient — here they matched to about 3e-11.
This single unit, stacked and trained, is all of deep learning. You’ve now done its math by hand.
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
The activation is the sigmoid. Complete sigmoid(z) so it returns 1 / (1 + e^(−z)). Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

e^(−z) is np.exp(-z). The formula is one divided by (1 + np.exp(-z)).



Show solution

import numpy as np

def sigmoid(z):
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-z))
At z = 0, e^0 = 1, so the value is 1 / (1 + 1) = 0.5 — the sigmoid’s midpoint. Large positive z drives it toward 1; large negative z toward 0.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now the whole forward pass. Write neuron_forward(x, w, b) that returns the activation σ(w · x + b). Use NumPy for the dot product. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

The weighted sum is the dot product of w and x plus the bias. With NumPy arrays that’s np.array(w) @ np.array(x) + b.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def neuron_forward(x, w, b):
    z = np.array(w) @ np.array(x) + b
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-z))
np.array(w) @ np.array(x) is the weighted sum; adding b shifts it; the sigmoid squashes the result. For our example z = −1.5, so the output is ≈ 0.1824.



Exercise 3 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
The key step of the backward pass. Write dL_dz(a, y) — the gradient of the squared-error loss with respect to the weighted sum z. By the chain rule this is 2(a − y) · a(1 − a). Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Two links of the chain multiplied: dL/da = 2*(a - y) and da/dz = a*(1 - a). The answer is their product, 2 * (a - y) * a * (1 - a).



Show solution

def dL_dz(a, y):
    return 2 * (a - y) * a * (1 - a)
At a = 0.5, the sigmoid slope a(1 − a) is 0.25. If the truth is y = 1.0 we under-predicted, so the gradient is negative (−0.25) — pushing the prediction up. If y = 0.0 we over-predicted, so it’s positive (+0.25) — pushing it down.



Exercise 4 — Stretch (optional, ≈10 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Put it all together: write a forward pass, derive grad_w and grad_b analytically, then gradient-check them against finite differences for an input of your choice. Print the max error.
Rubric — a good answer:

Computes the forward pass and the analytic gradient for w and b.
Estimates the same gradient numerically with (L(p+eps) − L(p−eps)) / (2·eps).
Reports the max absolute difference and confirms it is tiny (well under 1e-6).



Show one possible answer

import numpy as np

def sigmoid(z):
    return 1.0 / (1.0 + np.exp(-z))

x = np.array([1.0, -2.0, 0.5])
w = np.array([0.3, 0.8, -0.5])
b = -0.1
y = 0.0

def loss_at(w, b):
    return float((sigmoid(w @ x + b) - y) ** 2)

a = sigmoid(w @ x + b)
dL_dz = 2 * (a - y) * a * (1 - a)
analytic = np.append(dL_dz * x, dL_dz)   # grad_w then grad_b

eps = 1e-5
numeric = []
for i in range(len(w)):
    wp = w.copy(); wp[i] += eps
    wm = w.copy(); wm[i] -= eps
    numeric.append((loss_at(wp, b) - loss_at(wm, b)) / (2 * eps))
numeric.append((loss_at(w, b + eps) - loss_at(w, b - eps)) / (2 * eps))

max_error = float(np.max(np.abs(analytic - np.array(numeric))))
print("max error = {:.2e}".format(max_error))
Any reasonable input gives a max error around 1e-10 or smaller — confirmation that the hand-derived gradient is correct, on data different from the chapter’s example.
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
text: Check yourself
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: The math of one neuron
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You have now done, by hand, the exact computation that runs billions of times inside a trained network: forward pass, loss, chain-rule backward, gradient check. Stacking these neurons into layers and repeating the nudge is gradient descent — the engine of every model in this course.
That closes Module 2. You came in worried about math; you leave having built a neuron’s brain from a dot product and a derivative. Next, in Module 3 — Data & Tooling, you turn from the math to the craft: loading real datasets, exploring them, and preparing the numbers that a neuron like this one learns from.
crumbs: Ch16 — The math of one neuron

title: NumPy & vectorization
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: NumPy & vectorization
text:
The hook
Picture a checkout line where the cashier rings up one item, waits, rings up the next, waits, and so on. Now picture a scanner that prices the whole basket in one pass. A plain Python list of numbers is the first cashier: to double every value you write a loop and handle items one at a time. A NumPy array is the scanner: you say “double everything” once, and a layer of fast compiled code sweeps the whole array for you.
That move — from one element at a time to the whole array at once — is called vectorization, and it is the habit this chapter builds. The payoff is two-fold: your code runs much faster on real data, and it reads closer to the math you meant.

You’ll need from before: NumPy arrays as a container of numbers (M1 Ch12) and a little linear-algebra-in-NumPy practice (M2 Ch15). We build straight on those.

title: NumPy & vectorization
section: A list of numbers vs an array of numbers
text:
A list of numbers vs an array of numbers
A Python list is a general-purpose box. It can hold anything — numbers, strings, other lists — so each element carries its own type information, and arithmetic over the list means a loop. To double every number in a list you write:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
doubled = []
for n in nums:
    doubled.append(n * 2)
# doubled == [2, 4, 6, 8]
A NumPy array is a tighter box built for numbers: every element is the same type, laid out together in memory. That lets NumPy push the loop down into compiled code and express the same idea in one line. Two reasons to prefer it for numeric work:

Speed — the per-element loop runs in optimized C, not in the Python interpreter, so large arrays are processed far faster.
Clean syntax — arr * 2 says what you mean. The loop and the temporary list disappear, and the code looks like the formula.

Let’s see the array version side by side.






The * 2 applied to every element at once. There is no for and no growing list — NumPy did the sweep. This is the shape of almost everything that follows.

title: NumPy & vectorization
text:
Vectorized arithmetic
Vectorized operations work element-by-element across a whole array. A single number combines with every element:

arr * 2 multiplies each element by two,
arr + 10 adds ten to each element,
arr ** 2 squares each element.

And two arrays of the same shape combine position-by-position: a + b adds the first element of a to the first of b, the second to the second, and so on. This matches how vectors add in the math from M2 — now it is one line of code.






Read a * b carefully: it multiplies matching positions to give [10, 40, 90]. That is not the dot product from M2 (which would sum those into a single number); it is the plain element-wise product. NumPy keeps the two ideas separate, and you pick the one you need.

title: NumPy & vectorization
text:
Broadcasting: mixing shapes on purpose
In the first example, nums * 2 combined an array with a single number even though they are different shapes. NumPy handled it by broadcasting: it stretches the smaller operand across the larger one so the shapes line up, without ever building the stretched copy in full.
The everyday case is a scalar with an array, which is how arr + 10 adds ten to every element. A useful mental picture: NumPy treats the lone number as if it were repeated to match the array, then does the element-wise operation.






The * 9 / 5 + 32 is a chain of broadcasts: each scalar combines with the whole array in turn. One readable line converts the entire dataset — and it stays the same line whether the array holds four temperatures or four million.

title: NumPy & vectorization
text:
Aggregations: summarizing an array
Often you want one number out of an array: a total, an average, a peak. NumPy calls these reductions or aggregations, and they read as methods on the array:

arr.sum() — add up every element,
arr.mean() — the average (sum divided by count),
arr.max() and arr.min() — the largest and smallest values.







Each call collapses the five scores into one summary value. These reductions pair naturally with the arithmetic above: vectorize to transform the data, then aggregate to describe it.

title: NumPy & vectorization
section: Boolean masks: filtering without a loop
text:
Boolean masks: filtering without a loop
Here is the idea that ties the chapter together. Compare an array to a value and NumPy gives you back an array of True/False — one verdict per element. That is a boolean mask.






Indexing an array with a mask keeps only the elements where the mask is True, so arr[arr > 3] returns the values above three. No loop, no running list — the comparison and the selection are each one vectorized step.
Because True counts as 1 and False as 0, you can count matches by summing the mask. (arr > 3).sum() adds up the Trues, which is exactly how many elements pass the test:






Three elements (5, 8, and 4) clear the bar, so the count is 3. Combine a mask with a reduction and you can answer questions like “how many?” and “what’s the average of the ones that qualify?” — all without writing the loop yourself.

title: NumPy & vectorization
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A NumPy array stores numbers of one type together, so it is faster and reads cleaner than a Python list for numeric work.
Vectorization applies an operation to a whole array at once (arr * 2, a + b) — no element-by-element loop in your code.
Broadcasting stretches a smaller operand (often a single number) to fit a larger array, so a scalar combines with every element.
Reductions (.sum(), .mean(), .max()) collapse an array into a summary number.
A boolean mask like arr > 3 selects elements with arr[arr > 3] and counts them with (arr > 3).sum().

title: NumPy & vectorization
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Complete scale so it multiplies a sequence of numbers by a factor using vectorized NumPy, then returns a plain Python list. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Turn the input into an array with np.array(arr), multiply the whole thing by factor, then convert the result back with .tolist() so the return value is an ordinary list.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def scale(arr, factor):
    return (np.array(arr) * factor).tolist()
np.array(arr) * factor broadcasts the single factor across every element in one vectorized step; .tolist() hands back a plain Python list so the result matches the expected values.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write count_above so it reports how many elements of arr are strictly greater than threshold, as a plain int. Build a boolean mask and sum it. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

np.array(arr) > threshold gives a True/False mask. Summing it counts the True values because each counts as 1. Wrap the sum in int(...) so the return type is a plain Python integer.



Show solution

import numpy as np

def count_above(arr, threshold):
    return int((np.array(arr) > threshold).sum())
(np.array(arr) > threshold) builds the mask, .sum() adds up its Trues, and int(...) returns a plain integer. For [1, 5, 3, 8, 2, 4] with threshold 3, the elements 5, 8, and 4 pass, so the count is 3.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Build a NumPy array of a few numbers, compute its mean, and use a boolean mask to print only the values that sit above the mean. Print both the mean and the selected values.
Rubric — a good answer:

Creates a NumPy array and computes its mean with .mean().
Builds a boolean mask comparing the array to its mean and uses it to index the array.
Prints the mean and the above-mean values, and the printed values are all greater than the mean.



Show one possible answer

import numpy as np

arr = np.array([4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42])
avg = arr.mean()
above = arr[arr > avg]

print("mean:        ", avg)
print("above mean:  ", above)
arr > avg is a boolean mask, and arr[arr > avg] keeps only the elements that beat the average — the same masking move from the chapter, now pointed at a value you computed from the data itself.

title: NumPy & vectorization
text: Check yourself

title: NumPy & vectorization
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
Vectorization is the habit every data tool in this module rewards: think in whole arrays, not loops. You can now transform data with element-wise arithmetic and broadcasting, summarize it with reductions, and filter it with boolean masks. Next we move from a bare array of numbers to labeled, tabular data — rows and columns with names — where these same ideas reappear one level up.

title: Indexing & selection
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Indexing & selection
text:
The hook
A table is rarely interesting all at once. The questions you actually ask are small slices of it: what species is in row 0? show me the first five rows. which penguins weigh more than four kilograms? Each of those is a selection — you point at part of the table and pandas hands you back exactly that part.
In the last chapter you loaded the Palmer Penguins table and sized it up. Now you learn to reach into it. There are three moves, and almost everything you do with data is a combination of them:

Pick a column by name — df['species'].
Pick rows — by their position with .iloc, or by their label with .loc.
Filter rows with a boolean mask — keep only the rows that pass a test, like body_mass_g > 4000.

Get these three reflexes and a DataFrame stops being a wall of numbers and becomes something you can interrogate.

You’ll need from before: a DataFrame is a table of named columns, and df['col'] returns one column as a Series (M3 Ch2). We build straight on that.

title: Indexing & selection
text:
Picking a column
The simplest selection is one column, by name, in square brackets. What comes back is a Series — the labelled column you met last chapter.






df["species"] is a Series, so every Series tool carries over. A column of text is often best summarised by counting its values:






That tallies Adelie 152, Gentoo 124, Chinstrap 68. To pull several columns at once, pass a list of names inside the brackets — note the double brackets, an outer pair for indexing and an inner pair for the list. The result is a smaller DataFrame, not a Series:






One name in brackets gives a Series; a list of names gives a DataFrame. Keep that distinction in mind — it explains a lot of “why did I get a different shape?” surprises later.

title: Indexing & selection
text:
Picking rows by position with .iloc
Columns have names, but rows are easiest to reach by position: the first row, the first five rows, the last one. That is what .iloc does — i for integer position. It works like indexing a Python list, and it is 0-based, so the first row is position 0:






A single row comes back as a Series, with the column names now acting as its index. Reading down it, row 0 is an Adelie penguin seen on Torgersen Island.
To grab a range of rows, slice with a colon. .iloc[:5] means “positions 0 up to but not including 5” — the same half-open slicing Python lists use, so it returns exactly five rows:






This is exactly what df.head() gave you last chapter; .iloc makes the positions explicit, so you can also ask for df.iloc[10:13] or the last row with df.iloc[-1]. Position-based selection never cares what the row labels are — only where the rows sit.

title: Indexing & selection
text:
Picking rows by label with .loc
.loc selects by label — the value in the row index, and the column name. For the penguins table the index is the default 0, 1, 2, …, so a label often looks like a position, but it is a different idea: .loc asks “the row labelled 0”, not “the row in position 0”. You can name the column in the same call:






Two things to notice. First, df.loc[0, "species"] reaches a single cell — the species of the row labelled 0, which is Adelie. Second, df.loc[0:4, ...] returns five rows, labels 0 through 4 — .loc slices are inclusive of the end label. That is the one gotcha worth memorising:

.iloc[0:4] → positions 0, 1, 2, 3 — four rows (end excluded).
.loc[0:4] → labels 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — five rows (end included).

Use .iloc when you think in positions (“the first ten rows”) and .loc when you think in labels (“rows 0 through 4, and only these two columns”). Mixing them up is the most common early pandas bug, and naming which one you mean fixes it.

title: Indexing & selection
section: Filtering rows with a boolean mask
text:
Filtering rows with a boolean mask
The selection you’ll reach for most is the filter: keep only the rows that meet a condition. It happens in two steps. First, compare a column to a value — this runs the test on every row at once and produces a boolean mask, a Series of True/False:






The mask has one entry per row: True where the penguin weighs more than 4000 g, False otherwise. Now pass that mask back into the DataFrame in square brackets, and pandas keeps only the True rows:






Out of 344 penguins, 172 weigh more than 4000 g. You can read df[df["body_mass_g"] > 4000] as a single sentence: “from df, take the rows where body_mass_g is over 4000.” Filtering with == works the same way for text — this is how you isolate one species:






That filter keeps 124 Gentoo penguins, and selecting the body_mass_g column of that subset and averaging it gives a mean of about 5076.02 g — Gentoos are the heavyweights of this dataset, well above the 4201.75 g average across all three species. Combining a mask (to choose rows) with a column selection (to choose what to measure) is the core loop of exploratory data analysis: narrow the rows, then read a number off them.

title: Indexing & selection
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

df['col'] selects one column as a Series; df[['a', 'b']] (a list of names) selects several columns as a DataFrame.
.iloc selects by integer position (0-based, end-exclusive slices, like Python lists); .loc selects by label (and its slices are inclusive of the end label).
A boolean mask — df['col'] > value — is a Series of True/False; df[mask] keeps only the rows that are True.
Narrow the rows with a mask, then read a column off the subset — for penguins, df[df['species'] == 'Gentoo'] keeps 124 rows.

title: Indexing & selection
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made data so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Build a DataFrame from a dictionary, take its first n rows with .head(n), and return how many rows that is as an int. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Build the table with pd.DataFrame(data), take the first rows with .head(n), then read the row count from .shape[0] (the first item of the (rows, columns) tuple). Wrap it in int(...) so the return type is a plain integer.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def first_n(data, n):
    return int(pd.DataFrame(data).head(n).shape[0])
pd.DataFrame(data) builds the table, .head(n) keeps the first n rows, and .shape[0] is the number of rows that remain. Asking for head(0) keeps no rows, so the count is 0.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Write count_above(data, col, threshold): build a DataFrame, then return how many rows have col greater than threshold. This is the boolean-mask pattern — compare a column, then count the True values. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

pd.DataFrame(data)[col] is the column as a Series. Comparing it with > threshold gives a boolean mask (True/False per row). Calling .sum() on a boolean Series counts the True values, because True counts as 1; wrap that in int(...).



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def count_above(data, col, threshold):
    return int((pd.DataFrame(data)[col] > threshold).sum())
(pd.DataFrame(data)[col] > threshold) is the boolean mask; .sum() adds up its True values (each worth 1). With threshold 2 the values 3 and 4 pass, so the count is 2; with threshold 9 none pass, so it is 0.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset, filter it down to the Gentoo penguins with a boolean mask, and report two facts in your own print statements: how many Gentoo penguins there are (124) and their mean body mass (about 5076.02 g).
Rubric — a good answer:

Loads penguins with load_csv and builds a mask df["species"] == "Gentoo".
Filters with df[mask] and prints the row count (124) read from the data.
Selects body_mass_g on that subset and prints its mean (≈ 5076.02 g).



If you’re stuck

Build the mask df["species"] == "Gentoo", then df[mask] is the Gentoo-only table. Its .shape[0] is the count; selecting ["body_mass_g"] on that subset and calling .mean() gives the average mass.



Show one possible answer

from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")
gentoo = df[df["species"] == "Gentoo"]

print("Gentoo penguins:", gentoo.shape[0])
print("mean body mass (g):", round(gentoo["body_mass_g"].mean(), 2))
This prints 124 Gentoo penguins and a mean body mass of 5076.02 g — both read straight from the filtered subset rather than typed in by hand.

title: Indexing & selection
text: Check yourself

title: Indexing & selection
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now reach into a table three ways: pick columns by name, pick rows by position (.iloc) or label (.loc), and filter rows with a boolean mask. Those moves combine into nearly every question you’ll ask of data. Next we go from selecting what’s already there to cleaning it — handling the missing values that .describe() quietly skipped, so your selections and summaries rest on solid ground.

title: Joins & groupby
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: Joins & groupby
text:
The hook
You have a table of 344 penguins, and someone asks a question every analyst hears on day one: “What’s the average body mass for each species?” You could filter to Adelie, take the mean, write it down; filter to Chinstrap, take the mean, write it down; then Gentoo. Three passes, three numbers, copied by hand. With thirty species it would be a miserable afternoon.
Pandas has one tool that does the whole sweep in a single line: groupby. It splits the table into one mini-table per species, summarises each one, and stacks the answers back together — three numbers, no copying. And when the answer to a question lives in two tables instead of one, merge stitches them together on a shared column so you can read across both at once.
This chapter covers both: grouping a table to summarise it, and joining two tables into one.

You’ll need from before: a clean DataFrame you can select columns from (M3 Ch4). A pandas column is still a Series, so every per-column summary you already know applies inside each group.

title: Joins & groupby
section: Split-apply-combine: the shape of every group operation
text:
Split-apply-combine: the shape of every group operation
groupby is one idea wearing three steps, and naming them makes every later example read the same way:

Split — divide the rows into groups by the value in some column. Group by species and you get three piles: every Adelie row, every Chinstrap row, every Gentoo row.
Apply — run a summary on each pile on its own. Take the mean body mass of the Adelie pile, then of the Chinstrap pile, then of the Gentoo pile.
Combine — collect those per-group answers into one result, labelled by the group they came from.

That pattern — split-apply-combine — is the whole mental model. Once you see a question as “for each , compute ”, you know a groupby is coming, and you know the result will have one row per group.
Let’s run it on the penguins. We group by species, pick the body_mass_g column, and apply .mean():






Read that one line left to right and you can hear the three steps: groupby("species") splits, ["body_mass_g"] chooses the column to summarise, .mean() applies the summary, and pandas combines the answers into a Series indexed by species. The result:

Adelie — 3700.66 g
Chinstrap — 3733.09 g
Gentoo — 5076.02 g

Three lines of fieldwork in one expression. The index of that Series is the species name, so the label travels with each number — exactly the labelled-column behaviour from earlier chapters, now produced one value per group.

🔬 Going deeper (optional): the same call works for any summary that collapses a column to one number — .median(), .max(), .std(). Swap the verb and the split-apply-combine skeleton stays identical.

title: Joins & groupby
text:
Counting rows per group with .size()
Before you trust a per-group average, ask how many rows went into it — a mean of two penguins is far shakier than a mean of two hundred. .size() counts the rows in each group, no column needed, because you’re counting rows rather than summarising a value:






That returns the group sizes:

Adelie — 152
Chinstrap — 68
Gentoo — 124

Now the means above have context. Gentoo’s heavier average rests on 124 birds, and Chinstrap’s on 68 — enough in both cases to take the numbers seriously. Reading the group means and the group sizes together is a habit worth building: a per-group summary is only as solid as the count behind it.

title: Joins & groupby
text:
Several summaries at once with .agg
Often you want more than one number per group — the mean and the count, or the mean of two different columns. Calling groupby repeatedly would re-split the table each time. Instead, hand a list of summary names to .agg and pandas computes them together in a single pass:






The result is a small table: one row per species, a mean column, and a size column side by side. You get the same 3700.66 / 3733.09 / 5076.02 means as before, now paired with their 152 / 68 / 124 counts — the average and its backing in one glance.
.agg also extends across columns. Group once, then summarise body mass and flipper length together:






This reports, per species, the mean body mass alongside the mean flipper length — the flipper means landing at Adelie 189.95 mm, Chinstrap 195.82 mm, and Gentoo 217.19 mm. The longer-flippered Gentoo is also the heaviest, a pattern the grouped view makes easy to spot. The point stands: group once, summarise as widely as the question needs.

title: Joins & groupby
text:
Joining two tables with merge
Grouping answers questions that live inside one table. But data is usually scattered across several. One table holds each penguin’s measurements; another might hold a fact about each species — say, the island where it is mostly found. To answer “how heavy is the average penguin on each species’ main island?” you first need both facts in the same table. That stitching-together is a join, and in pandas it is pd.merge.
A merge lines up rows from two tables by matching a key — a column they share. Picture two small tables that both have a species column:






Each table knows something the other doesn’t: counts has the group sizes, islands has the main island per species. They overlap on species. Hand both to pd.merge and name the key with on="species":






The result is one wider table: species, count, and main_island together, with each row matched up by species. pd.merge walked the key column, found the row in the right table whose species equals the left row’s species, and pasted its extra columns alongside. Adelie’s count now sits next to Adelie’s island, and so on.
The mental model is the one your eye uses with two printouts and a shared ID column: find the matching row in the other sheet, copy its columns across. on="species" tells pandas which column is that shared ID. Get the key right and the rest lines up; name the wrong column and rows match by accident or not at all — so the key choice is the decision that matters in a join.
Grouping and joining are the two everyday reshaping moves. Grouping collapses many rows into one summary per group; joining widens a table by bringing a second table’s columns alongside on a shared key. Most real analysis is a handful of these, chained.

title: Joins & groupby
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

groupby is split-apply-combine: split rows into groups by a column, apply a summary to each, combine into one result with a row per group.
df.groupby('species')['body_mass_g'].mean() gives a mean per group (Adelie 3700.66, Chinstrap 3733.09, Gentoo 5076.02); .size() counts the rows per group (152 / 68 / 124).
.agg(['mean', 'size']) computes several summaries per group in one pass, so you read each average next to its backing count.
pd.merge(left, right, on='key') joins two tables into one by matching rows that share the same value in the key column.

title: Joins & groupby
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made data so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Write group_totals(data, key, val): build a DataFrame from data, group by the key column, and return a dict of the per-group sums of the val column. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Build the table with pd.DataFrame(data), then group with .groupby(key), pick the value column with [val], and apply .sum(). Finish with .to_dict() so the per-group result comes back as a plain dictionary keyed by group.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def group_totals(data, key, val):
    return pd.DataFrame(data).groupby(key)[val].sum().to_dict()
groupby(key) splits the rows by group, [val].sum() adds the value column within each group (the a rows total 1 + 2 = 3, the b row totals 10), and .to_dict() combines the answers into {"a": 3, "b": 10}.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now the same shape, but report means rounded to two decimals. Write group_means(data, key, val): group by key and return a dict of the per-group mean of val, each value rounded to 2 places. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

It is the Exercise 1 pattern with .mean() in place of .sum(), plus a .round(2) before .to_dict(). The a rows average (2 + 4) / 2 = 3.0; the lone b row averages 10.0.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def group_means(data, key, val):
    return pd.DataFrame(data).groupby(key)[val].mean().round(2).to_dict()
groupby(key)[val].mean() is split-apply-combine with the mean as the summary: group a averages to 3.0, group b to 10.0. .round(2) trims each to two decimals and .to_dict() returns {"a": 3.0, "b": 10.0}.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset, group by species, and report the mean body mass per species (Adelie ≈ 3700.66, Chinstrap ≈ 3733.09, Gentoo ≈ 5076.02 g). Then print which species is heaviest on average (Gentoo) — read from the data, not typed in by hand.
Rubric — a good answer:

Loads penguins with load_csv and groups by species to compute the mean body_mass_g per species.
Prints the three per-species means read from the grouped result.
Reports the heaviest species (Gentoo) using .idxmax() on the grouped means, not a hand-typed name.



If you’re stuck

Group with df.groupby("species")["body_mass_g"].mean() and keep that Series. Print it to see all three means. The heaviest species is the index label of its largest value, which .idxmax() returns for you.



Show one possible answer

import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins")
means = df.groupby("species")["body_mass_g"].mean().round(2)
print(means)
print("heaviest on average:", means.idxmax())
This prints the per-species means (Adelie 3700.66, Chinstrap 3733.09, Gentoo 5076.02) and reports Gentoo as the heaviest — both read straight from the grouped result rather than typed in by hand.

title: Joins & groupby
text: Check yourself

title: Joins & groupby
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now collapse a table into one summary per group with groupby, read each group’s average next to its count with .agg, and widen a table by joining a second one on a shared key with merge. Those two moves — group to summarise, join to combine — are the backbone of everyday analysis. Next we turn from reshaping data to picturing it, plotting these grouped summaries so a difference like Gentoo’s heavier mass jumps off the page.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
text:
The hook
You already met a table in pandas and learned to size it up — shape, head, a numeric summary. But a column of 344 numbers still hides its story. A picture hands that story over in one glance: whether a value is rising, which category is biggest, whether two measurements move together, or how a single column is spread out.
Matplotlib is the drawing tool the whole Python data world is built on. You won’t learn all of it here — you’ll learn the four charts you reach for every day and the small, repeatable recipe that draws each one:

A line chart — for a value that changes over an ordered sequence, often time.
A bar chart — for comparing one value across a handful of categories.
A scatter plot — for the relationship between two numeric columns.
A histogram — for the distribution of a single numeric column.

The skill we’re after is less about memorising code and more about a habit: pick the chart that fits your question, then label it so anyone can read it.

You’ll need from before: loading a table and pulling out a column as a Series (M3 Ch2). Matplotlib loads itself the moment you write import matplotlib.pyplot, so there is nothing extra to install.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
section: The one recipe behind every chart
text:
The one recipe behind every chart
Almost every Matplotlib chart you’ll draw follows the same five-step shape. Learn it once and the four charts become variations on a theme:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt          # 1. the plotting tool

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))    # 2. a figure and a set of axes to draw on
ax.plot(x, y)                             # 3. a plot method: plot / bar / scatter / hist
ax.set_xlabel("...")                      # 4. label the axes and give it a title
ax.set_ylabel("...")
ax.set_title("...")
plt.show()                                # 5. render the figure
Two names are worth pinning down. The figure (fig) is the whole picture — the canvas. The axes (ax) is the area inside it with the x- and y-axis where the data is actually drawn. plt.subplots() hands you both at once; figsize=(5, 3) sets the width and height in inches. From there you call one plot method on ax, label it, and show it. That’s the entire pattern — only step 3 changes between chart types.
Step 4 is not optional. A chart is read on its own, away from the code that made it, so unlabelled axes are a bug, not a style choice. Every chart below names what each axis means and what the whole picture is about.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
section: A line chart: a value over an ordered sequence
text:
A line chart: a value over an ordered sequence
A line chart joins points in order with a line, which makes it the natural choice for something that changes along a sequence — most often over time. The connecting line draws the eye along the trend: rising, falling, or flat.






The line climbs from left to right, so the takeaway reads straight off the picture: active users grew every month, from 120 up to 260 over the half-year. The marker="o" puts a dot on each measured month so you can see the six real data points the line connects. Reach for a line chart whenever your x-axis has a natural order — days, months, epochs of training — and you care about the shape of the change across it.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
section: A bar chart: comparing across categories
text:
A bar chart: comparing across categories
A bar chart draws one bar per category, with the bar’s height showing its value. It is the right tool for comparing a count or an amount across a small set of named groups, where there is no “order” to the x-axis — only different things to line up side by side.
Let’s count how many penguins of each species are in the dataset and compare them. We get the counts with value_counts() from the previous chapter, then hand the category names and their heights to ax.bar.






The bars make the comparison instant: Adelie is the most common species at 152 birds, Gentoo follows at 124, and Chinstrap is the smallest group at 68. Heights are easy to compare because they all start from the same baseline of zero — which is exactly why a bar chart beats a table when your question is “which category is biggest?” Use bars for categories; if your x-axis were a time sequence instead, you’d reach for a line.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
section: A scatter plot: the relationship between two columns
text:
A scatter plot: the relationship between two columns
The line and bar charts each describe values along one axis of meaning. A scatter plot describes two numeric columns at once: put one on the x-axis, the other on the y-axis, and draw one dot per record. The shape of the cloud tells you whether the two move together.
Penguins offer a natural pair to test: do birds with longer flippers also weigh more?






The dots form a clear upward-sloping band from lower-left to upper-right, so the takeaway is that penguins with longer flippers tend to be heavier — a positive relationship. The s=10 shrinks each dot and alpha=0.6 makes them slightly see-through, so where many penguins overlap you can still tell the cloud is dense rather than a single blob. Reach for a scatter plot whenever your question is “do these two numbers move together?”

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
section: A histogram: the distribution of one column
text:
A histogram: the distribution of one column
A scatter needs two columns. A histogram needs only one, and answers a different question: how are the values of this column spread out? It chops the range into equal slices called bins, counts how many values land in each, and draws a bar of that height. The result is the column’s distribution — its silhouette.






Read the shape left to right. The bars are tallest around 3500–4000 g — that is where most penguins sit — and then trail off toward the heavier birds on the right. So the takeaway is that body mass clusters near 3500–4000 g with a typical range of roughly 3000–5500 g and a gentle tail of heavier penguins. The number of bins is a dial you choose: too few blurs real detail into one fat bar, too many makes random wobble look like structure. Twelve is a sensible start here.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
text:
Choosing the right chart
The hardest part is not the code — it’s matching the chart to the question. A quick map:

“How does this change over time / an ordered sequence?” → a line chart.
“Which category is biggest?” → a bar chart.
“Do these two numbers move together?” → a scatter plot.
“How is this one column spread out?” → a histogram.

Pick the wrong chart and a true pattern can hide; pick the right one and it jumps out.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

The four everyday charts each answer one question: a line for a trend over an ordered sequence, a bar for comparing categories, a scatter for a relationship between two columns, and a histogram for one column’s distribution.
Every chart follows one recipe: import matplotlib.pyplot as plt, fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3)), a plot method on ax, then plt.show().
Always label the chart — ax.set_xlabel, ax.set_ylabel, ax.set_title — so it can be read on its own, units and all.
The skill is choosing the chart that fits the question; the wrong chart can hide a pattern the right one makes obvious.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two prepare the data for a chart with tiny hand-made inputs, so the answers are exact — you cannot assert on a drawn picture, but you can check the numbers behind it.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
A line chart needs a list of y-values to draw. To plot the parabola y = x², you need the squares of the x-values. Complete squared(xs) so it returns the square of each value. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

A list comprehension builds the new list in one line: for every x in xs, compute x * x. The result is the list of y-values you would pass to ax.plot(xs, ys).



Show solution

def squared(xs):
    return [x * x for x in xs]
[x * x for x in xs] walks the input list and squares each value, returning a new list the same length — so [1, 2, 3] becomes [1, 4, 9], the y-values that trace a parabola when plotted against the x-values.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
A bar chart compares a count per category, so first you need those counts. Write category_counts(items) that returns a dictionary mapping each value to how many times it appears — the heights for a bar chart. Use collections.Counter. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

Counter(items) tallies how often each value appears and behaves like a dictionary. Wrap it in dict(...) to return a plain dictionary — {"a": 2, "b": 1} for ["a", "a", "b"], and an empty {} for an empty list.



Show solution

from collections import Counter

def category_counts(items):
    return dict(Counter(items))
Counter(items) counts each distinct value in one pass; dict(...) turns the result into a plain dictionary. Those key→count pairs are exactly the category names and bar heights you would hand to ax.bar(counts.keys(), counts.values()).



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Make a labelled bar chart of a category count of your choice — the penguin species counts work well, or any list of categories you like. Then write one sentence describing what the chart shows.
Rubric — a good answer:

Draws a bar chart with ax.bar from a set of category counts.
Labels both axes with ax.set_xlabel / ax.set_ylabel and gives it a title with ax.set_title.
Describes in words what the chart shows — which category is biggest, and by roughly how much.



If you’re stuck

Get the counts with df["species"].value_counts(), then pass counts.index (the names) and counts.values (the heights) to ax.bar. Don’t forget the three label calls before plt.show(), and read the tallest bar off the picture for your sentence.



Show one possible answer

from lib.data import load_csv
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = load_csv("penguins")
counts = df["species"].value_counts()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 3))
ax.bar(counts.index, counts.values)
ax.set_xlabel("species")
ax.set_ylabel("number of penguins")
ax.set_title("Penguin species counts")
plt.show()

print("Adelie is the most common species (152 birds), then Gentoo (124),")
print("with Chinstrap the smallest group (68) — Adelie is over twice Chinstrap.")
The three bars start from a common baseline of zero, so their heights compare directly: Adelie is tallest at 152, Gentoo next at 124, and Chinstrap shortest at 68. The chart answers a category-comparison question at a glance, which is what bar charts are for.

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
text: Check yourself

title: Plotting with Matplotlib
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You can now draw the four everyday charts, label them so they stand on their own, and — the real skill — pick the one that fits your question. That turns a wall of numbers into a picture you can reason about. Next we go deeper into the charts that describe data statistically: reading a histogram for shape, a boxplot for spread and outliers, and a scatter for the strength of a relationship.

title: The EDA workflow
text: Intermediate Browser Stable

title: The EDA workflow
text:
The hook
A new dataset lands in your lap. Before you build anything with it — a chart for a report, a feature for a model, a claim in a meeting — you need to know what you are holding. How big is it? What is in each column? What is missing? What patterns are already there?
The trap is to poke at it at random: a .head() here, a guess there, a chart that looks nice but answers nothing. Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the cure. It is not a single trick — it is a short, repeatable recipe you run on every new table, in the same order, so you never forget a step and never start modelling on data you don’t understand.
Here is the whole recipe, and it is the spine of this chapter:

shape → types → missing → summaries → groupby → plot → questions

Each step is one or two lines of pandas you already met in this module. What’s new is the discipline: running them in order, and treating the output as evidence. By the end you’ll run the full pass on the Palmer Penguins dataset and answer three concrete questions — each backed by a number or a plot, never a hunch.

You’ll need from before: split-apply-combine with groupby (M3 Ch5) and reading a histogram, boxplot, or scatter (M3 Ch8). EDA is where those skills come together.

title: The EDA workflow
section: Step 1 — Shape: how big is it?
text:
Step 1 — Shape: how big is it?
The first question about any table is the cheapest to answer: how many rows and columns? df.shape returns a (rows, columns) tuple. It costs nothing and tells you whether you have 300 records or 30 million — which changes everything about how you’ll work.






That returns (344, 8): 344 penguins, described by 8 columns. Small enough to explore comfortably in the browser, and the first fact in our running notebook of evidence.

title: The EDA workflow
section: Step 2 — Types: what is each column?
text:
Step 2 — Types: what is each column?
Next, ask what kind of value each column holds. df.dtypes lists the data type of every column. This catches a classic bug early: a column of numbers that pandas read as text (object) because one stray value had a comma or a stray space. You cannot average a column that is secretly a string.






Reading the list: the four body measurements and body_mass_g are numeric (float64 or int64), while species, island, and sex are object — text labels. That is exactly what we’d expect, so nothing to fix here. The point of the step is that you looked: numeric columns are numeric, categorical columns are text. If body_mass_g had shown up as object, that would be a red flag to chase down before going further.

title: The EDA workflow
section: Step 3 — Missing: where are the gaps?
text:
Step 3 — Missing: where are the gaps?
Real data has holes. df.isna().sum() counts, per column, how many cells are empty. This single line tells you which columns are complete and which you’ll have to handle — by dropping rows, filling values, or noting the gap when you report a result.






Most columns are complete. The four body measurements each miss 2 values, and sex misses 11. So if you analyse sex, you are working with 333 known penguins, not 344 — a fact you’d want to state out loud rather than quietly average over. Knowing this before you compute anything stops a missing-value surprise from skewing a result later.

title: The EDA workflow
section: Step 4 — Summaries: what do the numbers look like?
text:
Step 4 — Summaries: what do the numbers look like?
Now describe the numbers. df.describe() reports the count, mean, standard deviation, min, max, and quartiles for every numeric column at once — a one-line statistical X-ray of the whole table.






Read down the body_mass_g column and you’ll find a mean near 4201.75 g and a range from about 2700 g to 6300 g — penguins averaging a little over four kilograms, with the lightest under three and the heaviest over six. The count row is worth a glance too: it reads 342 for the measurement columns, not 344, because describe() quietly skips the two missing masses we found in Step 3. The summary and the missing-value count agree, which is reassuring.

title: The EDA workflow
section: Step 5 — Groupby: does a category explain the numbers?
text:
Step 5 — Groupby: does a category explain the numbers?
Summaries describe the table as a whole. The next question is whether a category splits those numbers into meaningful groups. That is exactly what groupby does: split the rows by a label, then aggregate each group. Here we ask whether average body mass differs by species.






The three group means tell a clear story: Adelie ≈ 3700.66 g, Chinstrap ≈ 3733.09 g, and Gentoo ≈ 5076.02 g. Adelie and Chinstrap sit within about 30 g of each other, while Gentoo penguins are roughly 1.36 kg heavier — a big, real gap. Species is not merely a label here; it explains a large chunk of the variation in body mass that describe() reported for the table as a whole.

title: The EDA workflow
