section: Step 6 — Plot: can you see it?
text:
Step 6 — Plot: can you see it?
A number convinces; a picture confirms and communicates. The last computational step turns the groupby result into a chart so the pattern is impossible to miss. A bar chart of the three group means is the natural choice.






The takeaway is one glance away: two short bars of nearly equal height (Adelie and Chinstrap) and one tall bar (Gentoo) standing well above them. The plot says the same thing as the three numbers from Step 5, but now anyone can read it in a second — which is the point of a chart in a report. The number is the proof; the plot is the explanation.

title: The EDA workflow
section: Step 7 — Questions: what can this data answer?
text:
Step 7 — Questions: what can this data answer?
The first six steps are mechanical. The seventh is the reason for the other six: now that you understand the table, you can pose concrete questions and answer each with the evidence you’ve gathered. A good EDA question is specific and checkable — not “what’s interesting?” but “which species is heaviest, and by how much?” Here are three, each answered straight from the steps above.
Q1 — How complete is the data? From Step 1 and Step 3: there are 344 rows, and the only sizeable gap is sex, with 11 missing values. Any analysis of sex covers 333 birds, not all 344 — a caveat to carry forward.
Q2 — What does a typical penguin weigh? From Step 4: the mean body mass is ≈ 4201.75 g, with most birds falling between roughly 3000 g and 5500 g. “Typical” is about four kilograms.
Q3 — Does species explain body mass? From Steps 5 and 6: yes, strongly. The heaviest species is Gentoo at ≈ 5076.02 g, about 1.36 kg above Adelie (≈ 3700.66 g) and Chinstrap (≈ 3733.09 g), which are nearly identical to each other. The bar chart makes the split visible at a glance.
Notice that not one of those answers is a guess. Each points back to a specific line of output — a shape, a count, a mean, a chart. That is the whole habit EDA is training: every claim is backed by a number or a plot. When you later hand the data to a model, you’ll know what it can learn and what would mislead it — because you looked first.

title: The EDA workflow
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

EDA is a repeatable recipe, run in order on every new table: shape → types → missing → summaries → groupby → plot → questions.
The first four steps size up the data — df.shape, df.dtypes, df.isna().sum(), and df.describe() — before you compute anything fancy.
groupby asks whether a category explains the numbers; a plot turns that answer into something anyone can read at a glance.
Ask concrete, checkable questions, and back every answer with a number or a plot read straight from the data — never a hunch.

title: The EDA workflow
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made dictionaries so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Step 1 of the recipe is shape. Write eda_shape(data) that builds a DataFrame from a dict and returns its (rows, columns) as a plain tuple. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

pd.DataFrame(data) builds the table; its .shape is already a (rows, columns) pair. Wrap that in tuple(...) so the return type is a plain tuple.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def eda_shape(data):
    return tuple(pd.DataFrame(data).shape)
pd.DataFrame(data) turns the dict into a table — keys become columns, so a dict with three keys of two values each is (2, 3). .shape reports (rows, columns), and tuple(...) returns it as a plain tuple.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Step 4 is summaries. Write summary(data, col) that returns a small dict {"mean": .., "min": .., "max": ..} for one column — the mean rounded to 2 decimals, the min and max as plain ints. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

s = df[col] is the column as a Series. Build the dict with s.mean() wrapped in round(float(...), 2), and s.min() / s.max() each wrapped in int(...) so the types are plain Python numbers.



Show solution

import pandas as pd

def summary(data, col):
    df = pd.DataFrame(data)
    s = df[col]
    return {"mean": round(float(s.mean()), 2), "min": int(s.min()), "max": int(s.max())}
df[col] selects the column as a Series; .mean(), .min(), and .max() are the summary statistics. Wrapping the mean in round(float(...), 2) and the extremes in int(...) returns plain, predictable Python types — so [1, 2, 3, 4] gives {"mean": 2.5, "min": 1, "max": 4}.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset and run the full EDA recipe — shape, types, missing, summary, groupby — then answer two concrete questions with numbers in your own print statements. For example: which species is heaviest, and by how much? How many rows are missing sex?
Rubric — a good answer:

Loads penguins with load_csv and runs at least three recipe steps (e.g. shape, isna().sum(), and a groupby).
Answers the heaviest-species question with a number (Gentoo at ≈ 5076 g), read from a groupby, not typed in by hand.
Answers a completeness question with a number (sex has 11 missing values), read from isna().sum().



If you’re stuck

Run df.shape, df.isna().sum(), and df.groupby("species")["body_mass_g"].mean(). For the heaviest species, .idxmax() gives its name and .max() its mean. For the missing count, index the isna().sum() result with ["sex"].



Show one possible answer

import pandas as pd
from lib.data import load_csv

df = load_csv("penguins")

# Recipe steps
print("shape:", df.shape)
missing = df.isna().sum()
print("missing sex:", int(missing["sex"]))
means = df.groupby("species")["body_mass_g"].mean()
print(means.round(2))

# Answers, each backed by a number
print("heaviest species:", means.idxmax(), "at", round(float(means.max()), 2), "g")
print("rows missing sex:", int(missing["sex"]))
This reports (344, 8), the heaviest species as Gentoo at 5076.02 g (about 1.36 kg above the other two), and 11 rows missing sex — every answer read straight from the data rather than typed in by hand.

title: The EDA workflow
text: Check yourself

title: The EDA workflow
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You now have a recipe you can run on any new table: size it up (shape, types, missing), summarise it, split it by a category, draw the pattern, and ask questions you can actually answer with evidence. That pass is the first thing a working data scientist does with a fresh dataset — and the habit of backing every claim with a number or a plot is what separates analysis from guesswork. This closes the data module; next you’ll carry these tools into the modelling work, where understanding the data first is what keeps a model honest.

title: Notebooks vs scripts
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Notebooks vs scripts
text:
The hook
You’ve been writing code in cells on this page — a notebook. But the data jobs that run every night, retrain a model, or ship in a product are almost always plain scripts: .py files run from a terminal. Same Python, two very different working styles — and knowing when to use each is part of being a data person, not only a learner.
A notebook lets you run little pieces in any order and see results (and plots) right next to the code — perfect for exploring. A script runs from the first line to the last, every single time — perfect for reproducing and automating. This chapter is the what and why of the two, and the one trap that catches everybody.

You’ll need from before: that you’ve met notebooks, an editor, and Colab (M1 Ch14). This page itself is a notebook-style environment.

title: Notebooks vs scripts
section: A notebook: cells, run in any order
text:
A notebook: cells, run in any order
A notebook is a list of cells. You run one, see its output, tweak it, run it again — all while the variables you’ve defined stay alive in memory. That instant feedback is why notebooks rule exploratory data analysis: load a CSV, peek at it, plot it, adjust, repeat.
The cell below behaves like a notebook cell — it runs, shares its state with the page, and shows output inline. Press Run:






Inside one cell, the lines still run top-to-bottom. The freedom — and the danger — is at the level of cells: you can run them in any order you like.

title: Notebooks vs scripts
section: A script: a .py file, run top-to-bottom
text:
A script: a .py file, run top-to-bottom
A script is a text file ending in .py. You run it from a terminal with python yourfile.py, and it executes from the first line to the last, with no cells and no inline output beyond what you print. Run it again tomorrow and it does exactly the same thing — that predictability is why pipelines and production code are scripts.

💡 On your own computer (this page runs in the browser, so the commands below are for your real machine, not here). This is a real transcript — a tiny script and what python actually printed when it ran:
$ cat greet.py
name = "Larnix"
total = 2 + 2
print(f"{name}: 2 + 2 = {total}")

$ python greet.py
Larnix: 2 + 2 = 4

No cells, no “run this bit first” — the file ran top-to-bottom and printed one line. Run it a hundred times and you get that same line a hundred times.

title: Notebooks vs scripts
text:
The trap: hidden state
Because a notebook lets you run cells out of order, it can show a result that a clean run would never produce. Define a variable in cell 5, use it in cell 2, then delete cell 5 — cell 2 still “works” because the value is lingering in memory, until you restart and everything breaks.

⚠️ Watch out: before you trust a notebook’s results — or share them — use “Restart & Run All”. If it doesn’t run top-to-bottom from a clean start, the result isn’t real. This is the single most common notebook bug.







Note🧱 For Java developers





A script is the familiar model: a main you run start-to-finish, like java Main. A notebook is closer to a long-lived REPL/JShell session where earlier statements stay in scope — powerful for experimenting, but the session’s accumulated state is exactly what makes “it worked in my session” unreproducible. “Restart & Run All” is the notebook equivalent of a clean rebuild.

title: Notebooks vs scripts
text:
Which one, when?

Notebook — exploring a new dataset, trying plots, explaining an analysis with prose and charts side by side (this course is notebooks for that reason).
Script — a cleaning or training step that must run the same way every time, a job on a schedule, code another program imports, anything headed for production.

A common real workflow: explore in a notebook, then move the settled code into a script so it runs reliably without you.

title: Notebooks vs scripts
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

A notebook is cells you can run in any order with inline output — best for exploring.
A script is a .py file that runs top-to-bottom every time — best for reproducing and automating.
The notebook trap is hidden state from out-of-order runs; “Restart & Run All” before you trust a result.
Explore in a notebook, then move the settled code into a script.

title: Notebooks vs scripts
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
A file’s extension tells you what it is. Complete is_script so it returns True for a Python script (a name ending in .py). Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

Strings have a method .endswith(...) that returns True or False. Try filename.endswith(".py").



Show solution

def is_script(filename):
    return filename.endswith(".py")
.endswith(".py") is True for "clean.py" and False for "explore.ipynb" — the extension is how you tell a script from a notebook on disk.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
When you move notebook cells into a script, you join their lines into one file. Write make_script(lines) — join a list of code lines into a single string, one per line. Complete the marked line.









If you’re stuck

"\n".join(lines) glues the items together with a newline between each — exactly how lines sit in a .py file.



Show solution

def make_script(lines):
    return "\n".join(lines)
"\n".join(lines) puts a newline between items (not after the last), so two cells become two lines of a script — the first step in turning exploration into a repeatable file.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). In two or three sentences, describe a data task you’d do in a notebook and a different one you’d put in a script, and say why each fits.
Rubric — a good answer:

Names a notebook task that is exploratory (peeking at data, plotting, trying ideas) and says why inline, out-of-order work helps.
Names a script task that must be reproducible or automated (a nightly clean, a shared pipeline) and says why top-to-bottom running matters.
Mentions the explore-then-move-to-a-script workflow, or the “Restart & Run All” habit.



Show one possible answer

answer = """
I'd explore a new sales CSV in a notebook: load it, run df.describe(), draw a few
plots, and adjust as I learn — the inline output makes that fast.
I'd put the nightly cleaning step in a script (clean_sales.py) so it runs the same
way every night with no one watching. I explore first, then move the settled code
into the script, and I 'Restart & Run All' the notebook before trusting any result.
"""
print(answer.strip())
The split is exploration vs reliability: notebooks for figuring it out, scripts for running it the same way every time.

title: Notebooks vs scripts
text: Check yourself

title: Notebooks vs scripts
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You know which tool fits which job — and the hidden-state trap that makes “Restart & Run All” a reflex. Next we tackle the habit that makes either one trustworthy on someone else’s machine: environments & reproducibility — pinned versions and fixed random seeds, so your analysis gives the same answer everywhere.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
text: Beginner Browser Stable

title: Data ethics (a preview)
text:
The hook
You have spent this module learning to load, clean, and read a table. Before you build anything on top of one, there are three plain questions worth asking — and they take about a minute each:

Where did this data come from, and may I use it? (provenance and license)
Who is in it, and who is missing? (representation)
Does it name real people? (privacy)

This is not a lecture on ethics. These are practical checks that save you from shipping a wrong conclusion, breaking a license, or leaking someone’s details. We’ll run each check against the penguins dataset you already know, then write the short “data note” that records the answers so the next person (often future-you) does not have to re-discover them.

You’ll need from before: the EDA recipe (M3 Ch9) — shape, types, missing values, and value_counts. The same tools answer these three questions.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
text:
Where the data came from (provenance)
Provenance is the story of a dataset: who collected it, from what, when, and how. The penguins table is body measurements of 344 penguins recorded at Palmer Station, Antarctica, by researcher Kristen Gorman and the Palmer LTER program. That single sentence already tells you a lot — these are real measurements of birds, taken by field scientists, in one place, in a few seasons. It is not a sample of penguins worldwide, and it was never meant to be.
Provenance matters because it bounds what the data can honestly tell you. A model trained on these three Antarctic species says nothing reliable about a penguin in a zoo, and a number computed here describes these birds in those seasons — not penguins in general. Knowing the source keeps you from over-claiming.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
text:
Why the license matters
A dataset’s license says what you are allowed to do with it — copy it, ship it inside a course, build a product on it, or nothing at all. The penguins data is released under CC0, which places it in the public domain: anyone may use it for any purpose, no permission or attribution required. That license is the reason this course can vendor the CSV — store a copy right next to the chapter — and why every exercise can load it offline at zero cost.
Swap in a dataset scraped from a website with no stated license, and none of that is safe. “I found it online” is not a license. Before you commit a file to a repo or train on it, find the license; if there isn’t one, treat the data as not yours to redistribute and look for a properly licensed alternative.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
section: Representation: who is in the data, and who is thin
text:
Representation: who is in the data, and who is thin
A dataset is a sample, and a sample can be lopsided. When one group has far fewer rows than another, every statement about that group rests on less evidence — the average is noisier, a rate could swing on a handful of records, and a model sees too few examples to learn the group well. Under-representation does not announce itself; you have to count.
Two cheap checks surface it: count the rows per group, and count the missing values. Let’s do both on penguins.






The counts are Adelie 152, Gentoo 124, Chinstrap 68. Chinstrap has fewer than half the rows of Adelie. That imbalance is not “wrong” — it is merely how the sampling fell — but it has a consequence: any claim about Chinstrap penguins stands on 68 records, so it is more fragile than the same claim about Adelie. If you later trained a classifier, this is exactly the kind of imbalance that makes a model quietly good at the common class and weak at the rare one.
Missing values are the other side of representation: a column can be present but empty for some rows, which thins out the group you can actually analyse.






Eleven rows have no recorded sex. If you set out to compare body mass by sex, those 11 penguins drop out of the comparison before you start — a small but real narrowing of the data. And the species shares (Adelie ≈ 44%, Gentoo ≈ 36%, Chinstrap ≈ 20%) put a number on the imbalance you saw above. Neither finding stops you from using the data; both change how strongly you should phrase a conclusion drawn from the thin parts of it.

🔬 The habit to keep: when a result depends on a small or patchy group, say so out loud — “based on 68 Chinstrap records” — instead of presenting it as if it rested on the full 344.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
section: Privacy: don’t collect or print what you don’t need
text:
Privacy: don’t collect or print what you don’t need
PII — personally identifiable information — is any field that can single out a real person: a name, email, phone number, address, or government ID. The penguins data is comfortable to work with precisely because it has none: a penguin is not a person, the measurements were taken with research consent, and no row points back to an individual human. That is why it is safe to print every row in a public notebook.
Most datasets you meet later will not be so clean. The habits that keep you out of trouble are simple:

Don’t collect identifiers you don’t need. The best way to avoid leaking a phone number is to never store it.
Don’t print PII into notebook output, logs, or charts — that output gets committed to git, shared in a PR, and rendered on a public site.
Reduce before you share: aggregate (counts and averages reveal far less than raw rows) or drop and pseudonymise identifying columns.

A handy test: if this output leaked, could it embarrass or endanger anyone in the data? For penguins the answer is no. For a table of customers, it often is yes — and the fix is to compute on the data without putting the raw identifiers on screen.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
text:
Write a short data note
The cheapest way to make all of the above stick is a data note: three lines you write once, at the top of the analysis, recording the source, the license, and one honest caveat. It costs a minute and pays off every time someone (including you) reopens the work.
For penguins it reads:

Source: Palmer Station LTER / Kristen Gorman — body measurements of 344 penguins, three species, near Palmer Station, Antarctica. License: CC0 (public domain) — free to use and redistribute. Caveat: classes are imbalanced (Chinstrap only 68 rows) and 11 rows are missing sex, so conclusions about the smaller groups are weaker.

That note is not bureaucracy. It is the difference between an analysis a stranger can trust and a pile of numbers with no provenance.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
text:
Key Takeaways

Key Takeaways

Provenance and license come first. Know where data came from and what you may do with it; penguins is CC0 (public domain), which is why this course can vendor it offline.
Count before you conclude. Row counts and missing-value counts expose under-representation — penguins is imbalanced (Adelie 152, Gentoo 124, Chinstrap

and 11 rows lack sex, so claims about the thin groups are weaker.

Protect people. Don’t collect, print, or share PII you don’t need; penguins is safe to show because it names no person.
Write a three-line data note — source, license, one caveat — so the next reader inherits the context instead of guessing.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
text:
Practice
Each exercise is auto-graded by run_tests, loaded for you from lib/grader.py. The first two use tiny hand-made numbers so the answers are exact.

Exercise 1 — Guided (≈4 min) 🟢
Representation starts with a share: what fraction of the whole is one group? Complete share(count, total) so it returns the proportion, rounded to two decimal places. Replace ____, then press Run.









If you’re stuck

A share is one division: the group’s count divided by the total. Wrap it in round(..., 2) so the result has two decimal places.



Show solution

def share(count, total):
    return round(count / total, 2)

run_tests([
    ("quarter", share(1, 4), 0.25),
    ("three quarters", share(3, 4), 0.75),
])
count / total is the proportion; round(..., 2) trims it to two decimals so 1 / 4 reads as a clean 0.25. This is the same calculation behind “Chinstrap is about 20% of the table”.



Exercise 2 — Implement (≈8 min) 🟡
Now do it for every group at once. Write class_shares(counts), which takes a dict of class → count and returns a dict of class → rounded share. Complete the marked lines.









If you’re stuck

sum(counts.values()) gives the total across every class. Then build a new dict with a comprehension, dividing each value by that total and rounding to two decimals.



Show solution

def class_shares(counts):
    total = sum(counts.values())
    return {k: round(v / total, 2) for k, v in counts.items()}

run_tests([
    ("imbalanced", class_shares({"a": 3, "b": 1}), {"a": 0.75, "b": 0.25}),
    ("even", class_shares({"x": 1, "y": 1}), {"x": 0.5, "y": 0.5}),
])
sum(counts.values()) is the denominator; the dict comprehension applies the same round(v / total, 2) to each class. Run it on {"Adelie": 152, "Gentoo": 124, "Chinstrap": 68} and you get the species shares from the chapter.



Exercise 3 — Stretch (optional, ≈8 min) 🔴
Open-ended, rubric-graded (not auto-checked). Load the penguins dataset and examine it for representation issues: count the rows missing sex (you’ll find 11) and the rows per species (the imbalance). Then write a three-line data note — source, license (CC0), and one bias caveat — as comments or print statements.
Rubric — a good answer:

Loads penguins and computes the evidence: missing-sex count and per-species counts read from the data, not typed in by hand.
Names the source and states the license as CC0 (public domain).
Records one honest caveat about bias — the class imbalance or the missing sex rows — and ties it to weaker conclusions for the thin groups.



If you’re stuck

Use df["sex"].isna().sum() for the missing count and df["species"].value_counts() for the per-species rows. Then write three lines: where it came from, that it is CC0, and which group is too thin to lean on.



Show one possible answer

from lib.data import load_csv
import pandas as pd

df = load_csv("penguins")

missing_sex = int(df["sex"].isna().sum())
counts = df["species"].value_counts()
print("rows missing sex:", missing_sex)
print(counts)

print("\n--- data note ---")
print("Source: Palmer Station LTER / Kristen Gorman — 344 penguins, 3 species.")
print("License: CC0 (public domain) — free to use and redistribute.")
print(f"Caveat: imbalanced (Chinstrap {int(counts['Chinstrap'])} rows) and "
      f"{missing_sex} rows miss sex, so conclusions about small groups are weaker.")
The note records exactly what a later reader needs: the provenance, the permissive CC0 license that makes the data safe to reuse, and one caveat that flags where the data is too thin to support a strong claim.

title: Data ethics (a preview)
text:
Where this fits / what’s next
You now have a one-minute habit to run on any dataset before you trust it: check its provenance and license, count who is in it and who is thin, guard anyone’s personal details, and write a three-line data note. This is a preview — Module 16 returns to responsible AI in depth, covering fairness metrics, dataset documentation, and model cards. For now, you close Module 3 able to not only read a table, but judge whether it deserves your trust.
